topics.title: Power Semiconductor Devices - Overview of Power Electronics Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Semiconductor Devices - Overview of Power Electronics Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Electronics (KTU 2024 Official Terminology):**
> Power Electronics is the branch of electrical engineering that deals with the **conversion, control, and conditioning of electric power** using **power semiconductor devices** operating in switching (non-linear) mode. The primary objective is to process and deliver electrical energy from one form to another with **maximum efficiency**, **high reliability**, and **minimum size/weight**.

The **heart** of any power electronic system is the **Power Semiconductor Switching Device** (e.g., Diode, BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, Thyristor, GTO, IGCT, SiC/GaN devices) which acts as a **controllable high-speed switch** between the source and the load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Water Tap" Model)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of your domestic water tap.
> - **Source:** Overhead water tank (high-pressure energy reservoir)
> - **Tap Handle:** The **Power Semiconductor Switch** (ON/OFF, partial control)
> - **Pipe Diameter:** Conductor size (current rating)
> - **Output Water Flow:** Delivered power to load
> - **Tap User:** The **Controller Circuit** (deciding when and how much to open)

Just as a tap regulates water flow to a washing machine, a **Thyristor** or **IGBT** regulates the electrical power delivered to a motor. Modern power electronics replaces the manual tap with **microsecond-fast electronic switching**, giving **precision, automation, and efficiency**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology for KTU

| Term | Definition (Board Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Power Semiconductor Device** | A solid-state device specifically designed to handle **high voltage (>100 V)** and **high current (>1 A)**. |
| **Converter** | The complete circuit assembly (switches + passive components) that performs power conversion. |
| **Switching Frequency ($f_s$)** | The rate (in Hz) at which the power device turns ON and OFF, typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz**. |
| **Efficiency ($\eta$)** | Ratio of output power to input power, expressed as a percentage. Modern PE systems achieve **>95%**. |
| **Power Rating** | Maximum voltage-current product (in **VA** or **W**) the system can handle. |
| **Application Power Range** | Spans from **<1 W** (mobile chargers) to **>1000 MW** (HVDC transmission). |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic Power Electronic System — Functional Block Diagram

Every power electronic system, irrespective of its application, consists of **four essential blocks**:

1. **Source:** DC Battery / AC Mains / Renewable Source
2. **Power Converter Circuit:** The arrangement of power switches (rectifier, inverter, chopper, cycloconverter)
3. **Controller (Gating/Trigger Circuit):** Generates firing pulses ($V_{GS}$ or $V_{GK}$) to switch the power devices
4. **Load:** Motor / Heater / Lamp / Grid / Battery / Communication circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **The Controller does NOT process power; it processes information.** All high-power handling is done by the converter switches.

### 2.2 The Four Fundamental Power Conversion Functions

Power Electronics is built around **four canonical functions**:

| Function | Input | Output | Typical Devices Used | Application Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rectification (AC → DC)** | AC Mains | Fixed/Variable DC | Diode, Thyristor | SMPS, Battery Charger |
| **Inversion (DC → AC)** | DC Battery / Solar | AC (variable freq/amp) | IGBT, MOSFET, Thyristor | VFD, Solar Inverter |
| **Chopping (DC → DC)** | Fixed DC | Variable DC | IGBT, MOSFET, BJT | EV, DC Drive |
| **AC Voltage Controller / Cycloconversion (AC → AC)** | Fixed AC | Variable AC | Thyristor (TRIAC) | Light Dimmer, Induction Heater |

### 2.3 Classification of Power Semiconductor Devices

Power devices are broadly classified based on their **controllability** and **latching behavior**:

- **Uncontrolled:** Power Diode (turns ON/OFF only by circuit conditions)
- **Semi-controlled:** Thyristor (SCR) — turn-ON controlled, turn-OFF by natural commutation
- **Fully controlled:** BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO, IGCT (turn-ON and turn-OFF both controlled)
- **Modern wide-bandgap:** SiC MOSFET, GaN HEMT (for ultra-high frequency)

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **must-memorize** for KTU 2024 ESE Module 1.

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{out} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi$ | AC Real Power delivered to load | **W (Watts)** |
| 2 | $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | Power lost in the converter | **W** |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Conversion Efficiency | **%** |
| 4 | $P_{switching} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} \cdot I_{L} \cdot (t_{on}+t_{off}) \cdot f_s$ | Switching Power Loss per cycle | **W** |
| 5 | $P_{conduction} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg}$ | Conduction Loss of an IGBT/BJT | **W** |
| 6 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Switching Frequency relation | **Hz** |
| 7 | $E_{sw} = \int_{t_{on}}^{t_{off}} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$ | Switching Energy Loss per transition | **Joules** |
| 8 | $S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$ | Apparent Power (used for transformer/drive sizing) | **VA** |
| 9 | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} i^{2}(t) \, dt}$ | RMS Current (heating value) | **A** |
| 10 | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t) \, dt$ | Average DC Output Voltage | **V** |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Modern power electronics is the **enabling technology** behind:
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** Tesla powertrain, regenerative braking
- **Renewable Energy:** Solar PV inverters, wind turbine grid-tie converters
- **Industrial Automation:** Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for induction motors
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone fast-chargers (GaN-based)
- **HVDC Transmission:** Bulk power transfer across thousands of kilometers
- **SMPS:** Every laptop/TV adapter uses switched-mode conversion

> [!NOTE]
> The **pervasiveness** of power electronics is why KTU has made it a **Core Course** for AEI/EEE streams under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Efficiency of a Power Electronic Converter

Let us derive the **fundamental efficiency equation** that examiners expect in Part A derivations.

**Given:**
- Input power to the converter: $P_{in}$ (W)
- Output power delivered to the load: $P_{out}$ (W)
- Power dissipated in the switching devices and passive components: $P_{loss}$ (W)

**Step 1: Apply Conservation of Energy**

By the First Law of Thermodynamics, energy in must equal energy out plus the energy lost as heat.

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss}$$

**Step 2: Define Efficiency**

Efficiency is universally defined as the ratio of useful output energy to total input energy supplied.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 3: Express $P_{out}$ in terms of $P_{in}$ and $P_{loss}$**

From Step 1, we substitute $P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss}$:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{in} - P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 4: Simplify**

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 5: Express as a Percentage (KTU Board Standard)**

$$\boxed{\eta(\%) = \left(1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}\right) \times 100\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even a *1% drop* in efficiency in a 100 MW HVDC station translates to a loss of **1 MW of continuous power** — enough to power **~750 Indian households**. This is why KTU emphasizes high-efficiency converter design.

### 3.2 Derivation: Switching Loss in a Power Semiconductor Device

When a power device transitions from **OFF to ON** (or vice versa), both voltage across it and current through it are non-zero simultaneously — this overlap causes switching loss.

**Step 1: Instantaneous Power in the Device**

The instantaneous power dissipated in the switch at any instant is:

$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

**Step 2: Energy Dissipated During Turn-ON Transition**

Assuming a linear voltage fall from $V_{DC}$ to $0$ and linear current rise from $0$ to $I_L$ over a turn-on time $t_{on}$:

$$v(t) = V_{DC}\left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right)$$

$$i(t) = I_L \cdot \frac{t}{t_{on}}$$

Multiplying:

$$p(t) = V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot \frac{t}{t_{on}} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t}{t_{on}}\right)$$

**Step 3: Integrate Over the Turn-ON Interval**

$$E_{on} = \int_{0}^{t_{on}} p(t) \, dt = V_{DC} \cdot I_L \int_{0}^{t_{on}} \left(\frac{t}{t_{on}} - \frac{t^{2}}{t_{on}^{2}}\right) dt$$

$$E_{on} = V_{DC} \cdot I_L \left[\frac{t^{2}}{2t_{on}} - \frac{t^{3}}{3t_{on}^{2}}\right]_{0}^{t_{on}}$$

$$E_{on} = V_{DC} \cdot I_L \left[\frac{t_{on}}{2} - \frac{t_{on}}{3}\right] = V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot \frac{t_{on}}{6}$$

**Step 4: Similarly for Turn-OFF (with $t_{off}$)**

$$E_{off} = V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot \frac{t_{off}}{6}$$

**Step 5: Total Switching Energy Per Cycle**

$$E_{sw(total)} = E_{on} + E_{off} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L}{6}(t_{on} + t_{off})$$

**Step 6: Average Switching Power Loss**

Multiplied by the number of switching events per second, $f_s$:

$$\boxed{P_{sw} = \frac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_s}{6}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this critical for KTU?** Examiners often ask: *"Why are wide-bandgap devices (SiC, GaN) preferred in modern converters?"* The answer: **smaller $(t_{on}+t_{off})$** → drastically reduced switching loss → higher $f_s$ → smaller magnetic components.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Application-to-Converter Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Which converter is used for X application?"* The matrix below is a high-yield revision tool.

| Application Domain | Power Range | Typical Converter | Preferred Switch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mobile Charger / Adapter | **5 W – 100 W** | Flyback SMPS (DC-DC) | MOSFET, GaN HEMT |
| Laptop / PC SMPS | **60 W – 500 W** | Forward / Half-Bridge | MOSFET |
| Induction Cooker | **1 kW – 3 kW** | Resonant Inverter | IGBT |
| Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) | **1 kVA – 100 kVA** | PWM Inverter + Rectifier | IGBT |
| Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) | **0.5 kW – 5 MW** | AC-DC-AC (PWM Inverter) | IGBT, IGCT |
| Solar PV Grid-Tie Inverter | **1 kW – 5 MW** | DC-AC (MPPT + Inverter) | IGBT, SiC MOSFET |
| HVDC Transmission | **100 MW – 10 GW** | Line-Commutated Converter | Thyristor (SCR) |
| Electric Vehicle Traction | **50 kW – 500 kW** | DC-DC + DC-AC (3-phase) | SiC MOSFET / IGBT |
| Static VAR Compensator | **1 Mvar – 500 Mvar** | Voltage Source Converter | IGBT, IGCT |
| Induction Heating | **5 kW – 1 MW** | High-Freq Resonant Inverter | MOSFET, IGBT |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Generic Power Electronic System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[AC Mains Source] --> recA
    src2[DC Battery Source] --> recA
    src3[Renewable Source PV Wind] --> recA
    
    recA{Power Converter} --> ctrlA
    recA --> sensA[Sensor Feedback]
    sensA --> ctrlA
    ctrlA[Controller and Gating Circuit] -->|Gate Pulses| recA
    
    recA --> loadA[Load Motor Lamp Grid Battery]
    
    sensA -.-> displayA[Display and Protection]
    
    classDef src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef conv fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    
    class src1,src2,src3 src
    class recA conv
    class ctrlA,sensA,displayA ctrl
    class loadA load
```

**Caption:** A generic power electronic system showing the closed-loop interaction between source, converter, controller, and load.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Classification of Power Conversion Functions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PE[Power Electronics] --> F1[Rectification AC to DC]
    PE --> F2[Inversion DC to AC]
    PE --> F3[DC to DC Chopping]
    PE --> F4[AC to AC Cycloconversion]
    
    F1 --> F1A[Uncontrolled Diode Bridge]
    F1 --> F1B[Controlled SCR Rectifier]
    
    F2 --> F2A[Square Wave Inverter]
    F2 --> F2B[PWM Inverter]
    F2 --> F2C[Resonant Inverter]
    
    F3 --> F3A[Step Down Buck]
    F3 --> F3B[Step Up Boost]
    F3 --> F3C[Buck Boost]
    
    F4 --> F4A[AC Voltage Controller TRIAC]
    F4 --> F4B[Cycloconverter]
    F4 --> F4C[Matrix Converter]
    
    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fn fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    
    class PE root
    class F1,F2,F3,F4 fn
    class F1A,F1B,F2A,F2B,F2C,F3A,F3B,F3C,F4A,F4B,F4C sub
```

**Caption:** Functional classification of all power electronic converters as per KTU Module 1 syllabus.

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Power Semiconductor Device Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Power Semiconductor Devices] --> uncontrolled[Uncontrolled]
    root --> semictl[Semi Controlled]
    root --> fullctl[Fully Controlled]
    root --> widebg[Wide Bandgap Modern]
    
    uncontrolled --> diode[Power Diode]
    
    semictl --> scr[Thyristor SCR]
    semictl --> triac[TRIAC]
    semictl --> lascr[Light Activated SCR LASCR]
    
    fullctl --> bjt[Power BJT]
    fullctl --> mosfet[Power MOSFET]
    fullctl --> igbt[IGBT]
    fullctl --> gto[GTO]
    fullctl --> igct[IGCT]
    
    widebg --> sic[SiC MOSFET]
    widebg --> gan[GaN HEMT]
    
    classDef lvl0 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lvl1 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef lvl2 fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    
    class root lvl0
    class uncontrolled,semictl,fullctl,widebg lvl1
    class diode,scr,triac,lascr,bjt,mosfet,igbt,gto,igct,sic,gan lvl2
```

**Caption:** Hierarchical classification of power semiconductor devices based on controllability.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Power Electronics. List any four applications of power electronics in the modern world.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks – Board-Standard Wording):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Power Electronics is the technology of converting and controlling electric power from one form to another using high-power electronic switching devices. The conversion is performed by **power semiconductor switches** that operate in either the cut-off (OFF) or saturation (ON) region, resulting in high efficiency (typically >90%).
>
> **Applications (Any 4, ¼ Mark each):**
> 1. Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for industrial motors
> 2. Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS) in electronics
> 3. Solar PV grid-tie inverters
> 4. Electric Vehicle (EV) powertrains and chargers
> 5. HVDC power transmission systems
> 6. Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)
>
> *(Examiner tip: At least 4 applications are mandatory for full 3 marks.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is a power semiconductor device? Differentiate between uncontrolled and fully-controlled power devices with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** A power semiconductor device is a solid-state electronic switch designed to handle **high voltages (100 V to 10 kV)** and **high currents (1 A to several kA)** in power conversion systems.
>
> **Difference (2 Marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Uncontrolled Devices | Fully-Controlled Devices |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Turn-ON Control** | Not possible (happens automatically when forward biased) | Achieved via gate/base signal |
> | **Turn-OFF Control** | Not possible (turns off only when current falls to zero naturally) | Achieved via gate/base signal |
> | **Examples** | Power Diode | BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO |
> | **Applications** | Rectifiers, Freewheeling | PWM Inverters, Choppers, SMPS |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for Module 1]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the general structure of a power electronic system. List the functions of each block. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A power electronic system consists of **four major functional blocks**:

1. **Source (1 Mark):** Provides raw electrical energy — may be AC mains, DC battery, or renewable sources (PV, wind). Specifies the input voltage/current rating.

2. **Power Converter (2 Marks):** The **heart of the system** comprising one or more power semiconductor switches (Diodes, Thyristors, IGBTs, MOSFETs). It performs the actual energy transformation:
   - **AC → DC** (Rectifier)
   - **DC → AC** (Inverter)
   - **DC → DC** (Chopper)
   - **AC → AC** (Cycloconverter/AC Voltage Controller)

3. **Control Circuit / Gating Unit (2 Marks):** A low-power electronic circuit (using op-amps, microcontrollers, DSPs, or dedicated gate driver ICs) that generates **precise gating pulses** ($V_{GS}$ for MOSFET, $V_{GE}$ for IGBT) to switch the power devices ON/OFF at the correct instants. It receives feedback signals from sensors.

4. **Load (1 Mark):** The end-application receiving the converted power — motors, lamps, heaters, grid, batteries, etc.

5. **Feedback/Sensing Block (1 Mark):** Monitors output parameters (voltage, current, speed, temperature) and feeds them back to the controller for closed-loop regulation and protection.

> **Block Diagram Reference:** See Mermaid Diagram 1 in SECTION 4.

---

**b) Classify power semiconductor devices based on their controllability. Explain the operating principle of any one fully-controlled device. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (3 Marks):**

| Category | Control | Examples | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Uncontrolled | No gate control | Power Diode | Conducts when forward biased |
| Semi-Controlled | Turn-ON controlled | SCR, TRIAC, LASCR | Turn-OFF by natural commutation only |
| Fully-Controlled | Both turn-ON and turn-OFF controlled | BJT, Power MOSFET, IGBT, GTO, IGCT | Fast switching, PWM operation possible |
| Modern Wide-Bandgap | Fully controlled, ultra-fast | SiC MOSFET, GaN HEMT | High frequency, high temperature |

**Operating Principle of Power MOSFET (4 Marks):**

The **Power MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field Effect Transistor)** is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier device** with three terminals: **Gate (G), Drain (D), Source (S)**.

- When a **positive gate-source voltage ($V_{GS}$)** exceeding the threshold voltage ($V_{th}$, typically 2–4 V) is applied, an **inversion layer (n-channel)** forms under the gate oxide, creating a conducting path between Drain and Source.
- The device turns **ON**, and current flows from Drain to Source.
- When $V_{GS} = 0$ (or negative), the channel disappears, and the device turns **OFF**.
- **Switching times are extremely short** ($t_{on}, t_{off} \approx 20$–$100$ ns), making it ideal for **high-frequency SMPS** applications.
- The **ON-state resistance ($R_{DS(on)}$)** causes conduction loss: $P_{cond} = I_{D}^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$.
- Unlike the BJT, the MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled device** — almost no gate current is required, making drive design simple.

**Advantages of MOSFET over BJT:** Faster switching, no secondary breakdown, voltage-controlled input, parallel operation easy.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks — Internal Choice)

**a) Explain the four basic functions of power electronics with one suitable application for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Function | Conversion | Circuit Used | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rectification** | AC → DC | Diode bridge / SCR rectifier | Battery charger, SMPS front-end |
| 2 | **Inversion** | DC → AC | IGBT/MOSFET-based PWM inverter | Solar PV inverter, VFD for motor |
| 3 | **Chopping (DC-DC)** | DC (Fixed) → DC (Variable) | Buck / Boost / Buck-Boost | EV battery management, LED drivers |
| 4 | **AC-AC Conversion** | AC (Fixed) → AC (Variable) | AC voltage controller / Cycloconverter | Light dimmer, induction heater, aircraft VSCF systems |

Each function uses **one or more power semiconductor switches** in a specific topology to achieve the desired energy transformation with high efficiency.

---

**b) Derive the expression for efficiency of a power electronic converter. A converter delivers 2.4 kW to a load. If the input power is 2.6 kW, calculate the efficiency and total power loss. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Apply conservation of energy:

$$P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss}$$

By definition, efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

Substituting $P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss}$:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{in} - P_{loss}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$$

In percentage:

$$\eta (\%) = \left(1 - \frac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}\right) \times 100\%$$

**Numerical Calculation (3 Marks):**

Given: $P_{out} = 2.4$ kW, $P_{in} = 2.6$ kW

**Step 1:** Power loss

$$P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 2.6 - 2.4 = 0.2 \text{ kW} = 200 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{2.4}{2.6} \times 100\% = 92.31\%$$

**Result:** The converter operates at **92.31% efficiency** with a total power loss of **200 W**. This is typical of well-designed SMPS systems operating in switched mode.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SCR with Triac:** Triac = bidirectional (AC); SCR = unidirectional (DC-side). Many students write "SCR can be used for AC control" — this is **wrong**; use Triac.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Always write **W, kW, %, Hz** explicitly. KTU examiners deduct **0.5 marks** per missing unit.
> 3. **Writing "MOSFET is current-controlled":** It is **voltage-controlled**. This conceptual error can cost **2 marks** in Part B derivations.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram in (a)-part questions:** A block diagram is **MANDATORY** in KTU. Without it, maximum 60% of allotted marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "derivation step" in efficiency question:** Direct substitution without showing the algebraic manipulation results in **partial marking only**.
> 6. **Mixing up "fully-controlled" and "semi-controlled":** SCR is **semi-controlled** (turn-ON only by gate). IGBT is **fully-controlled**.
> 7. **Not specifying switching frequency range in applications:** Examiners expect specific numbers (e.g., SMPS: $f_s = 50$–$500$ kHz).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- [x] **Power Electronics = Conversion + Control of electric power** using semiconductor switches
- [x] Generic system: **Source → Converter → Load** with **Controller (gating)** and **Feedback**
- [x] **Four canonical functions:** Rectification, Inversion, Chopping, AC-AC Conversion
- [x] **Power semiconductor device families:** Diode (uncontrolled), SCR/TRIAC (semi-controlled), BJT/MOSFET/IGBT/GTO/IGCT (fully-controlled), SiC/GaN (wide-bandgap)
- [x] **IGBT = combination of MOSFET (input) + BJT (output)** — most widely used in medium-power VFDs
- [x] **Power MOSFET** = voltage-controlled, majority carrier, ultra-fast, used in SMPS
- [x] **Thyristor (SCR)** = latching device, turn-ON by gate, turn-OFF by natural commutation, used in HVDC
- [x] **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}\right) \times 100\%$
- [x] **Switching loss:** $P_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{DC} \cdot I_L \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_s}{6}$
- [x] **Conduction loss:** $P_{cond} = V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg}$ (for IGBT) or $I^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ (for MOSFET)
- [x] **Modern trends:** Wide-bandgap SiC/GaN devices → higher $f_s$ → smaller magnetics → higher power density
- [x] **Application power range:** <1 W (chargers) to >1 GW (HVDC)
- [x] **KTU 2024 examiner focus areas:** Block diagram, classification table, efficiency derivation, device comparison
- [x] **Always mention:** Operating region (cut-off/saturation), switching frequency, and control type in any device description

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Board Tip:** Before writing your ESE answer, always **underline the key terms** (e.g., *fully-controlled*, *voltage-controlled*, *majority carrier*) — KTU examiners give **bonus visibility marks** for clean, highlighted terminology.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Diodes and Bipolar Power Transistors - Static and Dynamic Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Power Diodes and Bipolar Power Transistors - Static and Dynamic Characteristics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Power Diodes — Formal Definition A **Power Diode** is a two-terminal, uncontrolled semiconductor switching device designed to handle **high current (tens to thousands of amperes)** and **high reverse voltage (hundreds to thousands of volts)**. Unlike signal-level diodes, power diodes are engineered with a vertically-oriented, lightly doped **N⁻ drift region** that gives them a high reverse blocking capability but also introduces a substantial forward voltage drop (typically **0.7 V to 2.5 V** depending on the rating). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must understand the *static* (V-I) and *dynamic* (reverse recovery) behaviour of power diodes because they appear as freewheeling, rectifier, and clamp elements in nearly every power converter. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Heavy-Duty One-Way Valve" Imagine a heavy industrial water check-valve used in a dam pipeline: - In the **forward direction**, it allows huge volumes of water to flow, but with a small pressure loss (akin to forward voltage drop `V_F`). - In the **reverse direction**, it slams shut and can withstand enormous back-pressure without leaking (akin to reverse blocking voltage `V_RRM`). - However, when the flow is suddenly reversed, the valve flap does **not close instantly** — water sloshes back for a brief instant before sealing. This momentary "back-flow" is exactly the **reverse recovery** phenomenon (`trr`, `Qrr`) of a power diode. ## 1.2 Bipolar Power Transistors (Power BJTs) — Formal Definition A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** in power electronics is a **current-controlled, three-terminal** semiconductor switch (C, B, E) used primarily as a high-current switch. Power BJTs are **vertical npn structures** with a wide, lightly doped **N⁻ collector drift region** to support high blocking voltages. They are operated as **switches** in the **cut-off (OFF)** or **saturation (ON)** regions — rarely in the active region except during brief switching transients. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Static characteristics** describe the device behaviour under **DC / steady-state** conditions (ON-state, OFF-state, breakdown). > **Dynamic characteristics** describe the device behaviour during **transient switching** intervals (turn-on delay, storage time, turn-off, etc.). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Power Diode — Structure & Operating Principle A modern power diode is a **P⁺-N⁻-N⁺** three-layer structure: - **P⁺ emitter**: Heavily doped to inject a high density of holes. - **N⁻ drift (base) region**: Lightly doped and wide — supports the reverse blocking voltage. - **N⁺ substrate**: Provides low-resistance ohmic contact and mechanical support. ### 2.1.1 Static V-I Characteristics The static V-I curve of a power diode is divided into three regions: | Region | Condition | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **Forward Bias** | `V_AK > 0` (≈ 0.7–2.5 V) | Conducts large current with small drop; ON-state resistance `R_ON ≈ V_F / I_F` | | **Reverse Blocking** | `V_AK < 0`, `\|V_AK\| < V_BR` | Carries only a tiny leakage current `I_R` (µA to mA range) | | **Reverse Breakdown** | `\|V_AK\| > V_BR` | Avalanche multiplication; destructive unless current is limited | > [!NOTE] > The forward voltage drop has **two components**: > 1. Junction drop `V_j ≈ 0.7 V` (depends on bandgap and temperature) > 2. Resistive drop in the drift region: `I_F · R_drift` > Total: `V_F = V_j + I_F · R_drift` ### 2.1.2 Key Static Ratings of Power Diodes - **I_F(AV)** — Average forward current - **I_F(RMS)** — RMS forward current - **I_FSM** — Non-repetitive surge current - **V_RRM** — Peak repetitive reverse voltage - **V_RSM** — Peak non-repetitive reverse voltage - **P_R** — Maximum reverse power dissipation - **R_thJC, R_thJA** — Thermal resistance (junction-case, junction-ambient) ## 2.2 Power Diode — Dynamic Characteristics (Reverse Recovery) When a forward-conducting diode is suddenly reverse-biased, it does **not turn off instantly**. A large reverse current flows for a short time to **sweep out the stored minority carriers** in the N⁻ region before reverse blocking is established. This phenomenon is called **Reverse Recovery**. ### 2.2.1 Reverse Recovery Phases 1. **Storage phase (t_s)** — Diode still conducts; current reverses and rises to peak reverse current `I_RR`. 2. **Decay phase (t_f)** — Reverse current falls from `I_RR` to zero (or to leakage level `I_RM`). 3. **Total reverse recovery time:** `t_rr = t_s + t_f` ### 2.2.2 Types of Reverse Recovery Softness - **Snappy recovery**: Abrupt fall of current (hard turn-off, large `dv/dt`, EMI). - **Soft recovery**: Gradual fall (controlled, low EMI — preferred in power electronics). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reverse recovery charge `Q_rr`** is the area under the reverse current curve: > `Q_rr ≈ ½ · I_RR · t_rr` (for a triangular approximation) > This charge causes **additional switching losses** and is critical in high-frequency rectifiers, choppers, and SMPS. ## 2.3 Power Diode Types | Type | Reverse Recovery | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Line-frequency (Standard) Rectifier** | Slow (trr ≈ 25 µs) | 50/60 Hz rectifiers, battery chargers | | **Fast-Recovery Diode (FRD)** | Fast (trr ≈ 100 ns–1 µs) | Inductive switching, freewheeling | | **Schottky Diode** | None (no minority carriers) | Low-voltage (< 200 V) high-frequency SMPS | | **Ultrafast-Recovery Diode** | Very fast (trr < 100 ns) | High-frequency inverters, resonant converters | ## 2.4 Power BJT — Structure & Operating Principle A power BJT is a **vertical npn** device with three layers: - **N⁺ substrate** (collector contact at the bottom) - **N⁻ epitaxial drift layer** (supports high reverse voltage) - **P-type base** (diffused) - **N⁺ emitter** (diffused fingers for uniform current spreading) The wide **N⁻ drift region** is what differentiates a power BJT from a small-signal BJT — it gives high breakdown voltage but raises the saturation voltage `V_CE(sat)`. ### 2.4.1 Static Output (V-I) Characteristics The output characteristics (`I_C` vs `V_CE`) have four regions: | Region | V_CE | I_B | Application in PE | |---|---|---|---| | **Cut-off** | V_CE > V_CE(sat) | I_B = 0 | OFF-state switch | | **Active (Forward)** | V_CE > V_CE(sat) | I_B > 0 | Linear amp (rarely used in PE) | | **Saturation (Hard)** | V_CE ≈ V_CE(sat) (0.2–2 V) | I_B > I_C / h_FE | ON-state switch | | **Quasi-saturation** | V_CE small, I_C moderate | moderate I_B | Switching transition | ### 2.4.2 Critical Static Parameters of Power BJT - **V_CBO** — Collector-Base breakdown (emitter open) — highest rating - **V_CEO** — Collector-Emitter breakdown (base open) — usually the **limiting** rating - **V_CE(sus)** — Sustained collector-emitter voltage with reverse-biased base - **V_CER** — V_CEO with a base-emitter resistor R_BE - **V_CE(sat)** — ON-state voltage drop - **h_FE (β)** — DC common-emitter current gain (typically 5–20 for power BJTs) - **I_C(max)** — Maximum continuous collector current - **P_D** — Maximum power dissipation > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse `V_CBO` and `V_CEO`. Remember: `V_CEO < V_CBO` because with base open, the transistor enters avalanche multiplication and **secondary breakdown** occurs at a lower voltage. Always use `V_CEO` as the design limit. ## 2.5 Power BJT — Dynamic Switching Characteristics The BJT does not switch instantly because of **charge storage in the base region** and the finite transit time of carriers. ### 2.5.1 Turn-On Transient (4 sub-intervals) | Interval | Description | Approx. Duration | |---|---|---| | **t_d (delay time)** | Time for I_B to charge base-emitter capacitance to 0.7 V; I_C still 0 | 10–100 ns | | **t_ri (rise time)** | I_C rises from 10% to 90% of final value; transistor moves from active to saturation | 50–500 ns | | **t_on = t_d + t_ri** | Total turn-on time | 100 ns–1 µs | ### 2.5.2 Turn-Off Transient (3 sub-intervals) | Interval | Description | Approx. Duration | |---|---|---| | **t_s (storage time)** | Time to remove the **excess stored base charge** in saturation; I_C still ≈ I_C(sat) | 0.5–10 µs (longest!) | | **t_fi (fall time)** | I_C falls from 90% to 10%; transistor moves from active to cut-off | 50–500 ns | | **t_off = t_s + t_fi** | Total turn-off time | 1–20 µs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Storage time t_s is the dominant switching loss contributor** in a BJT. To minimise t_s, the base must be driven with a **negative current** during turn-off (reverse bias) — this is called **Baker's clamp** or **anti-saturation** driving. ### 2.5.3 Safe Operating Area (SOA) The **Forward-Biased SOA (FBSOA)** and **Reverse-Biased SOA (RBSOA)** define the safe (V_CE, I_C) combinations the BJT can sustain without: - **Thermal runaway** - **Secondary breakdown** - **Exceeding `P_D`** SOA is bounded by: - I_C(max) (horizontal current limit) - V_CEO (vertical voltage limit) - **P_D = V_CE · I_C** hyperbola (DC power limit) - **Secondary breakdown locus** (for FBSOA, only during turn-off) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Symbol | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | V_F | `V_j + I_F · R_drift` | Forward voltage drop | V | | I_R | Leakage current in reverse blocking | Static | µA – mA | | t_rr | `t_s + t_f` | Total reverse recovery time | µs / ns | | Q_rr | `∫ I_R dt ≈ ½ · I_RR · t_rr` | Reverse recovery charge | µC | | S-factor | `t_f / t_s` | Softness factor (soft ≈ 1, snappy < 0.3) | – | | V_CE(sat) | ≈ 0.2 – 2 V | ON-state drop | V | | I_B(sat) | `\gt I_C / h_FE` | Base current for saturation | A | | t_on | `t_d + t_ri` | Turn-on time | ns – µs | | t_off | `t_s + t_fi` | Turn-off time | µs | | P_cond(BJT) | `V_CE(sat) · I_C` | Conduction loss | W | | P_sw(BJT) | `½ · V_CC · I_C · (t_on + t_off) · f_s` | Switching loss | W | | P_tot | `P_cond + P_sw` | Total power loss | W | | T_J | `T_A + P_tot · R_thJA` | Junction temperature | °C | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Power diodes and BJTs are the building blocks of AC-DC rectifiers, DC-DC choppers, and early voltage-source inverters. Even though IGBTs and MOSFETs dominate modern designs, understanding diode reverse recovery and BJT storage time is essential for analysing *switching losses* and *EMI* in *every* converter topology. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Voltage Drop in a Power Diode The forward voltage across a P⁺-N⁻ junction under high injection has two distinct components: the **junction barrier drop** and the **drift-region ohmic drop**. ### Step 1 — Apply the Ambipolar Transport Equation Under high-level injection, the injected minority carrier density `p(x)` in the N⁻ drift region is governed by the ambipolar diffusion equation: $$D_a \cdot \frac{d^2 p(x)}{dx^2} = \frac{p(x)}{\tau_a}$$ where: - `D_a` = ambipolar diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) - `τ_a` = ambipolar carrier lifetime (s) - `x` = distance from the P⁺-N⁻ junction ### Step 2 — Solve for Carrier Profile The general solution with boundary condition `p(0) = p_0` and `p(W_n) = 0` is: $$p(x) = p_0 \cdot \frac{\sinh\left(\frac{W_n - x}{L_a}\right)}{\sinh\left(\frac{W_n}{L_a}\right)}$$ where `L_a = \sqrt{D_a \cdot \tau_a}` is the ambipolar diffusion length. ### Step 3 — Total Forward Current Using the diffusion current density at `x = 0`: $$J_F = 2 \cdot q \cdot D_a \cdot \frac{dp}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0}$$ Carrying out the differentiation: $$J_F = \frac{2 \cdot q \cdot D_a \cdot p_0}{L_a} \cdot \coth\left(\frac{W_n}{L_a}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Voltage Across the Drift Region The voltage drop across the drift region (modulated conductivity) is: $$V_{drift} = \frac{J_F \cdot W_n}{q \cdot \mu_n \cdot p_0 + J_F \cdot \left(\frac{W_n}{2 \cdot L_a}\right) \cdot \coth\left(\frac{W_n}{L_a}\right)}$$ ### Step 5 — Total Forward Voltage $$V_F = V_j + V_{drift} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_0 \cdot N_D}{n_i^2}\right) + V_{drift}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical insight:** As breakdown voltage `V_BR` increases (wider `W_n`), the resistive drop `V_drift` dominates — that is why **high-voltage power diodes have larger forward voltage drops** (1.5–2.5 V) compared to low-voltage Schottky diodes (< 0.5 V). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Reverse Recovery Charge `Q_rr` ### Step 1 — Stored Minority Charge at Turn-Off At `t = 0`, the diode carries forward current `I_F`. The stored charge in the drift region is: $$Q_s = I_F \cdot \tau_a$$ where `τ_a` is the ambipolar lifetime. ### Step 2 — Constant `di/dt` Turn-Off (Inductive Load) When the circuit applies a constant `di/dt = -I_F / t_s`, the reverse current is: $$i_R(t) = \frac{I_F \cdot t}{t_s}, \quad 0 \le t \le t_s$$ ### Step 3 — Reverse Recovery Charge The charge swept out during the storage phase is: $$Q_{rr} = \int_0^{t_{rr}} i_R(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$ For a triangular approximation with peak reverse current `I_RR`: $$Q_{rr} \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$ ### Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU-style) A diode carries `I_F = 100 A` and is switched at `di/dt = -200 A/µs`. The storage time is `t_s = 2 µs`. Peak reverse current: $$I_{RR} = \left|\frac{di}{dt}\right| \cdot t_s = 200 \cdot 2 = 400 \text{ A}$$ Reverse recovery charge: $$Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 400 \cdot 2 = 400 \text{ µC}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering meaning:** This 400 µC of charge must be *carried* by the switching transistor, causing **extra turn-on losses** and **voltage spikes** in inductive circuits. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: BJT Switching Times ### Step 3.3.1 Turn-On Delay `t_d` The base-emitter capacitance `C_be` must charge from 0 to `V_BE(on) ≈ 0.7 V` through base-drive resistance `R_B`. Treating it as an RC transient: $$t_d \approx R_B \cdot C_{be} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{BB}}{V_{BB} - V_{BE(on)}}\right)$$ For `V_BB >> V_BE(on)`: $$t_d \approx 0.69 \cdot R_B \cdot C_{be}$$ ### Step 3.3.2 Rise Time `t_ri` During rise time, the collector current ramps from 10% to 90% of the saturated value `I_CS`: $$t_{ri} \approx \frac{I_{CS}}{g_m \cdot V_{BB} / R_B} = \frac{I_{CS} \cdot R_B \cdot h_{FE}}{V_{BB} \cdot g_m}$$ A more practical empirical relation used in KTU textbooks: $$t_{ri} = \frac{h_{FE} \cdot (R_C \vert\vert R_L) \cdot C_{bc}}{R_B}$$ ### Step 3.3.3 Storage Time `t_s` (the dominant one) The excess base charge `Q_ex` must be removed. With a negative base drive `-I_B2`: $$t_s = \tau_s \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{I_{CM} / h_{FE} + I_{B2}}\right)$$ where: - `τ_s` = storage time constant - `I_B1` = forward base current - `I_B2` = reverse base current magnitude - `I_CM` = peak collector current - `h_FE` = DC gain > [!NOTE] > **To minimise t_s**, increase `I_B2` (strong reverse base drive) and reduce saturation (use anti-saturation clamp). ### Step 3.3.4 Fall Time `t_fi` $$t_{fi} = h_{FE} \cdot C_{bc} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{I_{B2} \cdot R_L}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: BJT Region Identification (KTU-Favourite) | Given V_CE, I_C, I_B | Region | Equation Relating Quantities | Switching Application | |---|---|---|---| | I_B = 0, I_C = I_CEO | **Cut-off** | I_C = I_CEO | Switch is OFF | | I_B > 0, I_C = h_FE · I_B, V_CE > V_CE(sat) | **Active (Forward)** | I_C = h_FE · I_B | Linear amp (avoid in PE) | | I_B > I_C / h_FE, V_CE = V_CE(sat) | **Saturation** | V_CE = V_CE(sat) | Switch is ON | --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank] **Explain the reverse recovery characteristics of a power diode with a neat sketch.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** When a forward-conducting power diode is suddenly reverse-biased, it does not turn off instantly. The stored minority carriers in the N⁻ drift region must be removed before reverse blocking is established. The reverse recovery process has two phases: 1. **Storage time `t_s`**: The diode continues to conduct; reverse current rises from zero to peak reverse current `I_RR`. The storage charge `Q_rr = ½ · I_RR · t_s`. 2. **Fall time `t_f`**: The reverse current decays from `I_RR` to the reverse leakage value `I_R`. The total reverse recovery time is `t_rr = t_s + t_f`. **Soft recovery** diodes (used in modern PE) have gradual decay, reducing EMI and switching stress. Snappy recovery causes high `dv/dt` and is avoided. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1] **Define the Safe Operating Area (SOA) of a power BJT. List its boundaries.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power BJT is the (V_CE, I_C) region over which the transistor can be safely operated without thermal runaway or secondary breakdown. It is bounded by four limits: 1. **I_C(max)** — Maximum rated collector current (horizontal limit). 2. **V_CEO** — Maximum collector-emitter voltage (vertical limit). 3. **P_D(max) = V_CE · I_C** — DC power dissipation hyperbola. 4. **Secondary breakdown locus (S/B)** — Critical during turn-off transients. **FBSOA** is for forward-biased operation; **RBSOA** is for reverse-biased (inductive) turn-off. Pulse operation extends the SOA because average heating is reduced. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 1, Part B] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **static V-I characteristics** of a power diode. Discuss the constructional features that give a power diode its high voltage and current capability. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **reverse recovery transient** of a power diode with a neat waveform. Derive the expression for reverse recovery charge `Q_rr` and explain its significance in chopper operation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — Static V-I Characteristics & Construction (7 marks) **Construction of a Power Diode (3 marks):** A power diode has a vertical **P⁺-N⁻-N⁺** structure: - **P⁺ emitter layer**: Heavily doped to inject large hole concentrations into the drift region. - **N⁻ drift region (base)**: The thickness `W_n` of this layer is proportional to the breakdown voltage `V_BR`. A wide `W_n` supports a high reverse voltage. It is lightly doped to widen the depletion region. - **N⁺ substrate**: Provides a low-resistance ohmic contact at the cathode and mechanical support. - The vertical structure allows **uniform current flow** over a large cross-section, enabling high current ratings. **Static V-I Characteristics (4 marks):** The static V-I curve has three regions: | Region | Behaviour | Key Equation | |---|---|---| | **Forward bias** (`V_AK > 0.7 V`) | Diode conducts large current with a small drop | `V_F = V_j + I_F · R_drift` | | **Reverse blocking** (`V_AK < 0`, `|V_AK| < V_BR`) | Carries only leakage `I_R` (µA) | – | | **Reverse breakdown** (`|V_AK| > V_BR`) | Avalanche multiplication; destructive if not limited | – | At rated forward current, `V_F ≈ 0.7 – 2.5 V` depending on `V_BR`. Higher voltage rating ⇒ wider drift region ⇒ larger `V_F`. #### Model Solution (b) — Reverse Recovery (7 marks) **Waveform Description (3 marks):** (See diagram in Section 4.3.) At `t = 0`, forward current `I_F` flows. After reverse bias is applied: - From `t = 0` to `t = t_s`: Reverse current rises linearly to peak `I_RR` (storage phase). Diode is still ON. - From `t = t_s` to `t = t_s + t_f`: Reverse current decays to leakage level `I_R` (fall phase). The diode begins to block. - Total: `t_rr = t_s + t_f`. **Derivation of `Q_rr` (3 marks):** For a linear `di/dt` turn-off, reverse current is: $$i_R(t) = I_F - \left|\frac{di}{dt}\right| t$$ Peak reverse current at `t = t_s`: $$I_{RR} = \left|\frac{di}{dt}\right| \cdot t_s$$ Reverse recovery charge (area under reverse current): $$Q_{rr} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_{RR} \cdot t_{rr}$$ **Significance in Chopper Operation (1 mark):** In a chopper, the reverse recovery of the freewheeling diode causes: - Additional **turn-on switching loss** in the controlled switch. - **Voltage spikes** across the diode due to sudden di/dt. - **EMI** and possible device failure if `Q_rr` is large. - **Soft-recovery FRDs** are therefore preferred in chopper and inverter circuits. --- ### Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 1, Part B] **(a)** Draw and explain the **static output characteristics** of a power BJT in CE configuration. Identify the cut-off, active, quasi-saturation, and hard-saturation regions, and explain their relevance to power switching. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat switching waveform, define **turn-on time `t_on` and turn-off time `t_off`** for a power BJT. Explain the **Baker's clamp** circuit and how it reduces the storage time. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — BJT Static Output Characteristics (7 marks) **Output Characteristics (`I_C` vs `V_CE`) (4 marks):** For a family of base currents `I_B1 < I_B2 < ... < I_Bn`, the curve has four regions: 1. **Cut-off region**: `I_B = 0`, only `I_CEO` flows. `V_CE > V_CE(sat)`. The transistor is fully OFF. 2. **Active (forward) region**: `I_C = h_FE · I_B`, V_CE > V_CE(sat). Transistor acts as a linear amplifier — **not desired** in power switching. 3. **Quasi-saturation**: The collector drift region is partially filled with carriers. V_CE is slightly above V_CE(sat), I_C is less than h_FE · I_B. 4. **Hard saturation**: V_CE = V_CE(sat) (0.2–2 V). Both junctions are forward-biased. **This is the ON-state of the switch**. **Relevance to Power Switching (3 marks):** - A power BJT used as a switch must be in **cut-off (OFF)** or **hard saturation (ON)** to minimise losses. - Conduction loss: `P_cond = V_CE(sat) · I_C`. - The switch **passes through** the active region during turn-on and turn-off, causing switching losses. Therefore, **faster transitions are desired**. #### Model Solution (b) — Switching Times & Baker's Clamp (7 marks) **Switching Waveform (3 marks):** (See diagram in Section 4.5.) - **t_d (delay time)**: From base drive application to I_C = 0.1 I_CS. Time to charge base-emitter capacitance. - **t_ri (rise time)**: I_C rises from 0.1 I_CS to 0.9 I_CS. - **t_on = t_d + t_ri**: Total turn-on time. - **t_s (storage time)**: Excess minority charge is removed; I_C still ≈ I_CS. **Longest interval.** - **t_fi (fall time)**: I_C falls from 0.9 I_CS to 0.1 I_CS. - **t_off = t_s + t_fi**: Total turn-off time. **Baker's Clamp (4 marks):** The **Baker's clamp** (anti-saturation network) prevents hard saturation, thereby reducing the stored base charge and storage time. **Circuit:** Two diodes `D1` and `D2` are connected in series between the collector and base, and a small resistor `R` is added in the base path. The diodes are forward-biased by the difference between `V_CE(sat)` and `V_BE(on)`. **Operation:** - During saturation, the diodes conduct, and a fraction of the collector current is diverted back to the base driver. - The collector-emitter voltage is clamped to approximately: $$V_{CE} \approx V_{BE} + V_{D1} + V_{D2} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2 \text{ V}$$ - This keeps the transistor in **quasi-saturation** with less stored charge. - Result: **`t_s` reduces dramatically** (by 3–5×), at the cost of a slightly higher `V_CE(sat)` (higher conduction loss). **Trade-off:** `t_s ↓` but `V_CE(sat) ↑` ⇒ lower switching loss, higher conduction loss. The designer chooses the optimum. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing `V_CBO` and `V_CEO`**: `V_CEO` is *smaller* than `V_CBO` and is the *practical* limit. Always use `V_CEO` in switch design. > 2. **Forgetting to define t_rr = t_s + t_f**: Many students only write `t_s` or `t_f`. Both must be present. > 3. **Saying BJT is "voltage-controlled"**: BJTs are **current-controlled** devices. This is a 1-mark sure-shot trap. > 4. **Quoting I_C = h_FE · I_B in saturation**: This relation is for the **active** region only. In saturation, `I_C < h_FE · I_B`. > 5. **Ignoring the leakage current** in reverse blocking: Always mention `I_R` and that it grows with temperature. > 6. **Not mentioning the storage time dominance**: When asked "which is the largest switching interval in a BJT?", answer is `t_s`, not `t_ri` or `t_fi`. > 7. **No waveform in the answer**: KTU examiners explicitly demand a *neat labelled sketch* for static and dynamic characteristics. **Always draw a figure**, even if the question does not say "with sketch". --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Power Diodes & Power BJTs (Module 1)** - 🔹 **Power Diode structure** is **P⁺-N⁻-N⁺**. The wide N⁻ drift region supports reverse voltage but increases forward drop. - 🔹 **Forward drop** `V_F = V_j + I_F · R_drift`; typical value **0.7 – 2.5 V** for rectifier diodes. - 🔹 **Reverse blocking** has only a small leakage current `I_R` (µA–mA). - 🔹 **Reverse recovery** is the *dynamic* phenomenon: `t_rr = t_s + t_f`, charge `Q_rr = ½ · I_RR · t_rr`. - 🔹 **Soft recovery** FRDs are preferred in choppers/inverters to limit EMI. - 🔹 **Schottky diodes** have *no* reverse recovery (no minority carriers) but limited to < 200 V. - 🔹 **Power BJT is a vertical npn** switch used in **cut-off** and **saturation** regions only. - 🔹 **V_CEO < V_CBO < V_CES** — always use `V_CEO` as the design rating. - 🔹 **Saturation condition**: `I_B ≥ I_C / h_FE`. Conduction loss: `P_cond = V_CE(sat) · I_C`. - 🔹 **Switching times** — `t_on = t_d + t_ri`, `t_off = t_s + t_fi`. **`t_s` is the longest**. - 🔹 **Switching loss**: `P_sw = ½ · V_CC · I_C · (t_on + t_off) · f_s`. - 🔹 **Baker's clamp** reduces `t_s` by preventing hard saturation. - 🔹 **SOA** is bounded by `I_C(max)`, `V_CEO`, `P_D`, and the **secondary breakdown locus**. - 🔹 **Darlington connection** = two BJTs cascaded → `h_FE_total = h_FE1 · h_FE2` → very high gain but slower switching. - 🔹 **Secondary breakdown** is a thermal-instability phenomenon unique to power BJTs and is the most common cause of BJT failure in switching applications. > **One-line takeaway for KTU exam:** > *"A power diode turns off via stored charge removal (Q_rr), and a power BJT turns off via storage-time removal (t_s) — both are minority-carrier devices, both suffer switching losses proportional to f_s."*

topics.title: Power MOSFET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power MOSFET**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** is a **voltage-controlled, majority-carrier (unipolar) power semiconductor device** used as a fast electronic switch in power electronic converters. It is constructed using a **vertical double-diffused (DMOS)** structure, which allows high current and high voltage blocking capability while maintaining a high input impedance and very fast switching speeds.

Unlike the **BJT**, which is a current-controlled minority-carrier device, the Power MOSFET is switched into conduction by applying a voltage greater than the **threshold voltage ($V_{th}$)** at the gate terminal relative to the source. Conduction occurs due to the flow of **majority carriers (electrons)** in an n-channel device, eliminating the storage time delay caused by minority-carrier recombination. This makes the Power MOSFET ideal for **high-frequency switching applications (typically 50 kHz to 1 MHz)**.

### Key Construction Variants
- **VDMOS (Vertical Double-Diffused MOSFET):** Most common in commercial power MOSFETs.
- **VMOS (V-groove MOSFET):** Older technology, now obsolete.
- **UMOS (Trench MOSFET):** Uses a vertical trench gate; lower on-state resistance.
- **HEXFET (Hexagonal cell MOSFET – International Rectifier):** Multiple cells in parallel for higher current.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Focus on the construction of vertical channel MOSFET, its I-V characteristics, switching characteristics, safe operating area, and comparison with BJT/IGBT.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **high-pressure water tap** connected to a large overhead tank.

- The **Gate (G)** is like the **handwheel of the tap**. You only need to turn it a little (apply a small voltage) — it does not require great physical strength (low gate current, high input impedance).
- The **Drain (D)** is the **inlet pipe** from the tank (where current enters).
- The **Source (S)** is the **outlet pipe** (where current leaves to the load).
- The **oxide layer** under the gate acts like a **rubber gasket** that prevents any leakage between the handwheel and the water.
- When you rotate the handwheel beyond a certain point (apply $V_{GS} > V_{th}$), a **channel opens** like a pathway for water, and current flows.
- Because the handwheel only controls a small mechanical link, the tap opens/closes **almost instantly** — which is why MOSFETs are **fast switches**.

## 1.3 Physical Symbols & Standard Notation

- **Drain current ($I_D$):** Measured in **Amperes (A)**.
- **Drain–Source voltage ($V_{DS}$):** Measured in **Volts (V)** — typically rated from **50 V to 1000 V** in commercial devices.
- **Gate–Source voltage ($V_{GS}$):** Standard drive voltage is **+10 V to +15 V** (logic-level MOSFETs accept **+4.5 V**).
- **On-state resistance ($R_{DS(on)}$):** Measured in **milliohms (mΩ)** — a critical figure-of-merit.
- **Threshold voltage ($V_{th}$):** Typically **2 V to 4 V** for standard power MOSFETs.
- **Maximum junction temperature ($T_{J,max}$):** Usually **150 °C** or **175 °C**.

> [!TIP]
> Always check the **gate charge ($Q_g$)** when designing a gate drive circuit — it determines how much current the driver must supply to switch the MOSFET rapidly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Vertical Power MOSFET (VDMOS)

The standard Power MOSFET uses a **vertical current flow** path to achieve high current capability. Its key features are:

1. **n+ Substrate (Drain):** Heavily doped silicon wafer forming the bottom drain contact.
2. **n– Drift Region (Epitaxial Layer):** Lightly doped layer that **supports the high blocking voltage** when the device is OFF.
3. **p-base (Body) Region:** Diffused into the n– epilayer; forms the body diode and the channel region.
4. **n+ Source Region:** Heavily doped region diffused into the p-base; provides the source contact.
5. **Gate Oxide (SiO₂):** Thin insulating layer between the gate metal and the silicon — the key to high input impedance.
6. **Gate (Polysilicon/Metal):** When $V_{GS} > V_{th}$, an **inversion layer (n-channel)** forms in the p-base just under the oxide, connecting n+ source to n– drift region.

> [!NOTE]
> The p-base and the n+ source are shorted together at the source terminal via a **metallization strap**. This prevents the parasitic n-p-n BJT (formed by n+ source / p-base / n– drift) from accidentally turning ON and causing **latch-up destruction**.

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **OFF State ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$):** No inversion layer exists. The p-n junction between the p-base and the n– drift region blocks voltage. The device supports the full $V_{DS}$ in the reverse-biased junction.
2. **ON State ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$):** An **n-type inversion layer** is induced under the gate oxide in the p-base region. This channel provides a continuous **n+ → channel → n– → n+ drain** path for electrons.
3. **Current Control:** For a given $V_{GS}$, the drain current $I_D$ depends on $V_{DS}$ in two distinct regions:
   - **Triode (Ohmic / Linear) Region:** $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ — the device behaves like a **voltage-controlled resistor** of value $R_{DS(on)}$.
   - **Saturation (Active) Region:** $V_{DS} \geq (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ — the channel is **pinched off** near the drain end, and $I_D$ becomes almost independent of $V_{DS}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Equation | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|----------------------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Drain Current (Linear / Triode Region) | $I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$ | $0 \le V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ |
| 2 | Drain Current (Saturation Region) | $I_D = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_{th}$ |
| 3 | On-State Resistance | $R_{DS(on)} = \dfrac{V_{DS}}{I_D} \bigg\vert_{V_{GS} = 10\,V}$ | Measured in **mΩ** |
| 4 | Transconductance (Saturation) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}}$ | Siemens (S) |
| 5 | Specific On-Resistance (Silicon Limit) | $R_{on,sp} = \dfrac{4 V_{BR}^2}{\varepsilon_s \mu_n E_c^3}$ | $V_{BR}$ = Breakdown voltage |
| 6 | Conduction Loss | $P_{cond} = I_{D,rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | Watts (W) |
| 7 | Switching Loss (per cycle) | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Gate Charge Energy | $E_g = Q_g \cdot V_{GS}$ | Joules (J) |
| 9 | Output Capacitance Stored Energy | $E_{oss} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{oss} V_{DS}^2$ | Joules (J) |
| 10 | Thermal Resistance | $T_J = P \cdot R_{th(JA)} + T_A$ | °C / W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Power MOSFETs operate predominantly in the **linear (triode) region** when used as switches — not in saturation. Saturation is the boundary between ON and OFF. The key design goal is to **minimize $R_{DS(on)}$** to reduce conduction loss.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Switch-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Power MOSFETs are the workhorse of DC-DC converters (buck, boost, flyback) operating at 100 kHz – 1 MHz.
- **Motor Drives:** Low-voltage (< 200 V) variable-frequency drives for BLDC and servo motors.
- **Automotive Electronics:** ECU drivers, electric vehicle traction inverters (lower voltage stages), airbag circuits.
- **RF Power Amplifiers:** Certain RF MOSFETs handle VHF/UHF amplification.
- **Solid-State Relays (SSR):** MOSFET-based SSRs replace electromechanical relays for fast, silent, long-life switching.
- **Synchronous Rectification:** Replacing Schottky diodes in low-voltage (< 5 V) high-current converters to achieve > 95% efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drain Current in the Linear (Triode) Region

**Problem Setup:** Derive the expression for the drain current $I_D$ of a power MOSFET when $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{th})$.

### Step 1 — Inversion Layer Charge Density
When a gate voltage $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ is applied, an inversion layer of electrons is formed under the oxide. The charge per unit area at any point $y$ along the channel is:

$$Q(y) = -C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right]$$

> **Logic:** The local voltage at point $y$ along the channel is $V(y)$ measured with respect to the source. The effective gate-to-channel voltage is therefore $(V_{GS} - V(y))$, and the **excess** above threshold is $(V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y))$. Multiplying by the oxide capacitance per unit area $C_{ox}$ gives the mobile electron charge density.

### Step 2 — Drift Velocity of Electrons
The lateral electric field along the channel is $E_y = -dV(y)/dy$. The electron drift velocity is $v_n = -\mu_n E_y$, where $\mu_n$ is the electron mobility. The current density is therefore:

$$J_n = -Q(y) \cdot v_n = \mu_n C_{ox} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right] \frac{dV(y)}{dy}$$

### Step 3 — Integrating Over the Channel Length
The drain current $I_D$ is constant along the channel. Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $W$ (channel width) and integrating from $y = 0$ (source end, $V = 0$) to $y = L$ (drain end, $V = V_{DS}$):

$$I_D \int_0^L dy = \mu_n C_{ox} W \int_0^{V_{DS}} \left[ V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y) \right] dV(y)$$

### Step 4 — Performing the Integration

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V - \frac{V^2}{2} \right]_0^{V_{DS}}$$

$$I_D \cdot L = \mu_n C_{ox} W \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **gradual channel approximation**. It is valid only as long as the inversion layer exists, i.e., $V_{DS} < (V_{GS} - V_{th})$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Saturation Condition & On-State Resistance

**Problem Setup:** Determine the boundary between the linear and saturation regions, and derive the on-state resistance in deep triode.

### Step 1 — Pinch-Off Condition
As $V_{DS}$ increases, the voltage across the oxide near the drain end decreases. At the critical point:

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$$

At this voltage, the inversion charge at the drain end becomes **zero** (channel pinch-off).

### Step 2 — Drain Current at Saturation
Substituting $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ into the linear-region equation:

$$I_{D,sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}{2} \right]$$

$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

### Step 3 — On-State Resistance ($V_{DS} \ll V_{GS} - V_{th}$)
For a fully-enhanced switch with large gate drive, $V_{DS}$ is very small. The $V_{DS}^2/2$ term becomes negligible:

$$I_D \approx \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS}$$

### Step 4 — Defining $R_{DS(on)}$

$$R_{DS(on)} = \frac{V_{DS}}{I_D} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})}$$

> **Logic:** This shows that $R_{DS(on)}$ is **inversely proportional** to $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$. Driving the gate harder (higher $V_{GS}$) reduces on-resistance — which is why high-side drivers push $V_{GS}$ to **+12 V or +15 V**.

### Step 5 — Temperature Coefficient
A useful engineering insight:

$$\frac{\partial R_{DS(on)}}{\partial T} > 0 \quad \text{(Positive Temperature Coefficient)}$$

> **Why it matters:** Unlike the BJT, the Power MOSFET exhibits a **positive temperature coefficient** of $R_{DS(on)}$. This means multiple MOSFETs in parallel **automatically share current** — the hotter one increases its resistance, diverting current to the cooler device. This is a major advantage over BJTs (which have negative temperature coefficient and **require** derating/snubber balancing for parallel operation).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Switching Loss Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A Power MOSFET is used in a buck converter switching at $f_{sw} = 100$ kHz with $V_{DS,max} = 200$ V, $I_D = 10$ A. Turn-on time $t_{on} = 50$ ns, turn-off time $t_{off} = 80$ ns. Find the total switching loss per switching cycle and the total switching power dissipation.

### Step 1 — Energy Lost During Turn-On
Assuming linear voltage and current transitions:

$$E_{on} = \int_0^{t_{on}} v_{DS}(t) \cdot i_D(t) \, dt \approx \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D t_{on}$$

$$E_{on} = 0.5 \times 200 \times 10 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{J} = 50 \,\mu\text{J}$$

### Step 2 — Energy Lost During Turn-Off

$$E_{off} \approx \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D t_{off} = 0.5 \times 200 \times 10 \times 80 \times 10^{-9} = 80 \,\mu\text{J}$$

### Step 3 — Total Switching Power Loss

$$P_{sw} = (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw} = (50 + 80) \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^3$$

$$P_{sw} = 130 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^5 = 13 \, \text{W}$$

### Step 4 — Conduction Loss (assuming duty ratio $D = 0.5$, $R_{DS(on)} = 0.1 \,\Omega$)
RMS current: $I_{D,rms} = I_D \sqrt{D} = 10 \times \sqrt{0.5} = 7.07$ A

$$P_{cond} = I_{D,rms}^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)} = 50 \times 0.1 = 5 \, \text{W}$$

### Step 5 — Total Power Loss

$$P_{total} = P_{sw} + P_{cond} = 13 + 5 = 18 \, \text{W}$$

> **Insight:** At 100 kHz, switching loss dominates. **Reducing $t_{on}$ and $t_{off}$** via a high-current gate driver is the most effective design lever.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Safe Operating Area (SOA)

A Power MOSFET has $V_{DS(max)} = 500$ V, $I_{D(max)} = 20$ A (continuous), and $P_{D(max)} = 150$ W.

Find the maximum allowable drain current at $V_{DS} = 300$ V for **DC operation (SOA bounded by $P_D$):**

$$I_{D,max} = \frac{P_{D,max}}{V_{DS}} = \frac{150}{300} = 0.5 \, \text{A}$$

> **Logic:** This is a common KTU trap. The rated current (20 A) is only valid for **low $V_{DS}$ (near short-circuit)**. For DC operation at high $V_{DS}$, the SOA **derates severely** due to thermal limits. The full 20 A is permissible only in the **pulsed / switching SOA** region, not DC.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Sectional Architecture of a VDMOS Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Drain Terminal D — Bottom Contact on n plus Substrate] --> B[n minus Drift Epitaxial Layer — Supports Blocking Voltage]
    B --> C[p base Body Region — Diffused into epilayer]
    C --> D[n plus Source Region — Heavily Doped n plus Inside p base]
    D --> E[Source Metallization — Straps n plus and p base Together]
    B --> F[Gate Oxide SiO2 Thin Insulating Layer]
    C --> G[Inversion Channel Induced when VGS greater than Vth]
    F --> H[Polysilicon Gate Electrode — Top Contact]
    H --> I[Gate Terminal G — High Impedance Input]
    E --> J[Source Terminal S — Common Reference]
    A --> K[Drain Terminal D — High Voltage Side]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style G fill:#00838f,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#5d4037,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** The current flows **vertically** from the drain (bottom) through the n– drift region, then **horizontally** through the inversion layer (channel) under the gate oxide, and out through the n+ source and the source metal. The n+ source and p-base are shorted at the source terminal to neutralize the parasitic BJT.

---

## 4.2 Power MOSFET Switching Waveforms (Hard Switching)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OFF State: VDS equals VDC, ID equals 0] -->|Gate drive applied| B[Turn on Delay tdon: VGS rises to Vth]
    B --> C[Current Rise tr: ID rises to Load Current]
    C --> D[Voltage Fall tf: VDS falls to ID times RDSon]
    D --> E[ON State: VDS low, ID equals Load Current]
    E -->|Gate drive removed| F[Turn off Delay tdoff: VGS falls to Miller plateau]
    F --> G[Voltage Rise: VDS rises back to VDC]
    G --> H[Current Fall: ID falls to 0]
    H --> A

    style A fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style C fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style D fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style F fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style G fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style H fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
```

> **Reading Guide:** The orange-shaded blocks represent the **switching transition intervals** where $V_{DS}$ and $I_D$ overlap — this overlap is the source of **switching losses**. The green block is the ON state, and the red block is the OFF state.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Power MOSFET in a Buck Converter

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[DC Source VDC] --> B[Power MOSFET Switch M1]
    B --> C[Switching Node SW]
    C --> D[Inductor L]
    D --> E[Output Capacitor C]
    E --> F[DC Load]
    C --> G[Freewheeling Diode OR Synchronous MOSFET M2]
    G --> A
    B --> H[Gate Driver IC]
    H --> I[PWM Controller]
    I --> H
    I --> J[Feedback from Output]
    J --> I

    style A fill:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#ef6c00,color:#000000
    style H fill:#f9a825,color:#000000
    style I fill:#5d4037,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#00838f,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** The Power MOSFET (M1) chops the DC input into a PWM waveform at the switching node. The L-C filter smooths it back to DC at the output. The freewheeling diode (or synchronous MOSFET M2) provides a path for inductor current when M1 is OFF.

---

## 4.4 Static I-V Characteristics (Symbolic Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Quadrant 1 Plot: ID vs VDS for different VGS values"] --> B["VGS1 greater than VGS2 greater than VGS3 greater than Vth"]
    B --> C["Ohmic Region: VDS small, ID rises linearly — Steep slope means low RDSon"]
    B --> D["Saturation Region: ID flattens out after pinch-off"]
    B --> E["Cutoff Region: Below the curves ID equals 0"]

    style A fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#00838f,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#f57c00,color:#000000
    style E fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading Guide:** In power switching applications, the device trajectory moves along the **load line** between the cutoff point (OFF) and the deep triode point (ON). The device spends minimal time in the saturation region (high dissipation) — fast switching is therefore essential.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Power MOSFET vs. Power BJT vs. IGBT

| Feature | Power MOSFET | Power BJT | IGBT |
|---------|--------------|-----------|------|
| **Control** | Voltage | Current | Voltage |
| **Input Impedance** | Very high | Low | Very high |
| **Carrier Type** | Majority | Minority | Minority (BJT output stage) |
| **Switching Speed** | Very fast (ns) | Slow (µs) | Moderate (hundreds of ns) |
| **Voltage Rating** | Up to ~1000 V | Up to ~1200 V | Up to ~6.5 kV |
| **Current Rating** | Up to ~500 A | Up to ~1000 A | Up to ~3600 A |
| **On-State Loss** | $I^2 R_{DS(on)}$ — voltage drop rises with current | $V_{CE(sat)}$ ~ 1-2 V (fixed-ish) | $V_{CE(sat)}$ ~ 1.5-3 V |
| **Parallel Operation** | Easy (positive tempco) | Difficult (negative tempco) | Moderate |
| **Typical Frequency** | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | < 10 kHz | 10 – 50 kHz |
| **Cost per Ampere** | High at high V | Low at high V | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define threshold voltage of a Power MOSFET. What happens to the device if $V_{GS}$ is kept below $V_{th}$?**

**Model Answer:**

The **threshold voltage ($V_{th}$)** of a Power MOSFET is the minimum gate-to-source voltage required to form a conducting **inversion layer (n-channel)** under the gate oxide, connecting the source to the drain. For a standard power MOSFET, $V_{th}$ is typically in the range of **2 V to 4 V**.

If $V_{GS} < V_{th}$:
- No inversion layer is formed; the device remains in the **cutoff (OFF) state**.
- The drain current $I_D \approx 0$.
- The device blocks the full applied $V_{DS}$ across the reverse-biased p-base / n– drift junction.
- The Power MOSFET acts as an **open switch**.

**Marking Key (3 marks):**
- Definition of $V_{th}$ — **1 mark**
- Typical value range — **1 mark**
- Consequence when $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ — **1 mark**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the body diode in a Power MOSFET? Why is it useful in converter applications?**

**Model Answer:**

The **body diode** is an inherent p-n junction diode formed between the **p-base (body)** region and the **n– drift (epitaxial layer)** of the Power MOSFET, with the n+ source acting as the anode short.

It is useful because:
1. It provides a **freewheeling path** for inductive load current when the MOSFET is switched OFF, eliminating the need for an external anti-parallel diode in many low-to-medium power applications (e.g., buck, half-bridge).
2. In **bridge-leg configurations**, the body diode of the opposite MOSFET conducts during dead-time, providing a natural commutation path.
3. It simplifies circuit topology and reduces BOM cost.

**Limitation:** Its **reverse recovery charge ($Q_{rr}$)** is higher than that of a dedicated fast diode, causing additional switching losses in hard-switched bridge applications.

**Marking Key (3 marks):**
- Origin of body diode — **1 mark**
- Freewheeling function — **1 mark**
- Reverse recovery limitation — **1 mark**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction and operating principle of a **vertical double-diffused (VDMOS) Power MOSFET** with a neat sketch. Why is the source metallization shorted to the p-base region?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A power MOSFET is operated in a switching application with $V_{GS} = 12$ V, $R_{DS(on)} = 0.15 \,\Omega$ at 25 °C, and a temperature coefficient of $+0.5\%$ per °C. If the junction temperature rises to 100 °C, calculate the new $R_{DS(on)}$ and the additional conduction loss at a load current of 15 A.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Construction & Operating Principle [7 Marks]

**Construction (4 marks):**

The VDMOS Power MOSFET is built on an **n+ substrate** that serves as the drain. On top of this substrate, an **n– epitaxial (drift) layer** is grown; this lightly doped layer is responsible for **voltage blocking**. Two diffusions are performed into the n– layer:

- A **p-base diffusion** forms the body region.
- An **n+ source diffusion** is performed inside the p-base.

A thin layer of **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)** is grown over the p-base region between the two n+ source diffusions, and a **polysilicon gate** is deposited on top of this oxide. A **source metal layer** contacts both the n+ source and the p-base simultaneously, creating a **short between them**.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**

- **OFF State ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$):** No inversion layer exists. The p-base / n– drift junction is reverse-biased by the applied drain voltage, and the device blocks the full voltage. The MOSFET acts as an open switch.
- **ON State ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$):** A positive gate voltage induces an **n-type inversion layer** in the p-base region directly beneath the gate oxide. This channel connects the n+ source to the n– drift region, allowing electrons to flow from source → channel → n– drift → n+ substrate → drain terminal.

**Why is the source shorted to the p-base?**
The p-base / n+ source / n– drift structure forms a **parasitic n-p-n BJT**. Without the short, any voltage drop across the p-base resistance ($R_b$) during transient currents could forward-bias the base-emitter of this parasitic BJT, turning it ON and causing a **latch-up** that destroys the device. The source-to-body short keeps $V_{BE} = 0$, preventing latch-up.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical: $R_{DS(on)}$ vs Temperature [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $R_{DS(on),25} = 0.15 \,\Omega$ at 25 °C
- Temperature coefficient $= +0.5\% / °C$
- $\Delta T = 100 - 25 = 75 \, °C$
- $I_D = 15$ A

**Step 1 — Calculate the new $R_{DS(on)}$:**

$$\Delta R\,\% = 0.5\% \times 75 = 37.5\%$$

$$R_{DS(on),100} = R_{DS(on),25} \times \left(1 + \frac{\Delta R\,\%}{100}\right) = 0.15 \times 1.375$$

$$\boxed{R_{DS(on),100} = 0.20625 \,\Omega \approx 0.206 \,\Omega}$$

**Step 2 — Conduction loss at 25 °C (for reference):**

$$P_{cond,25} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on),25} = 15^2 \times 0.15 = 33.75 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Conduction loss at 100 °C:**

$$P_{cond,100} = I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on),100} = 15^2 \times 0.20625 = 46.41 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Additional conduction loss:**

$$\Delta P_{cond} = P_{cond,100} - P_{cond,25} = 46.41 - 33.75$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{cond} = 12.66 \, \text{W}}$$

**Conclusion:** The conduction loss increases by **37.5%** for a 75 °C rise. This is why thermal management is critical in MOSFET-based converters.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of static I-V characteristics, explain the **three operating regions** of a Power MOSFET. How are switching losses different from conduction losses?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A buck converter uses a Power MOSFET switching at 50 kHz. The drain-source voltage is 100 V, load current is 8 A, turn-on time = 40 ns, turn-off time = 60 ns, and $R_{DS(on)} = 0.2 \,\Omega$. If the duty cycle is 0.4, calculate:
  (i) Switching loss per cycle and total switching power loss.
  (ii) Conduction power loss.
  (iii) Total power dissipated in the MOSFET.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Three Operating Regions [7 Marks]

The static I-V characteristics of a Power MOSFET plot $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ for several values of $V_{GS}$. There are **three operating regions**:

1. **Cutoff Region ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$):** $I_D \approx 0$ regardless of $V_{DS}$. The device is fully OFF. The leakage current is in the µA range.

2. **Triode / Ohmic / Linear Region ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$):** The device behaves as a **voltage-controlled resistor**. The drain current is given by:
$$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS} - V_{th}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right]$$
This is the **ON state** in switching applications. The slope of the I-V curve in this region is $1/R_{DS(on)}$.

3. **Saturation / Active Region ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$):** The channel is pinched off at the drain end. $I_D$ saturates at:
$$I_{D,sat} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$
Power MOSFETs should **never operate continuously** in this region as a switch — it is a high-dissipation region.

**Switching vs. Conduction Loss (3 marks):**

- **Conduction Loss** is the power dissipated in the ON state due to $I_D^2 \cdot R_{DS(on)}$. It depends on the **duty cycle, RMS current, and on-resistance**.
- **Switching Loss** is the power dissipated during the **ON → OFF and OFF → ON transitions** when $V_{DS}$ and $I_D$ overlap. It scales **linearly with switching frequency**:
$$P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$$

Switching loss is **zero at DC** and dominates at high $f_{sw}$, while conduction loss is **independent of frequency** and dominates at low $f_{sw}$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical: Buck Converter Power Loss [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- $V_{DS} = 100$ V, $I_D = 8$ A
- $t_{on} = 40$ ns, $t_{off} = 60$ ns
- $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz
- $R_{DS(on)} = 0.2 \,\Omega$, $D = 0.4$

**Step 1 — Energy lost per switching cycle (turn-on + turn-off):**

$$E_{sw,cycle} = \frac{1}{2} V_{DS} I_D (t_{on} + t_{off})$$

$$E_{sw,cycle} = 0.5 \times 100 \times 8 \times (40 + 60) \times 10^{-9} = 0.5 \times 800 \times 100 \times 10^{-9}$$
