## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1:** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define latching current and holding current of an SCR. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

**Latching Current ($I_L$):** It is the minimum anode current that must flow through the SCR *immediately after* the gate pulse is applied, to ensure the device stays in the ON state even after the gate signal is removed. If the anode current is less than $I_L$ when the gate pulse ends, the SCR returns to the OFF state.

**Holding Current ($I_H$):** It is the minimum anode current required to *maintain* the SCR in the ON state once conduction has been established. If the anode current falls below $I_H$ (e.g., during natural commutation), the SCR turns OFF.

**Relationship:**
$$I_L > I_H$$
- $I_L$ is always **greater than** $I_H$
- Typical ratio: $I_L \approx 2 \times I_H$ to $3 \times I_H$
- $I_L$ is measured at the instant of turn-on; $I_H$ is measured during steady-state conduction

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic**: **L**atching is at the **L**eading edge of conduction (turn-on), **H**olding is **H**ere-after during conduction. L is bigger because at turn-on, more charge must be injected to establish the regenerative feedback.

---

#### **Question 2:** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any three differences between an SCR and a GTO.**

**Model Answer:**

| **#** | **SCR** | **GTO** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Semi-controlled** — gate can turn ON only | **Fully controlled** — gate can turn ON and OFF |
| 2 | Turn-off requires external commutation circuit (force anode current below $I_H$) | Turn-off via large negative gate pulse |
| 3 | Higher conduction drop (~1.5 V–2 V) | Higher switching loss (tail current) but lower ON drop |
| 4 | No negative gate current needed | Requires $I_{GQ} = I_A / \beta_{off}$, typically huge |
| 5 | Symmetric gate–cathode structure | Interdigitated gate–cathode fingers for efficient carrier extraction |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 contains **two 14-mark questions** with internal choice. The student answers **ONE out of TWO** per slot. We present both choices below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat construction diagram, explain the working of an SCR using the two-transistor analogy. Derive the condition for turn-on. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
The SCR is a four-layer (P-N-P-N) device with three junctions (J1, J2, J3) and three terminals: Anode (A), Cathode (K), and Gate (G). The anode is connected to the outer P+ layer, the cathode to the outer N+ layer, and the gate to the inner P layer.

**Two-Transistor Model (Diagram):**
- $Q_1$ (PNP): Anode is emitter, gate-region is base, cathode-region is collector
- $Q_2$ (NPN): Cathode is emitter, gate-region is base, anode-region is collector
- They are **cross-coupled** — collector of each feeds base of the other

**Working:**
- With Anode positive w.r.t. cathode and no gate signal, J1 and J3 are forward-biased, J2 is reverse-biased. The SCR blocks forward voltage.
- A small positive gate current $I_G$ flows into the base of $Q_2$, producing collector current $I_{C2} = \alpha_2 I_K$. This $I_{C2}$ flows into the base of $Q_1$, producing $I_{C1} = \alpha_1 I_A$.
- The collector current of $Q_1$ now adds to the gate current, increasing the base drive of $Q_2$. This **regenerative feedback** continues until saturation.

**Derivation of Latching Condition:**

Using KCL at the anode node (as derived in Section 3.1):
$$I_A = \frac{\alpha_2 I_G + I_{CBO}}{1 - (\alpha_1 + \alpha_2)}$$

For the device to enter a regenerative (latching) state, the denominator must approach zero:
$$\boxed{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1}$$

This is the **latching condition**. Once it is satisfied, the SCR latches ON and the gate loses control.

---

**Q: (b)** Explain the different protection circuits required for an SCR in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An SCR, once turned ON, conducts very high currents. The four essential protection circuits are:

**1. Overcurrent Protection (Fuses / Circuit Breakers):**
- Semiconductor fuses with $I^2t$ rating **lower than the SCR's $I^2t$** rating
- Fast-acting; interrupt fault current before junction temperature rises
- Backup: inverse-time circuit breakers

**2. Overvoltage Protection (Snubbers / MOVs):**
- **RCD Snubber** across the SCR: absorbs switching transients
- **MOV (Metal Oxide Varistor)**: clamps voltage spikes (e.g., from inductive loads)
- **Selenium diodes**: alternative clamping device

**3. $dv/dt$ Protection (RC Snubber across device):**
- A high $dv/dt$ on the anode causes charging current $i = C_j (dv/dt)$ in the middle junction, possibly exceeding $I_{GT}$ and falsely triggering the device
- A **parallel RC snubber** slows the voltage rise: $dv/dt = V_s / (R_s C_s)$
- Typical design: $C_s$ = 0.1–1 μF, $R_s$ = 10–100 Ω

**4. $di/dt$ Protection (Series Inductor):**
- At turn-on, current rises from a small area near the gate — high localized current density can puncture the device
- A **series inductor $L_s$** in the anode line limits $di/dt$ to safe values
- $V_L = L_s \cdot (di/dt)$ — typically $L_s$ = 5–50 μH

**Summary Table (Write in Exam):**

| **Protection Type** | **Component** | **Position** | **Parameter Controlled** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Overcurrent | Fuse | Series | $I_{peak}$, $I^2t$ |
| Overvoltage | MOV / Snubber | Parallel | $V_{peak}$ |
| $dv/dt$ | R-C Snubber | Parallel | $dv/dt$ |
| $di/dt$ | Inductor $L_s$ | Series | $di/dt$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Many students write "snubber across SCR" and stop there. **You must distinguish**: RCD snubber is for **voltage transient absorption** (overvoltage), while the **R-C snubber across the SCR** is specifically for **$dv/dt$ protection** — they are different circuits with different component values!

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a GTO. Compare it with an SCR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
A GTO is a P-N-P-N, three-junction device similar to an SCR, but with a **heavily interdigitated** gate-cathode structure. The gate and cathode are formed as interleaved fingers to maximize the perimeter available for carrier extraction during turn-off.

**Working:**
- **Turn-on:** Apply a small positive gate pulse ($I_{GT} \approx 20$ mA, $V_{GT} \approx 1$ V). The device latches on via the same regenerative PNP-NPN mechanism as an SCR.
- **Turn-off:** Apply a large **negative** gate-cathode voltage ($V_{GQ} \approx -10$ to -20 V) drawing a large reverse gate current $I_{GQ}$.
  - This reverse gate current sweeps stored minority carriers out of the P-base and N+ emitter regions near the gate.
  - The NPN transistor $Q_2$ portion is starved of base current, breaking the regenerative feedback.
  - Once $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 < 1$, the device turns OFF.
- Required reverse gate current: $\vert I_{GQ}\vert = I_{AQ} / \beta_{off}$ (typically $\beta_{off} = 3$ to 5)

**Comparison with SCR:**

| **Parameter** | **SCR** | **GTO** |
|---|---|---|
| Turn-off control | Not possible via gate | Possible via negative gate pulse |
| $I_{GT}$ (turn-on) | 1–100 mA | 20–200 mA |
| $I_{GQ}$ (turn-off) | N/A | 0.2–0.33 of $I_A$ |
| Snubber | Yes (RCD) | Yes (RCD + diode) |
| Switching frequency | Low (< 400 Hz line freq) | Medium (1–2 kHz) |
| Application | Phase-controlled rectifiers | Choppers, inverters (older) |

---

**Q: (b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a MOSFET driver using a **totem-pole** output stage. Derive the equation for driver power dissipation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why a driver is needed:**
- A power MOSFET has a large input capacitance $C_{iss}$ (e.g., 1–5 nF)
- A logic output (e.g., 5 V, 20 mA from Arduino) cannot charge this capacitance quickly
- Switching time would be slow → high switching losses → device damage

**Totem-Pole Driver (Push-Pull):**
- Two transistors: one NPN/PMOS (top) to source current, one NPN/NMOS (bottom) to sink current
- They operate in **complementary** mode — one is ON while the other is OFF

**Circuit Diagram Description:**
- Logic input goes to both gates/bases
- Top transistor (PMOS or NPN) sources $I_{G(on)}$ into the MOSFET gate
- Bottom transistor (NMOS or NPN) sinks $I_{G(off)}$ from the MOSFET gate
- The totem-pole output is connected directly to the MOSFET gate

**Working:**
- Logic HIGH → top transistor ON → charges $C_{iss}$ rapidly → MOSFET turns ON
- Logic LOW → bottom transistor ON → discharges $C_{iss}$ rapidly → MOSFET turns OFF
- The driver acts as a **current amplifier** with peak current:
$$I_{G(peak)} = \frac{V_{DRV} - V_{GS(plateau)}}{R_G}$$

**Derivation of Driver Power:**
- Energy to charge the gate once: $E = \frac{1}{2} C_{iss} V_{GS}^2 = Q_G \cdot V_{GS}$ (work done by driver)
- Switching happens at frequency $f_{sw}$
- Average power dissipated by the driver:
$$P_{drv} = Q_G \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$$

**Numerical example (Write in Exam):**
- $Q_G = 71$ nC, $V_{GS} = 10$ V, $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz
- $P_{drv} = 71 \times 10^{-9} \times 10 \times 50 \times 10^3 = 35.5$ mW

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall**: Don't confuse **driver dissipation** with **MOSFET switching loss**. Driver dissipation = $Q_G V_{GS} f_{sw}$ (in driver IC). MOSFET switching loss = $0.5 V_{DS} I_D (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$ (in power MOSFET). They are different powers in different devices!

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** — Memorize this for the KTU exam.

**🔹 SCR Essentials**
- [ ] Four-layer P-N-P-N, three-junction device
- [ ] Semi-controlled: gate can turn ON only
- [ ] Latching condition: $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 \geq 1$ (two-transistor analogy)
- [ ] $I_L > I_H$ always (latching > holding current)
- [ ] Conduction drop: 0.8 V – 2 V
- [ ] Symbol has a single gate arrow (anode-side)

**🔹 GTO Essentials**
- [ ] Fully-controlled thyristor (turn-off via gate)
- [ ] Interdigitated gate-cathode structure
- [ ] $\beta_{off} = I_{AQ} / I_{GQ} = 3$ to 5
- [ ] Requires large negative gate current to turn off
- [ ] Has a long **tail current** during turn-off → higher switching loss
- [ ] Symbol has a bar/strike on the gate (indicates turn-off capability)

**🔹 Protection Circuits (Know ALL 4)**
- [ ] **Overcurrent** → Fuse ($I^2t$ rating) + Circuit Breaker
- [ ] **Overvoltage** → MOV / Selenium diode / Zener clamp
- [ ] **$dv/dt$** → Parallel R-C snubber across device
- [ ] **$di/dt$** → Series inductor $L_s$ in anode line
- [ ] Snubber capacitor: $C_s = I_L t_{off} / V_{pk}$
- [ ] Snubber resistor: $R_s \approx V_{pk} / (0.5 I_L)$

**🔹 BJT Driver**
- [ ] Need continuous base current: $I_B = I_C / h_{FE(sat)}$
- [ ] Forced $\beta = 5$–10 in saturation design
- [ ] Storage time $t_s$ is the bottleneck
- [ ] **Baker Clamp** = anti-saturation technique using two diodes
- [ ] Active (forced) base drive speeds up turn-off

**🔹 MOSFET Driver**
- [ ] Gate is capacitive: $Q_G = C_{iss} \Delta V_{GS}$
- [ ] Need **totem-pole (push-pull)** output stage
- [ ] Peak gate current: $I_{G(peak)} = V_{DRV} / R_G$
- [ ] Driver power: $P_{drv} = Q_G \cdot V_{GS} \cdot f_{sw}$
- [ ] **Bootstrap circuit** for high-side N-MOSFET (half-bridge)
- [ ] Common driver ICs: IR2110, TC4420, TLP250

**🔹 Standard Numerical Values (Quick Recall)**
- [ ] $V_{GS(th)}$ typical = 2–4 V; logic-level = 1–2 V
- [ ] $V_{GS(ON)}$ for power MOSFET = 10–15 V
- [ ] $dv/dt$ limit: 50–200 V/μs
- [ ] $di/dt$ limit: 50–100 A/μs
- [ ] $I_H$, $I_L$ typical: 1–100 mA

**🔹 KTU 2024 Exam Day Quick-Fire**
- [ ] Always draw the **circuit symbol** before the explanation
- [ ] For 14-mark questions, structure answer as: (i) Diagram (ii) Construction (iii) Working (iv) Derivation (v) Comparison/Application
- [ ] Always include **units** in numerical answers
- [ ] For 3-mark questions, use **bullet points + 1-line explanations** — avoid long paragraphs

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner Warning**: The **most lost marks** in this module come from (1) forgetting the **units** in derivations, (2) confusing SCR with TRIAC (SCR is DC; TRIAC is bidirectional), and (3) writing "snubber" without specifying **which parameter** it protects. Be explicit: "R-C snubber across SCR for $dv/dt$ protection" earns full marks; just "snubber" loses 2–3 marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Snubber Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Snubber Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Snubber Circuit:** A snubber is an auxiliary electrical network connected across a power semiconductor switch (SCR, BJT, MOSFET, IGBT, GTO) to **suppress transient overvoltages**, **limit the rate of change of voltage (dv/dt)** during turn-off, **limit the rate of change of current (di/dt)** during turn-on, **reshape the switching trajectory**, and **dissipate the switching energy** that would otherwise be lost inside the semiconductor device.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, snubbers are classified under **"Protection Circuits for Power Semiconductor Devices"** within Module 1 (Power Semiconductor Devices and Their Characteristics). They are essential auxiliary components in all hard-switched converters, choppers, and inverters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shock Absorber of Power Electronics"

Imagine driving a car over speed breakers. Without shock absorbers, every bump delivers a violent jerk to the chassis and the passengers. The mechanical energy from the impact has nowhere safe to go, so it hammers the suspension, the tyres, and ultimately the axle.

A **snubber circuit plays the identical role for a semiconductor switch**:

- The **car body** = the power semiconductor device
- The **speed breaker** = the abrupt switching transition (turn-on/turn-off)
- The **shock absorber** = the snubber (R, RC, or RCD network)
- The **damper fluid heating up** = energy safely dissipated as heat in the snubber resistor

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a poorly designed suspension causes fatigue failure, a missing or wrongly sized snubber causes **second breakdown**, **dv/dt false triggering**, **device explosion**, and **EMI emissions** in power converters.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Switching Frequency ($f_s$):** Typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz** in modern converters
- **DC Link Voltage ($V_d$):** **100 V to 1200 V** in industrial drives
- **Snubber Time Constant ($\tau$):** Should be **3 to 5 times** the switch turn-off time
- **Snubber Resistor Power Rating:** Sized for **2× to 3×** the calculated average dissipation (derating)
- **Switching Energy Loss ($E_{sw}$):** **0.1 mJ to 10 mJ** per transition for IGBT modules

## 1.4 Why Are Snubbers Needed? — The Five Enemy Transients

1. **Overvoltage Spikes ($V_{spike}$)** — caused by stray inductance ($L_s$) releasing stored energy: $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L_s I^2$
2. **dv/dt Triggering** — fast rising voltage across a thyristor causes false turn-on
3. **di/dt Destruction** — fast rising current exceeds the device's rated di/dt
4. **Ringing / Oscillation** — $L_s$ and junction capacitance ($C_j$) form an LC tank
5. **Switching Loss** — overlap of voltage and current during transition wastes energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 1 specifically tests the student's ability to identify *which* snubber (turn-off vs turn-on vs RCD) is required for a given switching waveform. Memorising the waveform shape is non-negotiable for scoring above 80%.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Snubber Circuits

Snubbers are classified based on **which side of the switch** they protect and **which transient** they suppress.

### A. Voltage (Turn-Off) Snubber
Connected **in parallel** with the semiconductor switch. Its job is to slow down the rise of voltage across the device when it turns OFF.

- **RC Snubber** (Resistor + Capacitor) — simplest form
- **RCD Snubber** (Resistor + Capacitor + Diode) — energy-recovery version

### B. Current (Turn-On) Snubber
Connected **in series** with the semiconductor switch. Its job is to slow down the rise of current through the device when it turns ON.

- **Series Inductor ($L_s$)** — basic form
- **Saturable Reactor Snubber** — saturates once current is established

### C. Combined Snubber Networks
A complete high-performance snubber consists of **both** a turn-off snubber and a turn-on snubber, often with a freewheeling diode path.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The RC Turn-Off Snubber

1. **Before turn-off (switch ON):** Capacitor $C_s$ is discharged (or held at near-zero voltage because of the conducting switch shorting it).
2. **At the instant of turn-off:** The load current $I_L$ that was flowing through the switch is **diverted** into the snubber capacitor $C_s$ because the switch is no longer a short circuit.
3. **During the voltage rise:** The capacitor charges linearly (approximately) from $V_{CE(sat)}$ up to $V_d$. The **dv/dt** across the device is now determined by:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{I_L}{C_s}$$

4. **After the switch is fully OFF:** $C_s$ is charged to the full DC bus voltage $V_d$.
5. **At the next turn-on:** The capacitor must be discharged through the snubber resistor $R_s$. The resistor limits the discharge current spike to a safe value.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why':** By forcing the current to flow into the capacitor, we ensure the switch voltage rises **after** the current has fallen. This eliminates the dangerous **simultaneous high V and high I** overlap region, slashing switching losses.

## 2.3 Operational Logic — The RCD Turn-Off Snubber

The plain RC snubber has a drawback: when the switch turns ON, the charged capacitor dumps its stored energy **back into the switch** through $R_s$, wasting it as heat.

The **RCD snubber** fixes this using a **polarity diode**:

1. **During turn-off:** Current flows through the **diode $D_s$** (which is forward biased) into the capacitor $C_s$. The resistor $R_s$ is bypassed during charging, so $C_s$ charges quickly to the full bus voltage.
2. **During turn-on:** The diode $D_s$ is reverse-biased, so the capacitor **cannot discharge back through the switch**. Instead, it discharges slowly through $R_s$ into the DC bus or a separate sink — losing energy **outside the switch**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This makes the RCD snubber essential for **high-frequency** converters (>$20$ kHz) where RC snubber losses would dominate the thermal budget.

## 2.4 Operational Logic — The Series Inductor (Turn-On) Snubber

1. **Before turn-on:** Current $I_L$ is freewheeling through the freewheeling diode.
2. **At turn-on:** The series inductor $L_s$ opposes any sudden change in current. The current through the switch rises linearly:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_d}{L_s}$$

3. **The voltage across the switch** drops quickly (limited by the turn-off snubber), while the current rises slowly. The overlap area (switching loss) is dramatically reduced.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Snubber Capacitance (RC) | $C_s = \dfrac{I_L \cdot t_{fi}}{2 \cdot V_d}$ | Farads | $t_{fi}$ = current fall time |
| 2 | dv/dt at turn-off | $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_s}$ | V/µs | Use $C_s$ in µF, $I_L$ in A |
| 3 | Snubber Resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{T_s}{3 \, C_s}$ | Ohms | $T_s$ = switching period |
| 4 | Peak Discharge Current | $I_{peak} = \dfrac{V_d}{R_s}$ | Amps | Occurs at turn-on |
| 5 | Energy Stored per Cycle | $E_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2$ | Joules | Stored in capacitor |
| 6 | Snubber Power Loss | $P_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s$ | Watts | $f_s$ = switching frequency |
| 7 | Series Snubber Inductance | $L_s = \dfrac{V_d \cdot t_{rv}}{I_L}$ | Henries | $t_{rv}$ = voltage rise time |
| 8 | di/dt at turn-on | $\dfrac{di}{dt} = \dfrac{V_d}{L_s}$ | A/µs | Limited rise of switch current |
| 9 | Spike Voltage (Stray L) | $V_{spike} = L_s \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | Volts | On stray loop inductance |
| 10 | Time Constant | $\tau = R_s C_s$ | Seconds | Should be $\approx 3 t_{off}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formula rows, **boundary conditions** are the physical limits a designer must respect. For example, $C_s$ cannot be made arbitrarily large or the discharge losses at turn-on will overheat the resistor.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IGBT Inverters in VFDs** (Variable Frequency Drives) — RCD snubbers mandatory above 5 kW.
- **IGBT Modules in Traction** (electric locomotives) — laminated bus bars and discrete RC snubbers.
- **TRIAC / SCR AC Controllers** — dv/dt snubbers prevent false triggering by supply transients.
- **High-Frequency SMPS** — combined $L_s + RCD$ reduce EMI and pass CE/FCC radiated tests.
- **GTO / IGCT Converters** in HVDC — sophisticated snubbers and clamp circuits are research areas.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Snubber Capacitance $C_s$ for RC Snubber

**Problem Setup:** A semiconductor switch carries a steady load current $I_L$. At turn-off, the current falls to zero in a time $t_{fi}$ (current fall time). We want the voltage across the switch to **remain near zero** during this current-fall interval, so the switching loss is minimised.

**Step 1 — Charge Balance Principle**

The total charge that was flowing through the switch must now flow through the snubber capacitor:

$$Q_{switch} = Q_{capacitor}$$

The charge that leaves the switch during turn-off is:

$$Q_{switch} = \int_0^{t_{fi}} i(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} I_L \cdot t_{fi}$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises because the current falls approximately linearly from $I_L$ to $0$ over the interval $t_{fi}$.

**Step 2 — Charge Accepted by Capacitor**

Assuming the capacitor voltage rises from $0$ to $V_d$ linearly during the same interval:

$$Q_{capacitor} = \int_0^{t_{fi}} C_s \frac{dv}{dt} \, dt = \frac{1}{2} C_s \cdot V_d$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve**

$$\frac{1}{2} I_L \cdot t_{fi} = \frac{1}{2} C_s \cdot V_d$$

$$C_s = \frac{I_L \cdot t_{fi}}{V_d}$$

**Step 4 — Conservative Design Correction**

In real circuits, stray inductance slows the voltage rise. A factor of 2 safety margin is conventionally added:

$$\boxed{\,C_s = \frac{I_L \cdot t_{fi}}{2 \cdot V_d}\,}$$

> This is the **canonical KTU formula** for the RC snubber capacitor. Every ESE question on snubber design starts here.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Snubber Resistor $R_s$ Selection

**Problem Setup:** The snubber capacitor $C_s$ is charged to $V_d$ when the switch turns OFF. At the next turn-on, we must limit the discharge current to a safe peak value (typically less than the device's rated surge current).

**Step 1 — Discharge Equation**

The capacitor discharges through $R_s$ (and the switch) following a first-order exponential:

$$v_c(t) = V_d \, e^{-t / (R_s C_s)}$$

**Step 2 — Peak Discharge Current**

The peak occurs at $t = 0^+$ (worst case, assuming $C_s$ is fully charged and switch closes instantaneously):

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_d}{R_s}$$

**Step 3 — Discharge Constraint**

We require the capacitor to be **almost fully discharged** before the next turn-off event. Define a discharge time $t_d$ as a fraction of the switching period $T_s$:

$$t_d \approx 3 R_s C_s$$

To allow 99.7% discharge within one switching period:

$$3 R_s C_s \leq T_s$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $R_s$**

$$R_s \leq \frac{T_s}{3 C_s}$$

Designers typically choose:

$$\boxed{\,R_s = \frac{T_s}{3 \, C_s}\,}$$

The smaller $R_s$, the faster the discharge — but the larger the peak discharge current. A compromise is to select $R_s$ such that $I_{peak} \leq 0.2 I_L$ (20% of load current) to avoid disturbing the converter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Snubber Power Loss $P_s$

**Step 1 — Energy Stored at Turn-Off**

The capacitor charges to $V_d$, storing:

$$E_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2$$

**Step 2 — Energy Dissipated per Switching Cycle**

In the RC snubber, **all** this energy is dumped through $R_s$ at the next turn-on (it has nowhere else to go). Hence:

$$E_{cycle} = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2$$

**Step 3 — Average Power Loss**

If the switch operates at frequency $f_s$, the number of switching events per second is $f_s$:

$$P_s = E_{cycle} \cdot f_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s$$

$$\boxed{\,P_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s\,}$$

> This is a **vital KTU result** because it sets the physical size of $R_s$. A 1 µF capacitor at 400 V at 20 kHz loses:
> $P_s = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 400^2 \times 20000 = 1600$ W — a massive heat burden!
> This is why RC snubbers are abandoned above a few kHz in favour of RCD.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Series Inductance $L_s$ for Turn-On Snubber

**Step 1 — Voltage Across Inductor at Turn-On**

When the switch closes, the full bus voltage $V_d$ appears across the series inductor (assuming the freewheeling diode has just turned off):

$$v_{L_s} = L_s \frac{di}{dt} = V_d$$

**Step 2 — Solve for di/dt**

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_d}{L_s}$$

**Step 3 — Limit di/dt to Safe Value**

The switch datasheet specifies a maximum di/dt. Let this be $(di/dt)_{max}$:

$$L_s \geq \frac{V_d}{(di/dt)_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Alternative Form Using Voltage Rise Time**

The voltage across the switch rises from $V_{CE(sat)}$ to $V_d$ in a time $t_{rv}$ (voltage rise time). During this time, the inductor current rises from $0$ to $I_L$:

$$L_s = \frac{V_d \cdot t_{rv}}{I_L}$$

$$\boxed{\,L_s = \frac{V_d \cdot t_{rv}}{I_L}\,}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Snubber Variants

| Parameter | RC Snubber | RCD Snubber | Series Inductor Snubber |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Placement** | Parallel to switch | Parallel to switch | Series with switch |
| **Suppresses** | dv/dt at turn-off | dv/dt at turn-off | di/dt at turn-on |
| **Capacitor Discharge Path** | Through $R_s$ into switch (lossy) | Through $R_s$ into bus (efficient) | N/A |
| **Power Loss $P_s$** | $\tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s$ | $\approx 0$ ideally | Conduction loss in $L_s$ |
| **Suitable Frequency** | $< 5$ kHz | $> 20$ kHz | All frequencies |
| **Component Count** | 2 | 3 | 1 |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Low–Medium |
| **Key Trade-off** | Heat in $R_s$ | Diode forward recovery | Bulky inductor at high $I$ |

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Question:** An IGBT in a chopper handles a load current of $50$ A. The DC bus voltage is $400$ V. The switching frequency is $5$ kHz. The current fall time is $0.4$ µs. Design an RC snubber.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_s$**

$$C_s = \frac{I_L \cdot t_{fi}}{2 V_d} = \frac{50 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 400}$$

$$C_s = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{800} = 0.025 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 0.025 \, \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $R_s$**

$$R_s = \frac{T_s}{3 C_s} = \frac{1 / 5000}{3 \times 0.025 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_s = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.075 \times 10^{-6}} = 2666.67 \, \Omega \approx 2.7 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute Power Loss**

$$P_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s = 0.5 \times 0.025 \times 10^{-6} \times 400^2 \times 5000$$

$$P_s = 0.5 \times 0.025 \times 10^{-6} \times 160000 \times 5000 = 10 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Choose Resistor Rating**

Derate by 2× for safety: $P_{rated} = 20$ W. Use a **25 W, 2.7 kΩ** non-inductive resistor.

**Final Design Summary:**

| Component | Value | Rating |
|-----------|-------|--------|
| $C_s$ | 0.025 µF | 630 V (DC rated) |
| $R_s$ | 2.7 kΩ | 25 W, non-inductive |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔌 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RC Snubber — Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Bus Positive Vd] --> B[Switch S IGBT or SCR]
    B --> C[Load Inductor L Load]
    C --> D[DC Bus Negative 0V]
    E[Snubber Capacitor Cs] --> F[Parallel Combination with Switch]
    G[Snubber Resistor Rs] --> F
    F --> B
    H[Freewheeling Diode Dfw] --> C
    A --> H
    stepA[At Turn OFF] --> stepB[Load current diverted into Cs]
    stepB --> stepC[Cs charges from 0 to Vd]
    stepC --> stepD[dv dt limited by Cs]
    stepE[At Turn ON] --> stepF[Cs discharges through Rs into switch]
    stepF --> stepG[Energy dissipated as heat in Rs]
```

## 4.2 RCD Snubber — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Bus Vd] --> B[Main Switch S]
    B --> C[Load]
    C --> D[DC Return 0V]
    E[Snubber Capacitor Cs] --> G[Snubber Diode Ds]
    G --> E
    E --> B
    F[Snubber Resistor Rs] --> E
    F --> A
    H[Freewheeling Diode] --> C
    stepA[Turn OFF phase] --> stepB[Current flows via Ds into Cs]
    stepB --> stepC[Rs bypassed during charging fast voltage rise limited]
    stepD[Turn ON phase] --> stepE[Ds reverse biased Cs cannot dump into switch]
    stepE --> stepF[Cs discharges slowly via Rs to DC bus or dump resistor]
```

## 4.3 Turn-On Snubber (Series Inductor) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Bus Vd] --> Ls[Series Inductor Ls]
    Ls --> B[Main Switch S]
    B --> C[Load]
    C --> D[DC Return 0V]
    F[Freewheeling Diode Dfw] --> C
    A --> F
    stepA[At Turn ON] --> stepB[Full Vd appears across Ls]
    stepB --> stepC[Current rises linearly di dt equals Vd by Ls]
    stepC --> stepD[Voltage across switch drops to VCE sat slowly]
    stepD --> stepE[Switching loss overlap area reduced drastically]
    stepF[Steady state] --> stepG[Ls acts as a wire only transient protection]
```

## 4.4 Complete Snubber Protection Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Converter Power Stage] --> B[Turn ON Snubber Ls]
    B --> C[Main Switch S]
    C --> D[Turn OFF Snubber RC or RCD]
    D --> E[DC Bus with Stray Ls]
    A --> F[Freewheeling Diode]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Load]
    H[Gate Driver] --> C
    I[Control Circuit] --> H
    stepA[Turn ON stress] --> stepB[Limp di dt using Ls]
    stepC[Turn OFF stress] --> stepD[Limp dv dt using RC]
    stepE[Overvoltage spike] --> stepF[Absorbed by Cs charging]
    stepG[Energy dissipation] --> stepH[Heat in Rs within safe limits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram style?** Physical schematics with exact component symbols are best drawn on paper in the exam hall, but the Mermaid block above maps the **functional interactions** and energy flow paths — exactly what KTU examiners want to see in design questions worth 7–14 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a snubber circuit. List any four functions it performs in a power electronic converter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A snubber circuit is an auxiliary network of passive components (R, L, C, D) connected across or in series with a power semiconductor switch to protect it from switching transients.

Four functions:
1. Limits the **dv/dt** across the switch during turn-off.
2. Limits the **di/dt** through the switch during turn-on.
3. Reduces **switching power loss** by reshaping the V-I trajectory.
4. Suppresses **overvoltage spikes** caused by stray inductance.
5. Protects against **dv/dt false triggering** in thyristors.
6. Suppresses **EMI / ringing** in the DC bus.

*(Any four well-explained points = 3 marks. Naming the circuit without functions = 1 mark only.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a turn-on snubber and a turn-off snubber. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Turn-On Snubber | Turn-Off Snubber |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Purpose | Limit di/dt at turn-on | Limit dv/dt at turn-off |
| Placement | Series with switch | Parallel with switch |
| Key Component | Inductor $L_s$ | Capacitor $C_s$ + Resistor $R_s$ |
| Example | Series saturable reactor | RC or RCD snubber |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE of the two questions. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each.**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram and associated waveforms, explain the operation of an **RC turn-off snubber** for an IGBT. Derive the expression for the snubber capacitance and resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IGBT chopper operates on a 500 V DC bus, switches a load current of 80 A, at a switching frequency of 4 kHz. The current fall time of the IGBT is 0.5 µs. **Design** the RC snubber (find $C_s$, $R_s$, and the snubber power loss). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram Description**

The RC snubber consists of a capacitor $C_s$ in series with a resistor $R_s$, and this series combination is connected **in parallel** with the IGBT switch. The freewheeling diode $D_f$ is connected across the load to provide a path for inductive load current when the switch opens.

**Step 2 — Waveform Explanation (chronological)**

1. **Switch ON interval:** Current $i_T$ flows through the IGBT, voltage $V_{CE}$ is low (saturation), capacitor $C_s$ is discharged.
2. **Turn-off command:** $i_T$ begins to fall; the load current diverts into $C_s$.
3. **Voltage rise phase:** $V_{CE}$ rises from $V_{CE(sat)}$ to $V_d$ with controlled slope $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_s}$.
4. **After turn-off:** $C_s$ holds voltage $V_d$ until next turn-on.
5. **Turn-on:** $C_s$ discharges through $R_s$ and the IGBT; discharge current spike is limited by $R_s$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of $C_s$** *(as derived in Section 3.1)*

$$C_s = \frac{I_L \cdot t_{fi}}{2 V_d}$$

**Step 4 — Derivation of $R_s$** *(as derived in Section 3.2)*

$$R_s = \frac{T_s}{3 C_s} = \frac{1}{3 C_s f_s}$$

**Step 5 — Key Role of $R_s$**

It limits the discharge current at the next turn-on to:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{V_d}{R_s}$$

**Step 6 — Switching Loss Comparison**

Without snubber: $E_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_d I_L t_{fi}$ — dissipated inside the IGBT.
With snubber: most of the overlap energy is redirected to $C_s$ and then to $R_s$, **outside the device**.

**Marks Distribution for Q-A(a):**
- Diagram with labelling — 2 marks
- Waveform description — 1 mark
- Derivation of $C_s$ — 2 marks
- Derivation of $R_s$ — 2 marks

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:** $V_d = 500$ V, $I_L = 80$ A, $f_s = 4$ kHz, $t_{fi} = 0.5$ µs.

**Step 1 — Snubber Capacitance**

$$C_s = \frac{I_L \cdot t_{fi}}{2 V_d} = \frac{80 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 500}$$

$$C_s = \frac{40 \times 10^{-6}}{1000} = 0.04 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 0.04 \, \mu\text{F}$$

**Step 2 — Snubber Resistance**

Switching period: $T_s = 1 / f_s = 1 / 4000 = 250$ µs.

$$R_s = \frac{T_s}{3 C_s} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 0.04 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{250}{0.12} \approx 2083 \, \Omega$$

Choose standard value: **$R_s = 2.2$ kΩ**.

**Step 3 — Snubber Power Loss**

$$P_s = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s = 0.5 \times 0.04 \times 10^{-6} \times (500)^2 \times 4000$$

$$P_s = 0.5 \times 0.04 \times 10^{-6} \times 250000 \times 4000 = 20 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Resistor Rating**

Derate by 2×: $P_{rated} = 40$ W. Use a **50 W, 2.2 kΩ** non-inductive resistor.

**Final Design Table:**

| Component | Value | Rating | Remarks |
|-----------|-------|--------|---------|
| $C_s$ | 0.04 µF | 1000 V polypropylene | High pulse current type |
| $R_s$ | 2.2 kΩ | 50 W non-inductive | Wirewound or metal oxide |

**Marks Distribution for Q-A(b):**
- Correct formula application — 2 marks
- $C_s$ numerical — 1.5 marks
- $R_s$ numerical — 1.5 marks
- $P_s$ and final rating — 2 marks

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **RCD turn-off snubber** with a circuit diagram. Show how it differs from the RC snubber in terms of energy dissipation. Why is the RCD preferred at high switching frequencies? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **turn-on snubber**? Derive the expression for the series snubber inductance $L_s$ required to limit the di/dt to a specified value. A chopper operates on a 600 V DC bus, switches a load current of 100 A, and the switch voltage rise time is 1 µs. Determine the value of $L_s$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Step 1 — RCD Circuit Description**

The RCD snubber places a **diode $D_s$ in series with the capacitor $C_s$**, with a resistor $R_s$ in **parallel** with $C_s$ (not in series as in the RC snubber). The $R_s C_s$ parallel combination is connected across the IGBT.

**Step 2 — Operation During Turn-Off**

1. The IGBT turns off. Load current $I_L$ flows through the forward-biased diode $D_s$ into $C_s$.
2. The resistor $R_s$ is bypassed by the diode during this interval, so $C_s$ charges rapidly to $V_d$.
3. The voltage across the IGBT rises with slope $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{I_L}{C_s}$, much like the RC snubber.

**Step 3 — Operation During Turn-On (the critical difference)**

1. The IGBT turns on, but the diode $D_s$ is now **reverse-biased** — it blocks.
2. Therefore, $C_s$ **cannot discharge back into the IGBT** through $D_s$.
3. Instead, $C_s$ discharges slowly through $R_s$ into the DC bus (or to a separate energy-recovery circuit).
4. The energy is dissipated **outside the switch** — the IGBT sees virtually no extra stress from the snubber.

**Step 4 — Energy Comparison**

| Snubber | Energy at Turn-On |
|---------|-------------------|
| RC | $\tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2$ dumped into the IGBT (loss) |
| RCD | $\approx 0$ — energy routed to $R_s$ and bus |

**Step 5 — Why RCD at High Frequency**

As $f_s$ increases, the RC snubber loss $P_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s$ grows linearly. Above ~20 kHz, this loss exceeds the converter's main switching loss. The RCD eliminates this drain, making high-frequency operation feasible.

**Marks Distribution for Q-B(a):**
- Diagram of RCD — 2 marks
- Turn-off operation — 1.5 marks
- Turn-on operation with diode role — 2 marks
- Energy comparison & justification — 1.5 marks

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Turn-On Snubber**

A turn-on snubber is a small inductor $L_s$ connected **in series** with the semiconductor switch. It opposes sudden changes in current, thereby limiting the rate of rise of current (di/dt) when the switch is turned on.

**Step 2 — Derivation of $L_s$**

At the instant of turn-on, the full DC bus voltage $V_d$ appears across $L_s$ (since the freewheeling diode has just turned off):

$$v_{L_s} = L_s \frac{di}{dt} = V_d$$

The current must rise from $0$ to $I_L$ during the voltage rise time $t_{rv}$:

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{I_L}{t_{rv}}$$

Equating:

$$L_s = \frac{V_d \cdot t_{rv}}{I_L}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Evaluation**

Given: $V_d = 600$ V, $I_L = 100$ A, $t_{rv} = 1$ µs $= 1 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

$$L_s = \frac{600 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{100} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{H} = 6 \, \mu\text{H}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of di/dt**

$$\frac{di}{dt} = \frac{V_d}{L_s} = \frac{600}{6 \times 10^{-6}} = 100 \times 10^{6} \, \text{A/s} = 100 \, \text{A/µs}$$

This is well within the safe di/dt of any industrial IGBT.

**Marks Distribution for Q-B(b):**
- Definition of turn-on snubber — 1 mark
- Derivation of $L_s$ — 3 marks
- Numerical substitution and final value — 2 marks
- Verification — 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls that Cost Marks in Snubber Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $C_s$** — Many students write $C_s = I_L t_{fi} / V_d$ and lose 1 mark. Always use the conservative form $C_s = I_L t_{fi} / (2 V_d)$.
> 2. **Mixing up turn-on and turn-off snubbers** — RC/RCD are turn-OFF (parallel), inductor is turn-ON (series). Writing the wrong placement forfeits 2 marks.
> 3. **No unit check in numericals** — $t_{fi}$ must be in **seconds** in the formula. Converting µs to seconds is mandatory.
> 4. **Skipping the power loss calculation** — KTU specifically tests whether you remember $P_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s$. Omitting it loses 1.5 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning derating** — Practical designers use $2 \times$ to $3 \times$ the calculated power. Examiners love this industrial touch.
> 6. **Confusing RC and RCD operation at turn-on** — In RC, the capacitor discharges **into the switch**; in RCD, it does **not**. Reversing this loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Not drawing waveforms** — A question worth 7 marks without a waveform sketch typically caps your score at 5.
> 8. **Wrong type of resistor** — Snubber resistors must be **non-inductive**. Wirewound inductors fail at high di/dt. Always specify "non-inductive."

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Snubber Circuits (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Snubber = protection network** (R, L, C, D) for power semiconductor switches.
- ✅ **Turn-off snubber (RC/RCD)** → **parallel** to switch → limits **dv/dt**.
- ✅ **Turn-on snubber ($L_s$)** → **series** with switch → limits **di/dt**.
- ✅ **Canonical $C_s$ formula:** $C_s = I_L t_{fi} / (2 V_d)$.
- ✅ **Canonical $R_s$ formula:** $R_s = T_s / (3 C_s) = 1 / (3 C_s f_s)$.
- ✅ **Snubber power loss:** $P_s = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_d^2 f_s$ — **grows linearly with $f_s$**.
- ✅ **Series inductor:** $L_s = V_d t_{rv} / I_L$.
- ✅ **RC snubber** is simple but wastes energy as heat — use below 5 kHz.
- ✅ **RCD snubber** routes energy away from switch via blocking diode — use above 20 kHz.
- ✅ **Diode $D_s$ in RCD** is forward biased during turn-off, reverse biased during turn-on — **memorise the bias states**.
- ✅ **Snubber resistor** must be **non-inductive** and **derated 2× to 3×**.
- ✅ **Stray inductance** $L_s$ in the DC bus is the **root cause** of voltage spikes — minimise loop area using laminated bus bars.
- ✅ **dv/dt false triggering** is a thyristor-specific failure mode — prevented by RC snubbers across the device.
- ✅ **Switching loss reduction** is the primary benefit — overlap area of $V \times I$ is shrunk.
- ✅ **Snubber time constant** $\tau = R_s C_s$ should be approximately $3 t_{off}$ of the device.
- ✅ **KTU favourite question pattern:** "Design an RC snubber for the following chopper specifications" — practise the numerical template thoroughly.
- ✅ **Reverse-recovery of the freewheeling diode** is another major source of switching stress — snubbers also help here.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase and Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Phase and Three Phase Diode Bridge Rectifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Diode Bridge Rectifier?

A **Diode Bridge Rectifier** is an AC-to-DC power conversion circuit that uses an array of diodes arranged in a *bridge topology* to convert a sinusoidal AC input voltage (single-phase or three-phase) into a unidirectional (pulsating DC) output voltage. Since diodes are *uncontrolled* switches (turn ON/OFF automatically at the AC zero crossings), the firing angle $\alpha = 0^\circ$ is implicit, and the circuit delivers the *maximum possible* average DC voltage for the given AC supply.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim intent):**  
> *An uncontrolled diode rectifier uses the inherent unidirectional conduction property of power diodes to perform rectification. The output DC voltage is therefore a fixed function of the AC input amplitude and circuit topology — it cannot be regulated without additional converter stages.*

There are **two principal families** covered in this module:

| Family | Pulses per Cycle | Typical DC Ripple | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **1-Phase Full-Wave Bridge** | 2 | High (≈ 48 %) | Low-power SMPS, adapters, battery chargers |
| **3-Phase Half-Wave (3-pulse)** | 3 | Moderate (≈ 18 %) | Legacy DC drives, field excitation |
| **3-Phase Full-Wave Bridge (6-pulse)** | 6 | Very Low (≈ 4.2 %) | Industrial DC drives, HVDC, electrolysis |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Flow Tap"

Imagine a rectangular water tank (the **DC load** $R_L$) being filled from a *to-and-fro* river (the **AC mains**). A simple check-valve (a **half-wave rectifier**) lets water flow in only one direction, but half the river's flow is wasted. Now imagine a **4-valve bridge** at the river mouth: as the river flows one way, two valves open; as it reverses, the *other* two valves open — but the water *always* enters the tank from the same inlet. The tank fills twice per cycle. That is exactly what a single-phase diode bridge does. The three-phase version is like **three rivers merging** into one tank, giving six "valve-pair" events per AC cycle — yielding nearly smooth DC.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key physical constant used throughout this module:**  
> - Line frequency: $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ (India, KTU standard)  
> - Peak phase voltage: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{rms}$  
> - For a 230 V single-phase domestic supply: $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 \approx 325.27 \text{ V}$

## 1.3 Why Diodes (and not Thyristors) Here?

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In **uncontrolled** rectifiers, we have no gate control. The diode starts conducting the moment its anode voltage exceeds its cathode voltage. This makes them **simpler, cheaper, rugged, and highly reliable** for fixed-output applications. Trade-off: **no voltage regulation** — output is purely a function of the AC input.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Phase Full-Wave Diode Bridge Rectifier (Most Important)

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology
- **4 diodes** ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) arranged in a closed loop.
- The AC source feeds two opposite corners; the DC load is connected across the other two corners.

### 2.1.2 Conduction Logic (Step-by-Step)
- **Positive half-cycle** ($0 \le \omega t \le \pi$): Diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ are forward-biased. Current path: **AC source → $D_1$ → Load ($R_L$) → $D_2$ → back to source**. The load sees $+V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- **Negative half-cycle** ($\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$): Diodes $D_3$ and $D_4$ conduct. Current still flows **down through the load in the same direction** — output voltage is again $+V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- **Net effect:** Both halves of the input are "flipped" into positive polarity → **2 output pulses per AC cycle**.

### 2.1.3 Why It Works (The 'Why' Behind Each Step)
- A diode conducts only when $V_{anode} \ge V_{cathode}$.
- During the positive half, $D_1, D_2$ form a low-impedance path, while $D_3, D_4$ are reverse-biased (they block the reverse voltage).
- Symmetry reverses in the negative half — the bridge therefore *commutates naturally* at every AC zero crossing.

### 2.1.4 Critical Performance Quantities

| Parameter | Formula | Value (in terms of $V_m$) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Average DC voltage, $V_{dc}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_m}{\pi}$ | $0.6366\,V_m$ |
| RMS DC voltage, $V_{rms}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \sqrt{2} = V_m$ *(after ripple)* | $V_m$ |
| Form Factor, $FF$ | $\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}$ | $1.11$ |
| Ripple Factor, $\gamma$ | $\sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1}$ | $0.482$ |
| Rectification Efficiency, $\eta$ | $\dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \dfrac{0.4053}{0.5} \cdot 2$ | $81.2\,\%$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage, $PIV$ | $V_m$ | $V_m$ |
| Transformer Utilization, $TUF$ | $\dfrac{P_{dc}}{V_s I_s}$ | $0.692$ (centre-tap) / $0.810$ (bridge) |
| Output Frequency, $f_o$ | $2 f_s$ | $100$ Hz |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Insight:** The 81.2 % rectification efficiency is the **theoretical maximum** for a full-wave diode rectifier with resistive load — it is *not* 100 % because the AC source must also deliver AC ripple power that is dissipated as heat.

## 2.2 Three-Phase Half-Wave (3-Pulse) Diode Rectifier

- **3 diodes**, one common cathode connection at the DC output.
- Each diode conducts for **120°** ($\pi/3$ rad) per cycle.
- Conduction order: $D_1 \to D_2 \to D_3 \to D_1$ (in sync with the highest phase voltage).

### 2.2.1 Conduction Logic
- The diode connected to the *highest* instantaneous phase voltage conducts.
- Commutation happens at the crossover points: $30^\circ,\ 150^\circ,\ 270^\circ$.

### 2.2.2 Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Formula | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $V_{dc}$ (avg) | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}$ | $0.827\,V_m$ |
| $V_{rms}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2\pi}\int_{\pi/6}^{5\pi/6} V_m^2 \sin^2\theta\,d\theta}$ | $\approx 0.840\,V_m$ |
| Ripple Frequency, $f_o$ | $3 f_s$ | $150$ Hz |
| Ripple Factor, $\gamma$ | $\approx 0.183$ | $18.3\,\%$ |
| $PIV$ per diode | $\sqrt{3}\,V_m$ (line-to-line peak) | $1.732\,V_m$ |
| Diode conduction angle | $2\pi/3$ | $120^\circ$ |

## 2.3 Three-Phase Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier (6-Pulse — Industry Workhorse)

- **6 diodes** in a bridge: 3 in the *upper group* ($D_1, D_3, D_5$) tied to +DC, 3 in the *lower group* ($D_2, D_4, D_6$) tied to –DC.
- At any instant, **one upper and one lower diode** conduct (the pair tied to the *maximum* and *minimum* phase voltages).

### 2.3.1 Conduction Sequence (Each Pair Lasts 60°)
$$D_1D_2 \to D_2D_3 \to D_3D_4 \to D_4D_5 \to D_5D_6 \to D_6D_1 \to D_1D_2 \cdots$$

### 2.3.2 Performance Parameters (Killer Sheet for Numerical Problems)

| Parameter | Formula | Numerical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| $V_{dc}$ (avg) | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}$ | $1.654\,V_m$ |
| $V_{dc}$ (in $V_{LL,rms}$) | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL,rms}}{\pi}$ | $1.350\,V_{LL,rms}$ |
| $V_{rms}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2\pi}\int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2}(\sqrt{3}V_m\sin\theta)^2 d\theta}$ | $1.655\,V_m$ |
| Ripple Factor, $\gamma$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2}{9}-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $0.042$ |
| Output Frequency, $f_o$ | $6 f_s$ | $300$ Hz |
| $PIV$ per diode | $\sqrt{3}\,V_m$ | $1.732\,V_m$ |
| Diode conduction angle | $2\pi/3$ | $120^\circ$ |
| THD of source current | (for highly inductive load) | $\approx 30\,\%$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> - **Three-phase full-wave bridges** feed the DC link of industrial VFDs, HVDC transmission converters, electroplating baths, and large DC motors.  
> - **Single-phase bridges** are inside every laptop charger, mobile adapter, and TV SMPS.  
> - Ripple factor dictates the **filter capacitor size** — lower ripple ⇒ smaller capacitor needed for the same DC quality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Output Voltage of 1-Phase Full-Wave Bridge

**Given:** Input $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$; load $R_L$ (resistive).

The output across the load is the *full-wave rectified* sine:
$$v_o(\omega t) = \begin{cases} +V_m \sin(\omega t), & 0 \le \omega t \le \pi \\ -V_m \sin(\omega t), & \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi \end{cases}$$

By symmetry, average over one half-cycle suffices. The average is:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{0}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos\pi + \cos 0\Big] = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[-(-1) + 1\Big]$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.6366\,V_m}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RMS Output Voltage (1-Phase Bridge)

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$

Using the identity $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2}\,d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \omega t - \frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2} \right]_0^{\pi}$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - 0 - 0 \right] = \frac{V_m^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ripple Factor (1-Phase Bridge)

The AC ripple component's RMS value is:
$$V_{ac,rms} = \sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{dc}^2}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{ac,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{2} - \frac{4 V_m^2}{\pi^2}}$$

$$\text{Ripple factor } \gamma = \frac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_m^2/2 - 4V_m^2/\pi^2}}{2V_m/\pi}$$

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2337 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337}$$

$$\boxed{\gamma \approx 0.482 \quad \text{or} \quad 48.2\,\%}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Rectification Efficiency (1-Phase Bridge, Resistive Load)

Efficiency is defined as:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms}^2 / R_L} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2}$$

$$\eta = \frac{(2V_m/\pi)^2}{V_m^2 / 2} = \frac{4V_m^2/\pi^2}{V_m^2/2} = \frac{8}{\pi^2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{8}{\pi^2} = 0.8106 = 81.06\,\% \approx 81.2\,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Logic step (textual):** "DC power = DC voltage squared over load; AC power = RMS voltage squared over load. Since the load is the same, the load resistance cancels, giving a pure ratio of voltage squares."

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Average Output Voltage of 3-Phase Full-Wave Bridge (6-Pulse)

**Given:** Phase voltages $v_a = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, $v_b = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120^\circ)$, $v_c = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120^\circ)$.

Line-to-line voltage: $v_{ab} = \sqrt{3}\,V_m \sin(\omega t + 30^\circ)$, peak $= \sqrt{3}\,V_m$.

The output is the *maximum line-to-line* voltage, segmented into 6 pulses of width $60^\circ$ each. By symmetry, integrate over one segment $\omega t \in [\pi/6,\ \pi/2]$ (where $v_{ab}$ dominates):

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} \sqrt{3}\,V_m \sin(\omega t + 30^\circ)\,d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \Big[-\cos(\omega t + 30^\circ)\Big]_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2}$$

Evaluating the limits:
- At $\omega t = \pi/2$: $-\cos(120^\circ) = -(-1/2) = +1/2$
- At $\omega t = \pi/6$: $-\cos(60^\circ) = -(1/2) = -1/2$

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \left[ \frac{1}{2} - \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \right] = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.654\,V_m}$$

In terms of line RMS voltage, using $V_{LL,rms} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{ph,rms} = \sqrt{3}\,(V_m/\sqrt{2})$:

$$V_{dc} = \frac{3 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{LL,rms}}{\pi} \approx 1.350\,V_{LL,rms}$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6: RMS Output Voltage of 3-Phase Full-Wave Bridge

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} \left(\sqrt{3}\,V_m \sin(\omega t + 30^\circ)\right)^2 d(\omega t)$$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{9 V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\pi/6}^{\pi/2} \sin^2(\omega t + 30^\circ)\,d(\omega t)$$

Using $\sin^2 u = (1-\cos 2u)/2$ and substituting $u = \omega t + 30^\circ$:

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{9 V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{u}{2} - \frac{\sin 2u}{4} \right]_{u=\pi/3}^{u=2\pi/3}$$

Computing the limits:
- At $u = 2\pi/3$: $(2\pi/3)/2 - \sin(4\pi/3)/4 = \pi/3 - (-\sqrt{3}/2)/4 = \pi/3 + \sqrt{3}/8$
- At $u = \pi/3$: $(\pi/3)/2 - \sin(2\pi/3)/4 = \pi/6 - (\sqrt{3}/2)/4 = \pi/6 - \sqrt{3}/8$

Difference: $\pi/3 + \sqrt{3}/8 - \pi/6 + \sqrt{3}/8 = \pi/6 + \sqrt{3}/4$

$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{9 V_m^2}{2\pi}\left(\frac{\pi}{6} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) = V_m^2 \left(\frac{3}{4} + \frac{9\sqrt{3}}{8\pi}\right)$$

Numerically: $V_{rms} \approx 1.6554\,V_m$.

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Ripple Factor of 3-Phase Full-Wave Bridge

$$\gamma = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{1.6554}{1.654}\right)^2 - 1} \approx \sqrt{1.0017 - 1} \approx 0.0417$$

$$\boxed{\gamma \approx 0.042 = 4.2\,\%}$$

## 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready)

| Quantity | 1-Φ Bridge | 3-Φ Half-Wave | 3-Φ Full Bridge |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| No. of diodes | **4** | 3 | 6 |
| Pulses per cycle | 2 | 3 | 6 |
| $V_{dc} / V_m$ | 0.637 | 0.827 | 1.654 |
| Ripple factor $\gamma$ | 0.482 | 0.183 | 0.042 |
| $PIV / V_m$ | 1.0 | 1.732 | 1.732 |
| Diode conduction | $180^\circ$ | $120^\circ$ | $120^\circ$ |
| Output ripple freq. | $2f$ | $3f$ | $6f$ |
| Rectifier efficiency $\eta$ | 81.2 % | 96.8 % | 99.7 % |
| Use | Adapters | Excitation | Industrial DC drives |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why is the 3-phase bridge efficiency near 100 %?** Because the output is already nearly DC (ripple ≈ 4 %), so almost all input power goes into the DC component — the AC ripple power lost is tiny.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Single-Phase Diode Bridge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Source 230V 50Hz] --> B[Transformer Optional]
    B --> C[Bridge Node 1]
    C --> D1[D1 Forward Diode]
    C --> D3[D3 Reverse Diode]
    D1 --> E[DC Positive Rail]
    D3 --> F[DC Negative Rail]
    B --> G[Bridge Node 2]
    G --> D2[D2 Forward Diode]
    G --> D4[D4 Reverse Diode]
    D2 --> E
    D4 --> F
    E --> H[Load Resistor RL]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Filter Capacitor Optional]
    I --> E
    J[Output Vdc Pulsating] --> E
```

## 4.2 Conduction State Diagram — Single-Phase Bridge (2 Modes)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state1: Mode 1 - Positive Half Cycle
    state2: D1 ON, D2 ON
    state3: Output = +Vm sin wt
    state4: Mode 2 - Negative Half Cycle
    state5: D3 ON, D4 ON
    state6: Output = -Vm sin wt = +Vm sin wt after inversion
    state1 --> state2
    state2 --> state3
    state3 --> state4 : at wt = pi
    state4 --> state5
    state5 --> state6
    state6 --> state1 : at wt = 2pi
```

```mermaid
        DB1[D3 from Phase B]
        DC1[D5 from Phase C]
    end
    subgraph LowerGroup[Lower Diode Group Anode Tied]
        DA2[D4 from Phase A]
        DB2[D6 from Phase B]

    end
    PA --> DA1
    PB --> DB1
    PC --> DC1
    PA --> DA2
    PB --> DB2
    PC --> DC2
    DA1 --> BusP[DC Positive Bus]
    DB1 --> BusP
    DC1 --> BusP
    DA2 --> BusN[DC Negative Bus]

    DC2 --> BusN

    Load --> BusN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Reading these diagrams in the exam:** Examiner expects you to label the *conduction interval* of each diode, the *output voltage segments*, and the *commutation angles* (30°, 90°, 150°... for 3-phase; 0° and 180° for 1-phase). Always annotate the PIV on at least one diode in your circuit diagram.

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define rectification efficiency and ripple factor. Calculate the rectification efficiency of a single-phase full-wave diode bridge rectifier with resistive load.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Rectification Efficiency** $\eta$ is the ratio of DC output power to the RMS AC input power:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{dc}}{P_{ac}} = \frac{V_{dc}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms}^2 / R_L} = \frac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2}$$
- For 1-phase full-wave bridge: $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$, $V_{rms} = V_m/\sqrt{2}$
- Substituting: $\eta = \dfrac{(2V_m/\pi)^2}{(V_m/\sqrt{2})^2} = \dfrac{8}{\pi^2} = 0.8106 = \mathbf{81.06\,\% \approx 81.2\,\%}$
- **Ripple Factor** $\gamma$ is the ratio of AC ripple RMS to DC average: $\gamma = \sqrt{(V_{rms}/V_{dc})^2 - 1} = \mathbf{0.482}$ **(0.5 + 1.5 + 1 mark distribution)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Compare single-phase and three-phase full-wave bridge rectifiers on the basis of (i) number of diodes, (ii) ripple factor, and (iii) any one application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per point):**

| Parameter | 1-Phase Bridge | 3-Phase Bridge |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| (i) Diodes | 4 | 6 |
| (ii) Ripple factor | 0.482 | 0.042 |
| (iii) Application | Mobile chargers, SMPS | Industrial DC drives, HVDC |

---

## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — KTU Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a single-phase full-wave diode bridge rectifier feeding a resistive load. Draw the input-output waveforms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply feeds a full-wave diode bridge rectifier with $R_L = 100\ \Omega$. Calculate: (i) Average DC output voltage, (ii) RMS output voltage, (iii) Ripple factor, (iv) PIV per diode, (v) Average and RMS load currents. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Circuit diagram** (2 marks): Draw a diamond bridge of 4 diodes ($D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$) with AC input across left-right corners and $R_L$ across top-bottom corners.

2. **Positive half-cycle** (2 marks): $D_1, D_2$ conduct; $D_3, D_4$ are reverse-biased. Current path: $A \to D_1 \to R_L \to D_2 \to B$. Output $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

3. **Negative half-cycle** (2 marks): $D_3, D_4$ conduct; $D_1, D_2$ reverse-block. Path: $B \to D_3 \to R_L \to D_4 \to A$. Output $v_o = -V_m \sin(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ *in magnitude, same polarity across load*.

4. **Waveform sketch** (1 mark): Show 2 positive half-cycles ("humps") per input cycle.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given: $V_{rms} = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_L = 100\ \Omega$.

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**(i) Average DC voltage:**
$$V_{dc} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} = \mathbf{207.07\ V}$$

**(ii) RMS output voltage:**
$$V_{rms,out} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{325.27}{\sqrt{2}} = \mathbf{230\ V}$$

**(iii) Ripple factor:**
$$\gamma = \sqrt{\frac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} = \sqrt{0.2337} = \mathbf{0.482}$$

**(iv) PIV per diode:** $\text{PIV} = V_m = \mathbf{325.27\ V}$

**(v) Currents:**
$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{207.07}{100} = \mathbf{2.07\ A}$$
$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms,out}}{R_L} = \frac{230}{100} = \mathbf{2.30\ A}$$

**Mark Split:** (i)+(ii) = 2 marks, (iii) = 2 marks, (iv) = 1 mark, (v) = 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit and waveforms, explain the operation of a three-phase full-wave diode bridge rectifier. Derive the expression for the average output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V, 50 Hz star-connected source feeds a 3-phase full-wave diode bridge rectifier with $R_L = 50\ \Omega$. Find: (i) $V_{dc}$, (ii) $I_{dc}$, (iii) PIV per diode, (iv) Ripple factor, (v) Output ripple frequency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Circuit diagram** (2 marks): 6 diodes, 3-phase AC input on one side, DC output $R_L$ on the other.

2. **Conduction rule** (1 mark): The diode tied to the *highest* phase conducts in the upper group; the diode tied to the *lowest* phase conducts in the lower group.

3. **Conduction sequence** (1 mark): $D_1D_2,\ D_2D_3,\ D_3D_4,\ D_4D_5,\ D_5D_6,\ D_6D_1$ — each pair lasts $60^\circ$.

4. **Waveform sketch** (1 mark): 6 humps per cycle, each of width $60^\circ$.

5. **Derivation of $V_{dc}$** (2 marks): See Section 3.5 — final result $V_{dc} = 3\sqrt{3}V_m/\pi \approx 1.654\,V_m$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

Given: $V_{LL,rms} = 400$ V, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_L = 50\ \Omega$.

Phase voltage peak:
$$V_m = \frac{V_{ph,rms} \cdot \sqrt{2}}{1} = \frac{400/\sqrt{3} \times \sqrt{2}}{1} = \frac{400\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{3}} = 326.6\ \text{V}$$

**(i) Average DC voltage:**
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 326.6}{\pi} = \mathbf{540\ V}$$

Alternative via line RMS:
$$V_{dc} = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{LL,rms}}{\pi} = \frac{3\sqrt{2} \times 400}{\pi} = \mathbf{540\ V}$$

**(ii) Average DC current:**
$$I_{dc} = \frac{V_{dc}}{R_L} = \frac{540}{50} = \mathbf{10.8\ A}$$

**(iii) PIV per diode:**
$$\text{PIV} = \sqrt{3}\,V_m = 1.732 \times 326.6 = \mathbf{565.7\ V}$$

**(iv) Ripple factor:** $\gamma = \mathbf{0.042}$ (4.2 %) — use the formula $\gamma = \sqrt{(V_{rms}/V_{dc})^2 - 1}$ with $V_{rms} \approx 1.655\,V_m$.

**(v) Output ripple frequency:** $f_o = 6 \times 50 = \mathbf{300\ Hz}$

**Mark Split:** (i)+(ii) = 3 marks, (iii) = 1 mark, (iv)+(v) = 1.5 + 1.5 marks.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common places students lose marks on this topic:**  
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RMS to peak** ($V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}$). Many students directly use $V_{rms}$ in $2V_m/\pi$. **−1 to −2 marks.**  
> 2. **Confusing $V_{LL}$ with $V_{ph}$** in 3-phase problems. Star-connected source: $V_{ph} = V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$. **−1 mark.**  
> 3. **Not specifying PIV formula explicitly** — just writing "PIV = Vm" without showing $PIV = V_m$ (1-phase) or $PIV = \sqrt{3}\,V_m$ (3-phase) costs you the formula mark. **−0.5 mark.**  
> 4. **Wrong conduction interval** — for 3-phase full bridge, each diode conducts $120^\circ$, not $60^\circ$. **−1 mark.**  
> 5. **Drawing the 3-phase bridge with wrong diode orientation** (anode-cathode reversal) — examiner awards 0 for the circuit diagram mark.  
> 6. **Not labelling the output polarity** in the circuit diagram — minor but cumulatively loses 0.5 mark.  
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — e.g., writing "207" instead of "207 V". Always include SI units.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Diode rectifier → uncontrolled → $\alpha = 0^\circ$** (always).
- ✅ **1-Phase Bridge** uses **4 diodes**; output has **2 pulses/cycle**; $V_{dc} = 2V_m/\pi$; ripple = **48.2 %**; efficiency = **81.2 %**; PIV = $V_m$.
- ✅ **3-Phase Half-Wave** uses **3 diodes**; **3 pulses/cycle**; $V_{dc} = 3\sqrt{3}V_m/(2\pi) \approx 0.827\,V_m$; PIV = $\sqrt{3}\,V_m$.
- ✅ **3-Phase Full Bridge** uses **6 diodes**; **6 pulses/cycle**; $V_{dc} = 3\sqrt{3}V_m/\pi \approx 1.654\,V_m$; ripple = **4.2 %**; PIV = $\sqrt{3}\,V_m$.
- ✅ Each diode in a **bridge** conducts for **$\pi$ rad (1-phase) or $2\pi/3$ (3-phase)**.
- ✅ **Commutation in diode rectifiers is *natural*** — happens at AC zero or phase crossover points, no external trigger.
- ✅ **Conversion chain:** $V_{rms} \xrightarrow{\times\sqrt{2}} V_m \xrightarrow{\times 2/\pi} V_{dc}$ (1-phase bridge).
- ✅ **Conversion chain (3-phase bridge):** $V_{LL,rms} \xrightarrow{\times\sqrt{2}} V_{LL,max} \xrightarrow{\times 3/\pi} V_{dc}$.
- ✅ **Output ripple frequency** = (pulses/cycle) $\times$ supply frequency.
- ✅ **Resistive load** → current discontinuous for 1-phase near zero-crossing; **Inductive load** → current continuous, smoother output.
- ✅ **Lower ripple factor** ⇒ **smaller filter capacitor** required for the same DC quality — engineering design insight.
- ✅ **PIV rating** must be ≥ 1.5 to 2 × the calculated value (derating safety margin) in real design.
- ✅ Always **draw waveforms first** before writing formulas — this earns the "neat diagram" mark.
- ✅ In numericals, **show the formula, substitute values, and box the final answer** with units — KTU examiners reward this structure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Phase and Three Phase Controlled Rectifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Phase and Three Phase Controlled Rectifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts a fixed AC supply voltage into a variable DC output voltage by phase-controlled switching of **Silicon Controlled Rectifiers (SCRs / Thyristors)**. The output DC voltage is regulated by varying the **firing angle (α)**, which is the instant (measured from the natural commutation point) at which the thyristor is gated into conduction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A controlled rectifier uses thyristors (SCRs) as the switching elements. The average output voltage $V_o$ is a function of the firing angle $\alpha$, given by the general form:
> $$V_o = V_{m,\text{line}} \cdot \frac{m}{\pi} \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{m}\right) \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$
> where $m$ is the pulse number and $V_{m,\text{line}}$ is the peak line voltage.

### Classification of Controlled Rectifiers (KTU Hierarchy)

| Configuration | No. of SCRs | No. of Diodes | Pulse Number ($m$) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1-φ Half-Wave** | 1 | 0 | 1 | Low-power DC drives, battery charging |
| **1-φ Full-Wave (M2)** | 2 | 0 | 2 | Mid-power DC drives, electroplating |
| **1-φ Bridge (B2)** | 4 | 0 | 2 | UPS systems, SMPS front-end |
| **3-φ Half-Wave (M3)** | 3 | 0 | 3 | High-current, low-voltage DC supplies |
| **3-φ Full-Wave (B6)** | 6 | 0 | 6 | Industrial DC motor drives, HVDC |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap" and "Faucet" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Delayed Faucet**
> Imagine an AC supply as a water pipe with water pressure oscillating in waves (positive and negative half-cycles). An **uncontrolled diode** is like a faucet that is permanently open — water flows whenever the pressure is in the right direction. A **controlled rectifier (thyristor)** is a faucet with a **time-delay lock**: even when water (positive voltage) is available, the faucet remains closed until you manually trigger the handle (apply a gate pulse). The longer you wait (larger $\alpha$), the less water flows through, and the lower the average tank level (DC output voltage) becomes. **The firing angle α is essentially the "delay" between the natural water arrival and your manual opening of the faucet.**

### Key Terminology — The Building Blocks

- **Firing Angle ($\alpha$):** The electrical angle (in degrees or radians) measured from the instant the thyristor becomes forward-biased to the instant it is triggered. Range: $0° \leq \alpha \leq 180°$ for full-wave bridge.
- **Extinction Angle ($\gamma$):** The instant (in electrical degrees) at which conduction ceases, measured from the zero-crossing of the supply.
- **Conduction Angle ($\theta_c$ or $\gamma - \alpha$):** The duration (in degrees) for which the thyristor actually conducts.
- **Commutation Angle ($\mu$):** The overlap period during which the outgoing and incoming thyristors both conduct due to source inductance. For ideal circuits, $\mu = 0$.
- **Natural Commutation Point (NCP):** The instant at which a diode would begin conduction if the rectifier were uncontrolled (i.e., $\alpha = 0$ for a thyristor).

### Physical Constants & Standard Parameters

- **Line frequency:** $f = 50$ Hz (India, KTU standard)
- **Angular line frequency:** $\omega = 2\pi f = 314.159$ rad/s
- **Peak phase voltage:** $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{\text{rms,phase}}$
- **Peak line voltage:** $V_{mL} = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{\text{rms,line}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_m$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier (1-φ HWCR)

### Circuit Topology
- A single SCR is connected between the AC source and the resistive load $R$.
- The SCR conducts only during the **positive half-cycle**, from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **$\omega t = 0$ to $\alpha$:** Supply is positive, but the SCR has not yet received a gate pulse. SCR is in **forward blocking mode**. Output $V_o = 0$.
2. **$\omega t = \alpha$:** Gate pulse $I_g$ is applied. SCR latches into conduction. $V_o = V_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
3. **$\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$:** SCR conducts. $V_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.
4. **$\omega t = \pi$:** Supply voltage reverses polarity. Anode current falls below holding current. SCR turns OFF naturally (line commutation).
5. **$\omega t = \pi$ to $2\pi$:** SCR is reverse-biased. $V_o = 0$.
6. **Cycle repeats** with period $2\pi$.

### Average Output Voltage Derivation
The average DC output voltage is obtained by integrating the instantaneous output over one full cycle divided by the period:

$$V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Evaluating the integral:
$$V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} (1 + \cos\alpha)$$

### RMS Output Voltage
$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)} = \frac{V_m}{2} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$$

## 2.2 Single-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifiers

### (a) Mid-Point (Two-Pulse M2) Configuration
Uses a center-tapped transformer and 2 SCRs. SCR $T_1$ conducts during the positive half-cycle (from $\alpha$ to $\pi + \alpha$ relative to $V_s$ zero), and $T_2$ conducts during the negative half-cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Each thyristor carries the full load current, but blocks a peak inverse voltage of $2V_m$** (twice the peak of each half-secondary). This is the major drawback of the M2 configuration.

**Average Output Voltage:**
$$V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{2\sqrt{2} V_{\text{rms}}}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

### (b) Full-Bridge (Two-Pulse B2) Configuration
Uses 4 SCRs in an H-bridge. Pairs $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_3, T_4)$ conduct alternately, providing full-wave rectification without a center-tapped transformer.

**Average Output Voltage (same form as M2):**
$$V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The PIV across each SCR in a bridge is only $V_m$ (half that of M2),** making the bridge configuration preferred for higher-voltage applications.

## 2.3 Three-Phase Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier (M3)

### Operational Sequence
- Three SCRs $T_1, T_2, T_3$ are connected to the three phase voltages $V_{an}, V_{bn}, V_{cn}$.
- The thyristor connected to the most positive phase conducts at any given time.
- Conduction sequence: $T_1 \to T_2 \to T_3 \to T_1$ (each conducts for **120°**).
- With firing angle $\alpha$ delayed from the natural commutation point, the conduction period for each thyristor becomes $\alpha$ to $\alpha + 120°$.

### Average Output Voltage
For a resistive load with continuous conduction:
$$V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{2\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{6} V_{\text{rms,line}}}{2\pi} \cos\alpha$$

## 2.4 Three-Phase Full-Wave Controlled Rectifier (B6 / 3-φ Bridge)

### Operational Sequence
- Six SCRs arranged as a bridge: $T_1, T_3, T_5$ (positive group, common cathode) and $T_4, T_6, T_2$ (negative group, common anode).
- **Pulse number $m = 6$**, giving superior output smoothing and lower ripple.
- Each thyristor conducts for **60°** in ideal conditions.
- The line-to-line voltage is the output, not the phase voltage.

### Average Output Voltage
$$V_{o,\text{avg}} = \frac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{6} V_{\text{rms,line}}}{\pi} \cos\alpha$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inversion Mode (Inverter Operation):** When $\alpha > 90°$, $V_{o,\text{avg}}$ becomes **negative**. With a DC source on the load side (e.g., DC motor back-EMF or battery), power flows from DC to AC. This is the fundamental principle behind **line-commutated inverters** used in HVDC transmission and regenerative braking of DC drives.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It covers 80% of KTU numerical problems on Module 1.**

| # | Configuration | $V_{o,\text{avg}}$ | $V_{o,\text{rms}}$ | $V_{o,\text{avg}}$ at $\alpha = 0$ | Range of $\alpha$ for $V_o \geq 0$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1-φ Half-Wave (R-load) | $\dfrac{V_m(1+\cos\alpha)}{2\pi}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{2}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{\alpha}{\pi}+\dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$ | $0°$ to $180°$ |
| 2 | 1-φ Full-Wave (M2/B2, R-load) | $\dfrac{2 V_m \cos\alpha}{\pi}$ | $\dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{2\alpha}{2\pi}+\dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | $0°$ to $90°$ |
| 3 | 1-φ Full-Wave (RLE-load) | $\dfrac{2 V_m \cos\alpha}{\pi}$ | Depends on load | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | $0°$ to $180°$ (extended due to load inductance) |
| 4 | 3-φ Half-Wave (M3) | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{V_m\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{2}+\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{8\pi}\cos 2\alpha}$ | $\dfrac{3\sqrt{3}V_m}{2\pi}$ | $0°$ to $90°$ |
| 5 | 3-φ Full Bridge (B6) | $\dfrac{3 V_{mL}}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | $\dfrac{V_{mL}\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{1-\dfrac{3\cos 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $\dfrac{3V_{mL}}{\pi}$ | $0°$ to $90°$ (RL); $0°$ to $180°$ (RLE inverter) |

### Auxiliary Formulas

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Rectification Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2 / R}{V_{rms}^2 / R} = \dfrac{V_{dc}^2}{V_{rms}^2}$ |
| Form Factor (FF) | $FF = \dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}$ |
| Ripple Factor (RF) | $RF = \sqrt{FF^2 - 1} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{V_{rms}}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 - 1}$ |
| Transformer Utilization Factor (TUF) | $TUF = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{V_s I_s}$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage (1-φ HW) | $V_m$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage (1-φ M2) | $2 V_m$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage (1-φ B2) | $V_m$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage (3-φ M3) | $\sqrt{3} V_m$ |
| Peak Inverse Voltage (3-φ B6) | $V_{mL} = \sqrt{3} V_m$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVDC Transmission Systems:** Three-phase fully-controlled bridge rectifiers (B6) are the heart of HVDC converter stations, transferring bulk power over hundreds of kilometers with minimal losses.
- **DC Motor Drives (Variable Speed):** Ward-Leonard systems and modern phase-controlled rectifiers provide adjustable armature voltage for precise speed control of DC motors in steel rolling mills, paper plants, and elevators.
- **Battery Chargers:** Single-phase half-wave and full-wave controlled rectifiers regulate charging current in traction battery systems and UPS backup batteries.
- **Electrochemical Processes:** Electroplating, electrolysis (chlor-alkali plants), and aluminum smelting use high-current controlled rectifiers.
- **Static VAR Compensators (SVCs):** Thyristor Controlled Reactors (TCRs) — based on anti-parallel thyristor pairs — provide dynamic reactive power compensation in power grids.
- **Renewable Energy Interfacing:** Grid-tie inverters for solar PV and wind use similar phase-control principles for synchronization.
