# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"Why is the Boost Converter also called a Step-Up Chopper? Justify with its voltage transfer ratio in CCM."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A boost converter is called a step-up chopper because its output voltage is always **greater than or equal to** the input source voltage. In CCM, the voltage transfer ratio is derived from volt-second balance on the inductor:

$$\frac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = \frac{1}{1-D}$$

Since the duty cycle $D$ satisfies $0 < D < 1$, the denominator $(1-D)$ is less than 1, making $V_{o}/V_{s} > 1$. Therefore $V_{o} > V_{s}$, confirming the step-up (boost) action. The output voltage can be varied from $V_{s}$ (at $D = 0$) to a theoretical infinity (at $D \to 1$) by adjusting the duty cycle. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for justification.)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Compare the polarity of output voltage in boost and buck-boost converters. Why does buck-boost produce an inverted output?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter |
|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| Output Polarity | Same as input (non-inverted) | **Inverted** (opposite polarity) |
| Voltage Sign | $V_{o}/V_{s} = +1/(1-D)$ | $V_{o}/V_{s} = -D/(1-D)$ |
| Ground Reference | Common ground between input and output | **Different** ground reference |

**Reason for inversion:** During the switch ON-time, the inductor is connected across $V_{s}$ and the load is supplied only by the capacitor. During the switch OFF-time, the inductor's stored current freewheels through the diode into the output in such a direction that the **lower terminal of the load becomes positive with respect to the upper terminal** (when referenced to the input ground). This reversal of current direction through the load produces the negative output polarity. **(3 marks — 1 for polarity identification, 1 for table, 1 for inversion reason.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a **Boost Converter** operating in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM). State clearly the assumption made during the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a Boost Converter with $V_{s} = 15$ V, $D = 0.3$, $L = 250\ \mu H$, $C = 200\ \mu F$, $f_{s} = 20$ kHz, and $R = 30\ \Omega$, calculate:
  - (i) Output voltage $V_{o}$
  - (ii) Critical inductance $L_{c}$
  - (iii) Inductor current ripple $\Delta I_{L}$
  - (iv) Output voltage ripple $\Delta V_{o}$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

> **Assumption:** The converter is in **steady-state CCM** with ideal components (lossless switch, diode, inductor, and capacitor).

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \leq t \leq DT_{s}$):** The switch is closed, diode is reverse-biased. The source is applied across the inductor:

$$V_{L,\text{ON}} = V_{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di_{L}}{dt} = \frac{V_{s}}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta I_{L,\text{ON}} = \frac{V_{s} \cdot D T_{s}}{L}$$

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $DT_{s} \leq t \leq T_{s}$):** The switch is open, diode is forward-biased. The inductor, source, and output form a series loop:

$$V_{L,\text{OFF}} = V_{s} - V_{o} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta I_{L,\text{OFF}} = \frac{(V_{s} - V_{o})(1-D)T_{s}}{L}$$

**Applying Volt-Second Balance** (net change in inductor current over one period is zero):

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{ON}} + \Delta I_{L,\text{OFF}} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{s} \cdot D T_{s}}{L} + \frac{(V_{s} - V_{o})(1-D)T_{s}}{L} = 0$$

$$V_{s} D + (V_{s} - V_{o})(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_{s} D + V_{s} - V_{s} D - V_{o}(1-D) = 0$$

$$V_{s} = V_{o}(1-D)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = \frac{1}{1-D}} \quad \text{(7 marks — 2 for assumption, 2 for Mode 1+2, 3 for volt-second balance and final result)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{o} = \frac{V_{s}}{1-D} = \frac{15}{1-0.3} = \frac{15}{0.7} = 21.43\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Critical Inductance:**

$$L_{c} = \frac{R (1-D)^{2} D}{2 f_{s}} = \frac{30 \cdot (0.7)^{2} \cdot 0.3}{2 \cdot 20000}$$

$$L_{c} = \frac{30 \cdot 0.49 \cdot 0.3}{40000} = \frac{4.41}{40000} = 110.25\ \mu H$$

Since $L = 250\ \mu H > L_{c} = 110.25\ \mu H$, the converter is in **CCM** ✓

**(iii) Inductor Current Ripple:**

$$T_{s} = \frac{1}{f_{s}} = \frac{1}{20000} = 50\ \mu s$$

$$\Delta I_{L} = \frac{V_{s} \cdot D \cdot T_{s}}{L} = \frac{15 \cdot 0.3 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{225 \times 10^{-6}}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.9\ \text{A}$$

**(iv) Output Voltage Ripple:**

$$\Delta V_{o} = \frac{D \cdot T_{s}}{R \cdot C} = \frac{0.3 \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6}}{30 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-6}}{6 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5\ \text{mV}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 1.5 for (iii), 1.5 for (iv))**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **operation of a Buck-Boost Converter in CCM** for both switch ON and OFF states. Derive its voltage transfer ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Buck-Boost Converter has $V_{s} = 20$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 150\ \mu H$, $f_{s} = 40$ kHz, $R = 5\ \Omega$. Determine:
  - (i) Output voltage $V_{o}$
  - (ii) Whether the converter is operating in buck or boost mode
  - (iii) Average and peak inductor currents
  - (iv) Critical inductance **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Operation and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \leq t \leq DT_{s}$):** Switch is closed; diode is reverse-biased. Inductor is connected across $V_{s}$ and stores energy; current rises linearly:

$$V_{L,\text{ON}} = V_{s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di_{L}}{dt} = \frac{V_{s}}{L}$$

The capacitor discharges into the load. The output is **isolated** from the source during this interval.

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $DT_{s} \leq t \leq T_{s}$):** Switch is open; diode is forward-biased. The inductor freewheels its stored energy into the capacitor and load. The current path forces a **polarity inversion** at the output:

$$V_{L,\text{OFF}} = V_{o} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di_{L}}{dt} = \frac{V_{o}}{L}$$

**Derivation using Volt-Second Balance:**

$$\frac{V_{s} \cdot D T_{s}}{L} + \frac{V_{o} (1-D) T_{s}}{L} = 0$$

$$V_{s} D + V_{o} (1-D) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = -\frac{D}{1-D}} \quad \text{(7 marks — 2 for block diagram, 2 for Mode 1+2, 3 for derivation)}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{o} = -\frac{D \cdot V_{s}}{1-D} = -\frac{0.4 \cdot 20}{1-0.4} = -\frac{8}{0.6} = -13.33\ \text{V}$$

**(ii) Buck or Boost Mode:**

Since $D = 0.4 < 0.5$, the converter operates in **buck mode** (output magnitude less than input). Specifically, $\vert V_{o} \vert = 13.33\ \text{V} < V_{s} = 20\ \text{V}$.

**(iii) Average and Peak Inductor Currents:**

$$I_{L,\text{avg}} = \frac{\vert V_{o} \vert}{(1-D) R} = \frac{13.33}{0.6 \cdot 5} = \frac{13.33}{3} = 4.44\ \text{A}$$

$$\Delta I_{L} = \frac{V_{s} \cdot D \cdot T_{s}}{L} = \frac{20 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6}}{150 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{150 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.33\ \text{A}$$

$$I_{L,\text{peak}} = I_{L,\text{avg}} + \frac{\Delta I_{L}}{2} = 4.44 + 0.667 = 5.11\ \text{A}$$

**(iv) Critical Inductance:**

$$L_{c} = \frac{R (1-D)^{2}}{2 f_{s}} = \frac{5 \cdot (0.6)^{2}}{2 \cdot 40000} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.36}{80000} = \frac{1.8}{80000} = 22.5\ \mu H$$

Since $L = 150\ \mu H > L_{c} = 22.5\ \mu H$, the converter is confirmed in **CCM** ✓

**(7 marks — 2 for (i), 1 for (ii), 2 for (iii), 2 for (iv))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the buck-boost transfer ratio costs **1 mark per instance**. Always include $V_{o}/V_{s} = -D/(1-D)$.
> 2. **Wrong $L_{c}$ formula for buck-boost:** It is $L_{c} = R(1-D)^{2}/(2f_{s})$ — **not** multiplied by $D$ like the boost. Confusion here costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the assumption** of ideal components and steady-state CCM at the start of derivations costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Numerical unit errors:** Converting $\mu H$ to $H$ or $kHz$ to $Hz$ incorrectly leads to ripple answers off by factors of $10^{6}$ — common **2-mark** deduction.
> 5. **Not verifying CCM** at the end of numerical problems: Showing $I_{L,\text{min}} > 0$ or $L > L_{c}$ earns **0.5–1 bonus mark** in valuation.
> 6. **Wrong $\Delta V_{o}$ formula:** For boost and buck-boost, $\Delta V_{o} = DT_{s}/(RC)$ — not $V_{o}DT_{s}/(RC)$. Marks lost: **1**.
