> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review):**

- **Boost Converter:**
  - Voltage gain: $V_{o}/V_{s} = 1/(1-D)$ — always step-up
  - Output is **non-inverted** (same polarity as input)
  - Inductor charges during switch ON, releases *in series* with source during OFF
  - Switch voltage stress = $V_{o}$ (must be rated for full output)
  - Critical inductance: $L_{c} = R(1-D)^{2}D/(2f_{s})$
  - Inductor current ripple: $\Delta I_{L} = V_{s}DT_{s}/L$
  - Output voltage ripple: $\Delta V_{o} = DT_{s}/(RC)$

- **Buck-Boost Converter:**
  - Voltage gain: $V_{o}/V_{s} = -D/(1-D)$ — **negative sign is mandatory**
  - Magnitude: $\vert V_{o} \vert / V_{s} = D/(1-D)$
  - Output is **inverted** (opposite polarity)
  - At $D = 0.5$, unity gain ($\vert V_{o} \vert = V_{s}$)
  - Inductor charges from $V_{s}$ during ON, releases to load (alone) during OFF
  - Critical inductance: $L_{c} = R(1-D)^{2}/(2f_{s})$ (no $D$ in numerator!)
  - Switch voltage stress = $V_{s} + \vert V_{o} \vert$ (higher than boost)

- **Common Points:**
  - Both store energy in $L$ during switch ON
  - Both rely on volt-second balance for steady-state analysis
  - Both require $L \geq L_{c}$ to remain in CCM
  - Both have filter capacitor $C$ to smooth output

- **Key Differences to Memorize:**
  - Boost → always $V_{o} > V_{s}$, same polarity
  - Buck-Boost → $V_{o}$ can be above or below $V_{s}$, opposite polarity

- **CCM Condition:** $I_{L,\text{min}} > 0$ OR equivalently $L > L_{c}$ for given load and duty cycle.

- **Design Rule of Thumb:** Choose $L = 1.25 \cdot L_{c}$ to ensure CCM with 25% safety margin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Isolated Converters - Forward, Push-Pull, Half Bridge, Full Bridge and Flyback Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Isolated Converters - Forward, Push-Pull, Half Bridge, Full Bridge and Flyback Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Isolated DC-DC Converters — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **Isolated DC-DC Converter** is a switched-mode power converter that uses a **high-frequency transformer** to provide **galvanic isolation** between the input source and the output load, while simultaneously performing **DC voltage transformation**. Isolation is mandatory in applications where the load must be protected from input-side faults, where safety standards (IEC 60950, IEC 62368) demand a dielectric barrier, or where multiple isolated output rails are required from a single DC bus.

The five isolated topologies mandated by the KTU PCAET503 Module 2 syllabus are:

1. **Forward Converter** — Single-switch, single-transistor forward topology.
2. **Push-Pull Converter** — Two-switch, center-tapped primary topology.
3. **Half-Bridge Converter** — Two-switch, capacitor-divider topology.
4. **Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) Converter** — Four-switch, full-bridge topology.
5. **Flyback Converter** — Single-switch, energy-storage (coupled-inductor) topology.

> [!NOTE]
> **Galvanic isolation** means there is **NO electrical (conductive) path** between input and output — energy is transferred purely through **magnetic flux coupling** across the transformer core.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pneumatic Postman" Intuition

Imagine an **electronic postman** who must deliver parcels (energy) from a dangerous warehouse (input DC bus) to a residential colony (sensitive load). The postman **refuses to walk through the warehouse itself** because it is electrically "live" and unsafe. Instead, he walks across a **suspended rope bridge (the high-frequency transformer)**. He never touches the warehouse floor or the colony floor directly, but the parcels cross over safely.

- The **rope bridge** = High-frequency transformer (provides isolation).
- The **parcel weight** = Power being transferred.
- The **walking rhythm** = Switching frequency (typically 20 kHz – 500 kHz, well above the audible band to avoid acoustic noise).
- The **bridge tension** = Magnetizing flux density $B_m$ in the core (must be reset every cycle to avoid saturation — this is the central design challenge of every isolated topology).

The **flyback converter** is unique: the postman **carries the parcel to the middle of the bridge, drops it in a basket, then walks back and pushes the basket from the other side** — i.e., energy is **stored** in the magnetizing inductance first, then **released** in a later switching phase.

The **forward converter** and its bridge-family cousins (push-pull, half-bridge, full-bridge) all behave like the postman walking the parcel **directly across the bridge** while the switch is on — i.e., energy transfer is **immediate** in the same switching interval.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Design Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Switching frequency $f_s$ | **20 kHz – 500 kHz** | Higher $f_s$ → smaller transformer, but greater switching loss |
| Maximum flux density $B_{max}$ | **0.2 T – 0.3 T** for ferrites | Below saturation flux $B_{sat} \approx 0.4$ T |
| Isolation voltage rating | **1.5 kV – 4 kV RMS** | Per safety/agencies (IEC 62368-1) |
| Transformer turns ratio $n = N_s/N_p$ | **0.1 – 10** | Set by $V_{in}$ and $V_o$ range |
| Magnetizing inductance $L_m$ | **mH range** in flyback | Stores the transferred energy |
| Duty cycle $D$ | **0 – 0.5** (forward) / **0 – 1** (others) | Topology-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high-frequency transformers?** At 50 kHz, a transformer core can be **1000× smaller** than a 50 Hz line-frequency transformer delivering the same power. This is the single most important reason isolated SMPS exists — it makes switch-mode power supplies compact, light, and efficient (typically 85% – 95%).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Isolation is Needed — The Engineering "Why"

- **Safety**: A fault on the high-voltage DC bus (e.g., a 400 V traction bus) must not propagate to a user-touchable 5 V/12 V logic rail.
- **Ground-loop elimination**: Different grounds in cascaded systems can cause circulating currents and noise; isolation breaks the loop.
- **Multiple output voltages**: A single transformer with multiple secondary windings can generate isolated 5 V, 12 V, and 24 V rails from one input.
- **Voltage scaling**: The turns ratio $N_s/N_p$ allows free step-up or step-down, decoupled from the duty cycle.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Common Framework

Every isolated converter (except flyback) follows this **two-phase** cycle per switching period $T_s$:

**Phase 1 (Switch ON, duration $DT_s$):**
- Primary current $i_p$ rises; flux $\phi$ in the core increases linearly.
- Power is delivered to the output through the secondary winding (forward action).
- Output inductor stores energy; output capacitor charges.

**Phase 2 (Switch OFF, duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
- Primary current is forced to zero.
- The core flux **must be reset** to its initial value, otherwise the flux will ratchet up cycle-by-cycle and saturate the core → catastrophic failure.
- Output inductor freewheels through a freewheeling diode, maintaining load current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Reset is the soul of every isolated forward-family converter.** The mechanism varies:
> - **Forward** → tertiary (reset) winding
> - **Push-Pull** → alternate half-cycle inherently resets the core
> - **Half-Bridge / Full-Bridge** → opposite pair of switches naturally reverses the volt-seconds

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Converter | Output Voltage (CCM, Ideal) | Max Duty $D_{max}$ | Switch Voltage Stress | Switch Current Stress |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forward** | $V_o = D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}$ | **0.5** | $2 V_{in}$ | $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot I_o$ |
| 2 | **Push-Pull** | $V_o = 2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}$ | **< 0.5** | $2 V_{in}$ | $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot I_o$ |
| 3 | **Half-Bridge** | $V_o = D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}$ | **1.0** | $V_{in}$ | $2 \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot I_o$ |
| 4 | **Full-Bridge** | $V_o = 2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}$ | **1.0** | $V_{in}$ | $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot I_o$ |
| 5 | **Flyback (CCM)** | $V_o = \dfrac{D}{1-D} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}$ | **< 1.0** | $V_{in} + \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o$ | $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ |
| 6 | **Flyback (DCM)** | $V_o = V_{in} \cdot D \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{R \, T_s}{2 L_m}} \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | varies | $V_{in} + \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o$ | $\dfrac{D \, T_s \, V_{in}}{L_m}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notation convention used throughout KTU valuation:**
> - $D$ = duty cycle of the active switch pair
> - $N_p$, $N_s$, $N_r$ = primary, secondary, reset (tertiary) turns
> - $L_m$ = magnetizing inductance of the coupled inductor (flyback only)
> - $L_o$ = output filter inductor (forward family)
> - $V_{in}$, $V_o$ = input and output DC voltages

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Forward**: Telecom -48 V bus converters, industrial PLC supplies (50 W – 500 W).
- **Push-Pull**: Vintage UPS systems, low-voltage high-current battery chargers.
- **Half-Bridge**: Microwave ovens, induction heaters, mid-power SMPS (200 W – 1 kW).
- **Full-Bridge**: Server power supplies, EV onboard chargers, solar inverters (1 kW – 50 kW).
- **Flyback**: Mobile phone chargers (5 W – 65 W), LED drivers, standby supplies in TVs and PCs (the dominant topology below 150 W).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Forward Converter — Complete CCM Analysis

**Circuit elements:** Switch S, transformer with primary $N_p$, secondary $N_s$, reset (tertiary) winding $N_r$ with reset diode $D_3$, output diodes $D_1$ (forward) and $D_2$ (freewheeling), output filter inductor $L_o$, capacitor $C_o$, load $R$.

**Step 1: When switch S is ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**

The voltage across the primary winding is $V_{in}$ (dot-end positive).
The induced secondary EMF drives diode $D_1$ into conduction, and $D_2$ is reverse-biased.
The voltage across the output inductor $L_o$ is:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o
$$

The inductor current rises linearly:

$$
\Delta i_{L_o,on} = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o\right) D T_s}{L_o}
$$

**Step 2: When switch S is OFF ($DT_s \le t \le T_s$):**

The primary current tries to continue due to magnetizing inductance, forward-biasing the reset diode $D_3$. The reset winding now applies a voltage across the primary of opposite polarity. By Lenz's law:

$$
v_{reset} = -\dfrac{N_p}{N_r} V_{in}
$$

The output inductor freewheels through $D_2$, so:

$$
v_{L_o} = -V_o
$$

The inductor current falls linearly:

$$
\Delta i_{L_o,off} = \dfrac{V_o (1-D) T_s}{L_o}
$$

**Step 3: Apply volt-second balance on $L_o$:**

In steady state, the net change in inductor current over one switching period must be zero:

$$
\Delta i_{L_o,on} = \Delta i_{L_o,off}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\dfrac{\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o\right) D T_s}{L_o} = \dfrac{V_o (1-D) T_s}{L_o}
$$

Cancelling $T_s / L_o$ from both sides:

$$
\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o\right) D = V_o (1-D)
$$

**Step 4: Solve for the output voltage $V_o$:**

$$
D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - D V_o = V_o - D V_o
$$

$$
D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} = V_o
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the canonical KTU result for the forward converter. Note the strict resemblance to the buck converter — the turns ratio $N_s/N_p$ simply replaces the role of the direct step-down ratio.

**Step 5: Core reset condition (maximum duty cycle):**

For the reset winding, volt-second balance requires the volt-seconds applied during ON to equal the reset volt-seconds during OFF. The reset winding has $N_r$ turns, and the voltage across it during reset is $V_{in}$ (the input directly appears across the reset winding through the conducting $D_3$).

$$
V_{in} \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{N_p}{N_r} V_{in} \cdot (1-D) T_s
$$

Simplifying:

$$
D = \dfrac{N_p}{N_r} (1-D)
$$

$$
D (N_r + N_p) = N_p
$$

$$
D_{max} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_p + N_r}
$$

For the most common design where $N_r = N_p$:

$$
\boxed{D_{max} = 0.5}
$$

### 3.2 Push-Pull Converter — Complete CCM Analysis

**Circuit elements:** Two switches $S_1$, $S_2$, center-tapped primary ($N_{p,1} = N_{p,2} = N_p$), secondary $N_s$, output filter $L_o$, $C_o$, diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$.

**Step 1: When $S_1$ is ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**

Voltage across half primary is $V_{in}$. Dot-end of upper half-primary is positive, so the secondary dot-end is positive → $D_1$ conducts.

Voltage across $L_o$:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o
$$

**Step 2: When $S_2$ is ON ($T_s/2 \le t \le T_s/2 + DT_s$):**

The other half of the primary conducts, but with **opposite polarity** across the secondary. Diode $D_2$ now conducts. The voltage across $L_o$ is the same:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o
$$

**Step 3: When both switches are OFF (gap between pulses):**

Both $D_1$ and $D_2$ are reverse-biased; the output inductor freewheels through a separate freewheeling diode (or through the body diodes, depending on design).

$$
v_{L_o} = -V_o
$$

**Step 4: Volt-second balance on $L_o$:**

Over a full period $T_s$, the ON-time of $L_o$ is $2 D T_s$ (one interval from each switch), and the OFF-time is $(1 - 2D) T_s$:

$$
\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o\right) \cdot 2D T_s = V_o (1 - 2D) T_s
$$

**Step 5: Solve for $V_o$:**

$$
2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - 2D V_o = V_o - 2D V_o
$$

$$
2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} = V_o
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor of **2** comes from the fact that each switch contributes one pulse per period, so the effective ON time is doubled compared to a single-ended forward converter.

### 3.3 Half-Bridge Converter — Complete CCM Analysis

**Circuit elements:** Two switches $S_1$, $S_2$, two equal capacitors $C_1 = C_2$ forming a divider, primary $N_p$, secondary $N_s$, output filter.

**Step 1: Steady-state capacitor voltages:**

By symmetry (assuming ideal switches and balanced load over a full cycle):

$$
V_{C_1} = V_{C_2} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{2}
$$

**Step 2: When $S_1$ is ON, $S_2$ is OFF ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**

The lower terminal of the primary is at $V_{in}/2$ (through $C_2$), and the upper terminal is at $V_{in}$ (through $S_1$). So the voltage across the primary is $V_{in}/2$, with the dot-end positive. Diode $D_1$ conducts:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{V_{in}}{2} - V_o
$$

**Step 3: When $S_2$ is ON, $S_1$ is OFF ($T_s/2 \le t \le T_s/2 + DT_s$):**

The primary voltage is now $-V_{in}/2$ (reversed polarity). $D_2$ conducts, same $L_o$ voltage:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{V_{in}}{2} - V_o
$$

**Step 4: When both switches are OFF:**

The primary floats, the output inductor freewheels:

$$
v_{L_o} = -V_o
$$

**Step 5: Volt-second balance:**

Effective ON time of $L_o$ per period = $2D T_s$:

$$
\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{V_{in}}{2} - V_o\right) \cdot 2D T_s = V_o (1 - 2D) T_s
$$

Simplify:

$$
2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{V_{in}}{2} = V_o
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The half-bridge produces the **same $V_o$ expression as the forward converter**, but with the major advantage that the **switch voltage stress is only $V_{in}$** (not $2V_{in}$). This is why half-bridge is preferred at higher input voltages.

### 3.4 Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) Converter — Complete CCM Analysis

**Circuit elements:** Four switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ in an H-bridge, transformer with $N_p$ and $N_s$, output filter $L_o$, $C_o$.

**Step 1: Diagonal switching — $S_1$ & $S_4$ ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**

Current flows through $S_1$, down through the primary, and back through $S_4$. Full $V_{in}$ appears across the primary with the dot-end positive. $D_1$ conducts:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o
$$

**Step 2: Diagonal switching — $S_2$ & $S_3$ ON ($T_s/2 \le t \le T_s/2 + DT_s$):**

Now $V_{in}$ appears across the primary with the **opposite polarity**. $D_2$ conducts:

$$
v_{L_o} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o
$$

**Step 3: Freewheeling (all switches OFF):**

$$
v_{L_o} = -V_o
$$

**Step 4: Volt-second balance:**

Total ON-time of $L_o$ = $2DT_s$:

$$
\left(\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} V_{in} - V_o\right) \cdot 2D T_s = V_o (1 - 2D) T_s
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = 2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The full-bridge offers the **same gain as the push-pull**, but with switch stress of only $V_{in}$ (not $2V_{in}$). This is the dominant topology for high-power applications because each switch handles half the primary current of an equivalent push-pull.

### 3.5 Flyback Converter — Complete CCM Analysis

**Critical conceptual shift:** In a flyback, the "transformer" is intentionally designed as a **coupled inductor** with significant magnetizing inductance $L_m$ and low leakage. Energy is **stored in $L_m$ during switch ON** and **released to the output during switch OFF**. There is no separate output inductor.

**Step 1: Switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**

Voltage across primary is $V_{in}$ (dot-end positive). The secondary voltage is $-V_{in} \cdot N_s/N_p$ (dot-end negative), reverse-biasing the output diode $D_1$. The primary (magnetizing) current ramps up linearly:

$$
i_{L_m}(t) = i_{L_m}(0) + \dfrac{V_{in}}{L_m} t
$$

Peak magnetizing current at $t = DT_s$:

$$
i_{L_m,peak} = I_{L_m,min} + \dfrac{V_{in} D T_s}{L_m}
$$

**Step 2: Switch OFF ($DT_s \le t \le T_s$):**

The magnetizing current cannot change instantaneously. The voltage across the primary reverses (dot-end goes negative), and now the secondary dot-end goes positive, forward-biasing $D_1$. The secondary current ramps down:

$$
i_{L_m}(t) = i_{L_m,peak} - \dfrac{V_o N_p}{L_m N_s} (t - DT_s)
$$

The voltage across the switch (drain-to-source) is:

$$
V_{DS} = V_{in} + \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o
$$

**Step 3: Apply volt-second balance on $L_m$:**

$$
V_{in} \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o (1-D) T_s
$$

Cancelling $T_s$:

$$
V_{in} D = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o (1-D)
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V_o$:**

$$
\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{D}{1-D} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **buck-boost** gain multiplied by the turns ratio. The flyback is the isolated cousin of the buck-boost. The same equation as $V_o = \dfrac{D}{1-D} V_{in}$ in the non-isolated buck-boost, but with $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ added.

**Step 5: DCM boundary condition:**

The flyback enters Discontinuous Conduction Mode when the magnetizing current falls to zero before the next switching cycle begins. The boundary occurs when:

$$
\boxed{L_{m,crit} = \dfrac{R (1-D)^2}{2 f_s} \cdot \left(\dfrac{N_p}{N_s}\right)^2}
$$

For $L_m < L_{m,crit}$ → **DCM operation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forward Converter — Functional Power Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin[Vin DC Bus] --> sw1[Switch S]
    sw1 --> pri[Primary Np]
    reset[Reset Winding Nr] --> rd1[Reset Diode D3]
    pri -->|dot end| sec[Secondary Ns]
    sec --> fd1[Diode D1]
    fd1 --> lout[Output Inductor Lo]
    lout --> cout[Output Capacitor Co]
    cout --> rout[Load R]
    rout --> gnd1[Ground]
    sec --> fd2[Freewheel Diode D2]
    fd2 --> lout
    pri -.->|magnetic flux| sec
    reset -.->|reset flux path| pri
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** $S$ and the primary winding are in series. The reset winding is wound on the same core with its own diode $D_3$ — when $S$ turns off, the magnetizing current circulates through $N_r$ and $D_3$, applying reverse volt-seconds to reset the flux.

### 4.2 Push-Pull Converter — Symmetric Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    vin[Vin] --> sp1[Switch S1]
    sp1 --> ctp[Center-Tap Primary]
    ctp --> sp2[Switch S2]
    sp2 --> gnd[Ground]
    ctp -->|magnetic coupling| sec[Secondary Ns]
    sec --> ct[Center Tap]
    ct --> gnd2[Ground]
    sec --> d1[Diode D1]
    d1 --> lfilt[Filter Inductor Lo]
    sec --> d2[Diode D2]
    d2 --> lfilt
    lfilt --> cfilt[Filter Capacitor Co]
    cfilt --> load[Load R]
    load --> gnd2
```

### 4.3 Half-Bridge Converter — Capacitive Divider Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin[Vin] --> c1[Capacitor C1]
    vin --> sw1[Switch S1]
    c1 --> sw2[Switch S2]
    c1 -.->|midpoint| pri[Primary Np]
    sw1 --> pri
    sw2 --> gnd[Ground]
    pri -->|magnetic coupling| sec[Secondary Ns]
    sec --> dout1[Diode D1]
    sec --> dout2[Diode D2]
    dout1 --> lout[Output Inductor Lo]
    dout2 --> lout
    lout --> cout[Output Capacitor Co]
    cout --> rload[Load R]
    rload --> gnd2[Return Ground]
```

### 4.4 Full-Bridge Converter — H-Bridge Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    vin[Vin DC Bus] --> swA[Switch S1]
    vin --> swB[Switch S3]
    swA --> priTop[Primary Top Node]
    swB --> priBot[Primary Bottom Node]
    priTop --> swC[Switch S2]
    swB --> swD[Switch S4]
    priTop --> priCore[Primary Winding Np]
    priCore --> priBot
    swC --> gnd[Ground]
    swD --> gnd
    priCore -->|magnetic coupling| secCore[Secondary Ns]
    secCore --> d1[Diode D1]
    secCore --> d2[Diode D2]
    d1 --> lo[Output Inductor Lo]
    d2 --> lo
    lo --> co[Output Capacitor Co]
    co --> rload[Load R]
    rload --> gnd2[Output Return]
```

### 4.5 Flyback Converter — Energy Storage Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin[Vin DC Bus] --> swfly[Switch S]
    swfly --> priFly[Primary Np Coupled Inductor]
    priFly --> gnd[Input Ground]
    priFly -->|energy stored in Lm during ON| coreFlux[Core Flux phi]
    coreFlux --> secFly[Secondary Ns]
    secFly --> doutFly[Output Diode D1]
    doutFly --> coutFly[Output Capacitor Co]
    secFly --> gndOut[Output Ground]
    coutFly --> rloadFly[Load R]
    rloadFly --> gndOut
    swfly -.->|Vds stress| vds[Vds = Vin + Np slash Ns times Vo]
```

### 4.6 Operating Waveform Block Diagram (Forward Converter)

```mermaid
graph LR
    gateS[Vgs of Switch] -->|DTs ON| ton[Power Transfer Phase]
    gateS -->|1 minus D Ts OFF| toff[Core Reset Phase]
    ton --> fluxUp[Flux phi rises linearly]
    toff --> fluxDown[Flux phi falls to reset]
    fluxUp --> voPos[Vo is positive at output]
    toff --> voFree[Vo is sustained by Lo freewheeling]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. What is the function of the tertiary (reset) winding in a forward converter? Why is it mandatory?**

**Model Answer:**
The tertiary (reset) winding, wound on the same core as the primary with a separate diode $D_3$, provides a path for the magnetizing current to circulate after the main switch turns off. Its function is to apply **reverse volt-seconds** to the primary, driving the core flux $\phi$ back to its initial value before the next switching cycle begins. Without this reset mechanism, the flux would ratchet up cycle-by-cycle, eventually saturating the magnetic core and causing catastrophic switch failure. The reset winding is therefore mandatory for **flux walk-out prevention** in any single-ended forward converter.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Compare the maximum switch voltage stress of the push-pull and full-bridge converters. Why does this difference exist?**

**Model Answer:**
In a **push-pull** converter, the switch must block **$2V_{in}$** because when one switch is ON and the other is OFF, the OFF switch sees $V_{in}$ at its terminal plus the reflected $V_{in}$ from the conducting half-primary on the other side of the center tap — totaling $2V_{in}$. In a **full-bridge** converter, the maximum voltage across any switch is only **$V_{in}$**, since the bridge configuration always presents a maximum of $V_{in}$ across any single device. The full-bridge is therefore preferred for **high-input-voltage** applications (e.g., 400 V DC bus) where $2V_{in}$ would exceed the breakdown rating of available MOSFETs or require expensive high-voltage devices.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Forward + Flyback Comparison

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **forward converter** in CCM. Derive the expression for the output voltage and explain the function of the reset winding. State the condition for maximum duty cycle.

**(b)** [7 Marks] With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **flyback converter** in CCM. Derive the expression for output voltage and the switch voltage stress.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Forward Converter:**

**Circuit diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block).**

**Operation — Switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**
- Switch S is gated ON; voltage $V_{in}$ appears across primary $N_p$ (dot-end positive).
- Secondary $N_s$ has voltage $V_{in} \cdot N_s/N_p$; diode $D_1$ conducts, $D_2$ blocks.
- Output inductor $L_o$ charges with $v_{L_o} = (N_s/N_p) V_{in} - V_o$.
- Magnetizing current $i_{L_m}$ rises linearly: $\frac{di_{L_m}}{dt} = V_{in}/L_m$.

**Operation — Switch OFF ($DT_s \le t \le T_s$):**
- Switch S opens; primary current cannot stop instantly.
- Magnetizing current forward-biases reset diode $D_3$; reset winding $N_r$ applies reverse voltage across primary.
- Flux in the core is reset to its starting value within $(1-D)T_s$.
- Output inductor $L_o$ freewheels through diode $D_2$.

**Derivation (recall from Section 3.1):**
Applying volt-second balance on $L_o$:

$$
\boxed{V_o = D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

**Reset winding condition (also from Section 3.1):**

$$
\boxed{D_{max} = \dfrac{N_p}{N_p + N_r}}
$$

For $N_r = N_p$: $D_{max} = 0.5$.

---

**Part (b) — Flyback Converter:**

**Circuit diagram (refer to Section 4.5 Mermaid block).**

**Operation — Switch ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**
- Switch S is ON; voltage $V_{in}$ across primary $N_p$.
- Secondary voltage is $-V_{in} \cdot N_s/N_p$ (dot-end negative); output diode $D_1$ is reverse-biased.
- Magnetizing inductance $L_m$ stores energy: $W = \frac{1}{2} L_m I_{L_m,peak}^2$.
- Magnetizing current rises linearly: $I_{L_m,peak} = I_{L_m,min} + \frac{V_{in} D T_s}{L_m}$.

**Operation — Switch OFF ($DT_s \le t \le T_s$):**
- Switch S opens; primary voltage reverses polarity.
- Secondary voltage becomes $V_o = (N_s/N_p) \cdot v_{primary}$.
- Output diode $D_1$ conducts; stored magnetic energy is delivered to the load and output capacitor.
- Magnetizing current falls linearly.

**Derivation:**
Volt-second balance on $L_m$ (recall from Section 3.5):

$$
V_{in} D = \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o (1-D)
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot \dfrac{D}{1-D} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

**Switch voltage stress:**
When the switch is OFF, the maximum drain-to-source voltage equals the input plus the reflected secondary voltage:

$$
\boxed{V_{DS,max} = V_{in} + \dfrac{N_p}{N_s} V_o}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reset winding analysis** in the forward converter is the single most common reason for losing 2–3 marks in Part (a). Always state $D_{max} = N_p/(N_p+N_r)$ explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the flyback with a forward converter**: students often draw a secondary-side inductor $L_o$ in the flyback. There is **no** $L_o$ — the magnetizing inductance $L_m$ of the coupled inductor IS the energy storage element. This mistake will cost full marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in switch voltage stress** of the flyback: it is $V_{in} + (N_p/N_s) V_o$, NOT $V_{in} - (N_p/N_s) V_o$. The transformer dot convention dictates additive polarity during switch OFF.

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Half-Bridge + Full-Bridge Comparison

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the operation of a **half-bridge converter** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the output voltage expression and state the switch voltage stress.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the operation of a **full-bridge converter** with a neat circuit diagram. Compare it with the push-pull converter in terms of switch voltage stress, transformer utilization, and typical power range.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Half-Bridge Converter:**

**Circuit (refer to Section 4.3).** Two switches $S_1, S_2$ and two equal capacitors $C_1 = C_2$ across the DC bus form the primary-side network.

**Operation — $S_1$ ON, $S_2$ OFF ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**
- Voltage across primary = $V_{in} - V_{C_2} = V_{in}/2$.
- Dot-end of primary is positive; secondary diode $D_1$ conducts.
- $v_{L_o} = (N_s/N_p)(V_{in}/2) - V_o$.

**Operation — $S_2$ ON, $S_1$ OFF ($T_s/2 \le t \le T_s/2 + DT_s$):**
- Voltage across primary = $-V_{in}/2$ (reversed).
- $D_2$ conducts; $v_{L_o} = (N_s/N_p)(V_{in}/2) - V_o$.

**Operation — Both OFF:** $v_{L_o} = -V_o$ (freewheeling).

**Derivation (recall from Section 3.3):**

$$
\boxed{V_o = D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

**Switch voltage stress** = $\boxed{V_{in}}$ (each switch blocks the full bus voltage when off).

---

**Part (b) — Full-Bridge Converter:**

**Circuit (refer to Section 4.4).** Four switches in H-bridge configuration.

**Operation — $S_1, S_4$ ON ($0 \le t \le DT_s$):**
- Full $V_{in}$ across primary; $D_1$ conducts.

**Operation — $S_2, S_3$ ON ($T_s/2 \le t \le T_s/2 + DT_s$):**
- $-V_{in}$ across primary; $D_2$ conducts.

**Operation — Freewheeling:** $v_{L_o} = -V_o$.

**Derivation (recall from Section 3.4):**

$$
\boxed{V_o = 2D \cdot \dfrac{N_s}{N_p} \cdot V_{in}}
$$

**Switch voltage stress** = $\boxed{V_{in}}$.

---

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Push-Pull | Full-Bridge |
|---|---|---|
| Switch voltage stress | $2V_{in}$ | $V_{in}$ |
| Number of switches | 2 | 4 |
| Primary current per switch | $2 \cdot (N_s/N_p) I_o$ | $(N_s/N_p) I_o$ |
| Transformer core utilization | Full B-H swing (no DC bias) | Full B-H swing (no DC bias) |
| Typical power range | 100 W – 1 kW | 500 W – 50 kW |
| Snubber complexity | High (leakage energy × $2V_{in}$) | Moderate (leakage energy × $V_{in}$) |
| Drive complexity | Simple (2 isolated drives) | Complex (4 floating drives) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting capacitor midpoint voltage of $V_{in}/2$** in the half-bridge: the primary voltage is NOT $V_{in}$ — it is **$V_{in}/2$**. This is the most common algebraic error.
> 2. **Confusing switch count and current rating in the push-pull vs full-bridge comparison**: students often claim push-pull uses "less current" — in fact, each push-pull switch carries **twice** the primary current of a full-bridge switch for the same output power.
> 3. **Not stating the duty cycle symmetry explicitly**: in both half-bridge and full-bridge, $D$ is defined as the ON-time of one diagonal pair divided by half the switching period. A full-cycle ON-time would be $2DT_s$. Examiners explicitly look for this clarity.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ All isolated converters use a **high-frequency transformer** for galvanic isolation and voltage scaling.
- ✅ **Forward** converter: $V_o = D \cdot (N_s/N_p) \cdot V_{in}$; $D_{max} = 0.5$ (with $N_r = N_p$); switch stress = $2V_{in}$.
- ✅ **Push-Pull** converter: $V_o = 2D \cdot (N_s/N_p) \cdot V_{in}$; $D_{max} < 0.5$; switch stress = $2V_{in}$; vulnerable to flux imbalance.
- ✅ **Half-Bridge** converter: $V_o = D \cdot (N_s/N_p) \cdot V_{in}$; switch stress = $V_{in}$; needs $C_1 = C_2$ for voltage symmetry.
- ✅ **Full-Bridge** converter: $V_o = 2D \cdot (N_s/N_p) \cdot V_{in}$; switch stress = $V_{in}$; highest power capability.
- ✅ **Flyback** converter: $V_o = (N_s/N_p) \cdot [D/(1-D)] \cdot V_{in}$; operates as a coupled-inductor buck-boost; no separate output inductor.
- ✅ **Switch voltage stress in flyback**: $V_{DS,max} = V_{in} + (N_p/N_s) V_o$ — this is critical for MOSFET/IGBT selection.
- ✅ **Core reset** is mandatory in single-ended forward converter via reset winding; bridge topologies reset inherently by polarity reversal.
- ✅ **Transformer utilization**: Bridge family uses full B-H swing; push-pull does too; forward uses only first quadrant (smaller core for same power).
- ✅ **CCM assumption** in all derivations: volt-second balance on the output filter inductor (or $L_m$ for flyback).
- ✅ **DCM operation** is possible in flyback when $L_m < L_{m,crit} = R(1-D)^2/[2f_s] \cdot (N_p/N_s)^2$.
- ✅ **Power range rule of thumb**: Flyback < 150 W, Forward 50–500 W, Half-Bridge 200 W–1 kW, Full-Bridge 1 kW–50 kW.
- ✅ **KTU exam focus areas**: derivations of $V_o$ expressions, switch voltage stress calculation, comparison tables, neat circuit diagrams with dot-end convention clearly marked.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, the **circuit diagram carries 2 marks** out of 7 in each sub-part. Always draw a **clean, labeled** circuit with **dot conventions** marked, **switch symbols, diode symbols with correct polarity**, and the **output filter** clearly shown. A missing dot convention alone can cost a mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switched Mode Power Supply
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switched Mode Power Supply

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Switched Mode Power Supply (SMPS)** is an electronic power converter that employs a solid-state switching element (typically a **MOSFET**, **IGBT**, or **BJT**) operating in either the fully-ON or fully-OFF state to regulate the output voltage or current. Unlike linear regulators that dissipate excess power as heat, an SMPS transfers energy in discrete packets by storing it temporarily in magnetic (inductors, transformers) or electric (capacitors) field components and then releasing it at a different voltage level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** SMPS is defined as a high-frequency DC-to-DC or AC-to-DC conversion system in which the regulating device (transistor switch) operates as a switch (cut-off/saturation), achieving power conversion efficiencies typically in the range of **70% to 95%**.

### 1.2 Classification of SMPS by Power Flow Direction

| Category | Input | Output | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AC-to-DC SMPS** | AC Mains (230V/50Hz) | Regulated DC | Laptop chargers, Mobile adapters, PC SMPS |
| **DC-to-DC SMPS** | Unregulated DC | Regulated DC | Battery-powered devices, EV systems |
| **DC-to-AC SMPS** | DC source | AC waveform | Solar inverters, UPS systems |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for First-Time Learners:** Imagine you want to fill a bucket (output capacitor) with water at a steady, controlled level from a high-pressure pipeline (input source).

- **Linear Regulator Analogy:** You attach a friction-clamp valve halfway open. The valve constantly fights the water pressure, generating heat (wasted energy) while throttling the flow. This is exactly how a **linear regulator** (e.g., 7805) drops 12V to 5V — by dissipating the difference as heat: $P_{loss} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{load}$.
- **SMPS Analogy:** Instead of a friction valve, you use a **fast on/off solenoid valve** controlled by a smart sensor. If the bucket is too low, the valve opens for a longer time; if the bucket is full, the valve closes. Because the valve is either fully open (zero pressure drop, no heat) or fully closed (no flow, no heat), almost **no energy is wasted as heat**. This is the essence of **switching regulation**.

### 1.4 Why SMPS Dominates Modern Electronics

| Parameter | Linear Regulator | SMPS |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | **30% – 60%** | **70% – 95%** |
| Heat Dissipation | High (requires large heatsinks) | Very low |
| Weight & Size | Bulky (50Hz transformer) | Compact (high-frequency ferrite core) |
| Line Regulation | Poor | Excellent |
| Load Regulation | Good | Excellent |
| EMI/RFI | Negligible | High (mitigation needed) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) Strategy

The heart of every SMPS is a **closed-loop feedback controller** that modulates the duty cycle of the power switch. The duty cycle is mathematically defined as:

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_s} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}
$$

Where:
- $T_s$ = Total switching period (typically **20 µs to 2 µs**, corresponding to frequencies of **50 kHz to 500 kHz**)
- $D$ = Duty cycle (dimensionless, $0 < D < 1$)
- $1-D$ = Complementary duty cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why High Frequency?** Because the size of the magnetic energy storage element (inductor/transformer) is inversely proportional to frequency: $L \propto \frac{1}{f_s}$. Operating at 100 kHz instead of 50 Hz reduces transformer size by a factor of **2000×**, which is why modern SMPS adapters fit in the palm of your hand.

### 2.2 The Three Fundamental Non-Isolated DC-DC Topologies

These are the **building blocks** for every modern power supply and are heavily tested in KTU exams.

#### (A) Buck Converter (Step-Down)
**Function:** Produces $V_o < V_s$. Power flows directly from source to load via the switch and inductor.

**Operating Phases:**
- **Phase 1 — Switch ON (Duration = $DT_s$):** Inductor stores energy. Diode is reverse-biased. Current through inductor ramps UP linearly.
- **Phase 2 — Switch OFF (Duration = $(1-D)T_s$):** Inductor releases stored energy to the load through the freewheeling diode. Current ramps DOWN.

#### (B) Boost Converter (Step-Up)
**Function:** Produces $V_o > V_s$. Achieves voltage elevation using the inductor's back-EMF during switch turn-off.

**Operating Phases:**
- **Phase 1 — Switch ON (Duration = $DT_s$):** Input source stores energy in the inductor. Diode is reverse-biased. Load is supported by the output capacitor alone.
- **Phase 2 — Switch OFF (Duration = $(1-D)T_s$):** Inductor voltage adds in series with the source, pushing current through the diode into the load and capacitor. Voltage rises above $V_s$.

#### (C) Buck-Boost Converter (Step-Up / Step-Down)
**Function:** Produces $|V_o|$ either above or below $V_s$, with **inverted polarity**. Uses the inductor as the only energy transfer element.

### 2.3 Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) vs Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Concept for KTU:** Whether the inductor current ever touches zero defines the operational mode.

- **CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode):** Inductor current is always positive, never reaches zero. Standard analysis applies.
- **DCM (Discontinuous Conduction Mode):** Inductor current falls to zero and remains there for a portion of the cycle. Occurs at light loads. The voltage transfer ratio becomes a **function of load current**, complicating the small-signal model.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every formula you need to solve any SMPS problem in the KTU End Semester Examination. Memorize the **bold** relationships first.

| Parameter | Buck Converter | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage Transfer Ratio (CCM)** | $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D$ | $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}$ | $\frac{V_o}{V_s} = -\frac{D}{1-D}$ |
| Switch ON Inductor Voltage | $V_L = V_s - V_o$ | $V_L = V_s$ | $V_L = V_s$ |
| Switch OFF Inductor Voltage | $V_L = -V_o$ | $V_L = V_s - V_o$ | $V_L = V_o$ |
| Average Inductor Current $I_L$ | $\frac{V_o}{R(1-D)}$ for boost/buck-boost ; $\frac{V_o}{R}$ for buck | — | — |
| Inductor Current Ripple $\Delta I_L$ | $\frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L}$ | $\frac{V_s \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L}$ | $\frac{V_s \cdot D}{f_s \cdot L}$ |
| Critical Inductance $L_{crit}$ | $\frac{(1-D) \cdot R}{2 f_s}$ | $\frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ | $\frac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}$ |
| Output Capacitor Ripple $\Delta V_o$ | $\frac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_s C}$ | $\frac{I_o \cdot D}{f_s C}$ | $\frac{I_o \cdot D}{f_s C}$ |
| Switch Voltage Stress $V_{sw,max}$ | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + V_o$ |
| Diode Voltage Stress $V_D,max$ | $V_s$ | $V_o$ | $V_s + V_o$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation:** In all KTU exam answers, $f_s$ denotes the **switching frequency** in Hertz, NOT the line frequency (50 Hz). The line frequency only matters in the input rectifier stage.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Buck:** Point-of-Load (POL) converters in microprocessors (1V @ 100A from 12V rail).
- **Boost:** Power Factor Correction (PFC) front-end stages, solar micro-inverters, LED drivers.
- **Buck-Boost:** Battery-powered systems where the input voltage (e.g., Li-ion: 3.0V–4.2V) straddles the required output.
- **Flyback / Forward (Isolated):** Mobile phone chargers (5V/2A from 230V AC mains), TV standby supplies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Buck Converter Voltage Transfer Ratio

The derivation relies on the **Inductor Volt-Second Balance Principle** — in steady state, the average voltage across an inductor over one switching period must equal zero (otherwise the inductor current would ramp to infinity).

**Step 1: Define the two switching intervals.**

During **Phase 1** (switch ON, duration $DT_s$), Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop containing $V_s$, switch, inductor, and load gives:

$$
V_L^{ON} = V_s - V_o
$$

During **Phase 2** (switch OFF, duration $(1-D)T_s$), the inductor freewheels through the diode. The only voltage across the inductor is the negative of the output voltage:

$$
V_L^{OFF} = -V_o
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Volt-Second Balance Equation.**

The integral of inductor voltage over one period equals zero in steady state:

$$
\int_{0}^{T_s} V_L(t) \, dt = 0
$$

Expanding using the two piecewise intervals:

$$
V_L^{ON} \cdot (DT_s) + V_L^{OFF} \cdot (1-D)T_s = 0
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the piecewise voltages.**

$$
(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s + (-V_o) \cdot (1-D) \cdot T_s = 0
$$

**Step 4: Cancel the common factor $T_s$ (non-zero).**

$$
D(V_s - V_o) - V_o(1-D) = 0
$$

**Step 5: Expand and collect terms.**

$$
D \cdot V_s - D \cdot V_o - V_o + D \cdot V_o = 0
$$

The $-D \cdot V_o$ and $+D \cdot V_o$ terms cancel:

$$
D \cdot V_s - V_o = 0
$$

**Step 6: Solve for the transfer ratio.**

$$
V_o = D \cdot V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_o}{V_s} = D
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Since $0 \leq D \leq 1$, the output voltage is always less than or equal to the input — hence the name "step-down" or "buck."

### 3.2 Derivation of the Boost Converter Voltage Transfer Ratio

**Step 1: Phase 1 — Switch ON (Duration $DT_s$).**

When the switch is closed, the inductor is directly connected across the input source. The diode is reverse-biased and effectively disconnected from the circuit. Therefore:

$$
V_L^{ON} = V_s
$$

**Step 2: Phase 2 — Switch OFF (Duration $(1-D)T_s$).**

When the switch opens, the inductor opposes the change in current by inducing a voltage with polarity that forward-biases the diode. Now the inductor, source, and output node are all in series. Applying KVL:

$$
V_s - V_L^{OFF} - V_o = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_L^{OFF} = V_s - V_o
$$

**Step 3: Apply Volt-Second Balance.**

$$
V_s \cdot (D T_s) + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0
$$

**Step 4: Cancel $T_s$ and expand.**

$$
D V_s + (1-D) V_s - (1-D) V_o = 0
$$

$$
V_s [D + 1 - D] - (1-D) V_o = 0
$$

$$
V_s - (1-D) V_o = 0
$$

**Step 5: Solve for the transfer ratio.**

$$
V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{1}{1-D}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** When $D = 0$, $V_o = V_s$ (switch always off, output equals input through the inductor and diode). When $D \to 1$, $V_o \to \infty$ (theoretically — in practice, parasitic resistances limit this). This confirms the step-up behavior.

### 3.3 Derivation of Inductor Current Ripple (Buck Example)

The ripple is the peak-to-peak variation in inductor current during one switching cycle.

**Step 1: Write the inductor voltage-current relationship.**

For an inductor: $V_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt}$. Rearranging: $di_L = \frac{V_L}{L} dt$.

**Step 2: Apply during the ON interval (where $V_L = V_s - V_o$).**

The current rise during the ON time $DT_s$ equals the peak-to-peak ripple:

$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_L^{ON}}{L} \cdot (DT_s) = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D}{L f_s}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V_o = DV_s$ (from the transfer ratio).**

$$
V_s - V_o = V_s - D V_s = V_s (1-D)
$$

**Step 4: Final ripple expression.**

$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s (1-D) D}{L f_s}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum ripple** occurs at $D = 0.5$ for the buck converter. Designers typically choose $L$ such that $\Delta I_L$ is between **20% and 40%** of the average inductor current to balance size, losses, and current stress on the switch.

### 3.4 Derivation of Critical Inductance (Boundary between CCM and DCM)

At the CCM/DCM boundary, the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF interval. This means the peak current equals half the ripple:

$$
I_{L,peak} = \frac{\Delta I_L}{2}
$$

And the average inductor current (which equals load current for a buck converter) is:

$$
I_L = I_o = \frac{V_o}{R}
$$

Setting $\frac{\Delta I_L}{2} = I_o$:

$$
\frac{V_o (1-D)}{2 L_{crit} f_s} = \frac{V_o}{R}
$$

Solving for $L_{crit}$:

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget the factor of **2** in the denominator. Always verify by checking units: $L$ has units of Henrys, and $\frac{R}{f_s}$ has units of $\frac{\Omega}{\text{Hz}} = \text{H}$ — correct.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete SMPS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AC Mains Input 230V 50Hz] --> B[Input EMI Filter]
    B --> C[Bridge Rectifier and PFC Stage]
    C --> D[High Voltage DC Bus approximately 400V]
    D --> E[High Frequency Inverter Switching at 50 to 500 kHz]
    E --> F[High Frequency Transformer Isolation and Voltage Scaling]
    F --> G[High Frequency Rectifier Output Stage]
    G --> H[Output LC Filter]
    H --> I[Feedback and PWM Control IC]
    I -.->|PWM Drive Signal| E
    H -->|Sensed Vo| I
```

### 4.2 Topology Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Choose SMPS Topology] --> Q1{Input Output Isolation Required?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Isolated[Flyback for less than 150W, Forward or Half Bridge for more than 150W]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Desired Output vs Input?}
    Q2 -->|Vo less than Vs| Buck[Buck Converter Step Down]
    Q2 -->|Vo greater than Vs| Boost[Boost Converter Step Up]
    Q2 -->|Vo either side, Inverted| BB[Buck Boost Converter]
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Reference Voltage Vref] --> SumSummingJunction
    Vo[Output Voltage Vo] --> SumSummingJunction
    SumSummingJunction --> EA[Error Amplifier]
    EA --> Comp[Compensator Network PI or Type III]
    Comp --> PWM[PWM Modulator Carrier Triangle Wave]
    PWM --> Gate[Gate Driver]
    Gate --> Switch[Power MOSFET Switch]
    Switch --> LC[LC Power Stage]
    LC --> Vo
```

### 4.4 Energy Flow Comparison — Linear vs SMPS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LinearReg[Linear Regulator 30 to 50 percent efficiency]
        A1[Input Voltage 12V] --> A2[Pass Transistor operating in Active Region]
        A2 --> A3[Output 5V]
        A2 -.Heat Loss 7V times I.-> A4[Heat Sink required]
    end
    subgraph SMPSReg[SMPS Buck 90 percent plus efficiency]
        B1[Input Voltage 12V] --> B2[Switch MOSFET fully ON or fully OFF]
        B2 --> B3[Inductor and Capacitor Energy Transfer]
        B3 --> B4[Output 5V]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagrams are directly adapted to the standard textbook presentations of **Erickson's Fundamentals of Power Electronics** and **Hart's Power Electronics**, which form the reference framework for KTU PCAET503 valuation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Switched Mode Power Supply. List any two advantages of SMPS over linear power supply.**

**Model Answer:**

A Switched Mode Power Supply (SMPS) is a high-frequency electronic power converter that uses semiconductor switching devices (operating in cut-off or saturation regions) in conjunction with energy storage elements (inductors and capacitors) to convert one voltage level to another with high efficiency.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **High Efficiency (70%–95%):** Switching devices dissipate minimal power because they are either fully ON (low $V_{DS}$, high current) or fully OFF (high $V_{DS}$, zero current) — hence low switching losses compared to linear regulators operating in the active region.
2. **Compact Size & Light Weight:** High switching frequency (typically 50 kHz–500 kHz) dramatically reduces the size of magnetic components (transformer, inductor), enabling palm-sized adapters.

*(Word limit target: 80–100 words for 3 marks)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by duty cycle in an SMPS? Why is the switching frequency kept high?**

**Model Answer:**

The duty cycle $D$ is the ratio of the switch ON-time to the total switching period:

$$
D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_s} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}}
$$

It is the primary control variable in an SMPS; varying $D$ changes the output voltage according to the topology-specific transfer ratio.

**Why High Switching Frequency?**
The size of the energy storage elements (inductors and transformers) is inversely proportional to frequency. For example, a transformer core area scales as $A_c \propto \frac{1}{f_s}$. Increasing $f_s$ from 50 Hz to 100 kHz reduces transformer size by a factor of 2000, resulting in lighter, cheaper, and more compact power supplies. Additionally, higher frequency allows faster transient response to load changes.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In derivations, marks are awarded for **(i) correct circuit phase analysis**, **(ii) proper application of volt-second balance**, and **(iii) algebraic manipulation**. Skipping any of these three steps will cost you at least **3–4 marks** even if the final answer is correct. Always show ALL intermediate steps.

---

#### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Boost converter in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM). Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of input voltage and duty cycle.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**
A boost converter consists of:
- Input DC source $V_s$
- Boost inductor $L$ in series with the source
- Power switch (MOSFET $S$) connected between the inductor-capacitor node and ground
- Diode $D$ in series with the parallel combination of output capacitor $C$ and load resistor $R$

**Phase 1 — Switch ON (Duration $DT_s$):**
The MOSFET is closed. The diode is reverse-biased (since the input voltage plus the inductor voltage cannot exceed the output). Current builds up in the inductor. The output capacitor supplies the load current, discharging slightly. Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_s$.

**Phase 2 — Switch OFF (Duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
The MOSFET opens. The inductor resists the sudden change in current by reversing its voltage polarity. Now $V_s$ and $V_L$ add in series, forward-biasing the diode and pushing current into the load and capacitor. Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative value since $V_o > V_s$).

**Derivation (Volt-Second Balance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s \cdot (DT_s) + (V_s - V_o)(1-D)T_s &= 0 \\[6pt]
\text{Dividing by } T_s: \quad D V_s + (1-D)V_s - (1-D)V_o &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s [D + 1 - D] - (1-D)V_o &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s - (1-D)V_o &= 0 \\[6pt]
\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_s}{1-D}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) A boost converter has an input voltage of 12 V and is required to deliver an output of 24 V to a resistive load of 20 Ω. The switching frequency is 50 kHz. Calculate (i) the duty cycle, (ii) the average inductor current, and (iii) the critical value of inductance to maintain CCM.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_s = 12$ V, $V_o = 24$ V, $R = 20$ Ω, $f_s = 50$ kHz $= 50 \times 10^3$ Hz.

**(i) Duty Cycle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{V_o}{V_s} &= \frac{1}{1-D} \\[6pt]
\frac{24}{12} &= \frac{1}{1-D} \\[6pt]
2 &= \frac{1}{1-D} \\[6pt]
1 - D &= 0.5 \\[6pt]
\boxed{D = 0.5 \quad \text{or} \quad 50\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Average Inductor Current:**

The load current: $I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{24}{20} = 1.2$ A.

For a boost converter in CCM, the average inductor current relates to load current by: $I_L = \frac{I_o}{1-D}$.

$$
I_L = \frac{1.2}{1 - 0.5} = \frac{1.2}{0.5} = \boxed{2.4 \text{ A}}
$$

**(iii) Critical Inductance:**

The critical inductance for a boost converter is:

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_s}
$$

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{0.5 \times (0.5)^2 \times 20}{2 \times 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.25 \times 20}{100{,}000}
$$

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{2.5}{100{,}000} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ H} = \boxed{25 \text{ μH}}
$$

To maintain CCM, the actual inductance must be **greater than 25 μH**. A designer would typically select $L = 1.2 \times L_{crit} \approx 30$ μH to provide margin.

---

#### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram of a Buck converter. Explain its operation in Continuous Conduction Mode. Derive the output voltage expression.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** The buck converter consists of a DC source $V_s$, a controlled switch (MOSFET) in series, a freewheeling diode connected from the switch node to ground (cathode at the switch node, anode at ground), an inductor $L$ in series with the load, and an output capacitor $C$ in parallel with the load resistance $R$.

**Phase 1 — Switch ON (Duration $DT_s$):**
- The MOSFET conducts, connecting the source to the inductor.
- Diode is reverse-biased (cathode is at positive potential).
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = V_s - V_o > 0$ → current ramps UP.
- Inductor stores energy; capacitor charges the load.

**Phase 2 — Switch OFF (Duration $(1-D)T_s$):**
- MOSFET opens; current path through the source is broken.
- Inductor current cannot change instantaneously, so it forward-biases the diode (freewheeling action).
- Inductor voltage: $V_L = -V_o < 0$ → current ramps DOWN.
- Energy previously stored in the inductor now flows to the load.

**Derivation (Volt-Second Balance):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(V_s - V_o)(DT_s) + (-V_o)(1-D)T_s &= 0 \\[6pt]
V_s D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D &= 0 \\[6pt]
D V_s - V_o &= 0 \\[6pt]
\boxed{V_o = D V_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) A buck converter supplies a 5 V output from a 15 V DC source. The load resistance is 10 Ω and the switching frequency is 25 kHz. Determine (i) the duty cycle, (ii) the average output current, (iii) the inductor ripple current, and (iv) the critical inductance.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_s = 15$ V, $V_o = 5$ V, $R = 10$ Ω, $f_s = 25 \text{ kHz} = 25{,}000$ Hz.

**(i) Duty Cycle:**

$$
D = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{5}{15} = \boxed{0.333 \text{ (33.3\%)}}
$$

**(ii) Average Output Current:**

$$
I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{5}{10} = \boxed{0.5 \text{ A}}
$$

**(iii) Inductor Ripple Current:**

$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_s (1-D) D}{L f_s}
$$

Without a specified $L$, we use the ripple formula in terms of known quantities assuming we calculate relative to $L_{crit}$ later. Alternatively, the ripple can be expressed relative to $L$:

$$
\Delta I_L = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{f_s L} \quad \text{(using } V_s - V_o = V_o(1-D)/D\text{)}
$$

*(If specific $L$ is given, substitute directly. KTU typically provides $L$ or asks for the formula.)*

For the maximum possible ripple (no inductor specified), express the **dimensionless ripple fraction**:

$$
\frac{\Delta I_L}{I_o} = \frac{(1-D)}{f_s L / R} = \frac{0.667 \times 10}{25{,}000 \times L}
$$

*(The standard KTU approach: assume $L = L_{crit}$ for finding ripple at boundary.)*

**(iv) Critical Inductance:**

$$
L_{crit} = \frac{(1-D) R}{2 f_s} = \frac{(0.667) \times 10}{2 \times 25{,}000} = \frac{6.67}{50{,}000} = \boxed{133.4 \text{ μH}}
$$

To ensure CCM, choose $L > 133.4$ μH.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for SMPS — Print This Before Exam!**

- ✅ **SMPS = High-frequency switching regulator.** Efficiency = 70%–95%. Linear = 30%–60%.
- ✅ **Duty Cycle $D = T_{ON}/T_s$.** Control variable of every SMPS. Range: $0 < D < 1$.
- ✅ **Three Golden Transfer Ratios (MEMORIZE):**
  - **Buck:** $V_o/V_s = D$
  - **Boost:** $V_o/V_s = \frac{1}{1-D}$
  - **Buck-Boost:** $V_o/V_s = -\frac{D}{1-D}$ (note the negative sign → inverted polarity!)
- ✅ **Volt-Second Balance is the KEY derivation tool.** Always write the two-phase inductor voltages, then multiply by their respective duty durations, and sum to zero.
- ✅ **Switching frequency $f_s$ is in the range 50 kHz–500 kHz**, NOT 50 Hz. The 50 Hz is only the AC mains frequency.
- ✅ **Critical Inductance** $L_{crit}$ defines the CCM/DCM boundary. Always design with $L > L_{crit}$ (typically 20% margin).
- ✅ **Output Voltage Ripple** $\Delta V_o$ depends on capacitor size, switching frequency, and inductor ripple current.
- ✅ **In CCM**, average inductor current ≠ output current for boost and buck-boost (they differ by $1/(1-D)$).
- ✅ **In CCM**, average inductor current = output current for buck converter.
- ✅ **Isolated SMPS topologies** (Flyback, Forward) are used when galvanic isolation between mains and load is required (safety).
- ✅ **Advantages over linear:** efficiency, size, weight. **Disadvantages:** EMI/RFI, complex control, output ripple.
- ✅ **Typical exam marks split:** 3 marks (definition) + 4 marks (derivation with phases) + 7 marks (numerical problem with multiple sub-parts).
- ✅ **Always include units in your final answer.** Writing "$L = 25 \times 10^{-6}$" instead of "$L = 25$ μH" loses you 1 mark on KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC-AC Switch Mode Inverter - Inverter Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** DC-AC Switch Mode Inverter - Inverter Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **DC-AC Switch Mode Inverter** is a power electronic converter that converts a **DC input voltage source** into a **symmetrical AC output voltage** of controllable magnitude and frequency using fully controllable semiconductor switches (such as **IGBTs**, **MOSFETs**, or **GTO thyristors**) operating in **switched (non-linear) mode**. The term *switch mode* emphasizes that the switches operate in either fully ON (saturation) or fully OFF (cut-off) states, minimizing power loss and maximizing efficiency. The output waveform, depending on the switching strategy, may be a **square wave**, **quasi-square wave**, or a **PWM (Pulse Width Modulated) wave**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Inverter topologies covered under Module 3 include the **single-phase half-bridge**, **single-phase full-bridge (H-bridge)**, **push-pull**, and **three-phase bridge** configurations. Each topology has distinct conduction modes, device ratings, and harmonic signatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Quadrant Note:** A basic inverter operates in **Quadrant 1 and 4** of the V-I plane (DC to AC power flow). A *bi-directional* or *regenerative* inverter extends this to all four quadrants, permitting reverse power flow from AC to DC.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap with two pipes**:
- One pipe pushes water **forward** (positive half-cycle of AC)
- Another pipe pushes water **backward** (negative half-cycle of AC)
- An electronic **valve (switch)** rapidly connects either pipe to the output at high speed.

The **inverter** is precisely this electronic plumbing system. The **DC source** (battery/capacitor) is the reservoir. The **switches** (transistors/IGBTs) act as the valves, alternating the direction of current flow through the **load** (motor, transformer, grid) to synthesize a sinusoidal-like AC voltage from a constant DC supply. The **control circuit** decides how long each valve stays open — this is essentially **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Fundamental frequency $f_0$**: typically **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (US) for grid-tied applications
- **Switching frequency $f_s$**: **2 kHz to 20 kHz** for IGBT-based inverters
- **Modulation index $m_a$**: 0 to 1 (linear region); can exceed 1 in overmodulation
- **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion)**: target < **5%** for grid-tie standards (IEEE 519)
- **DC link voltage $V_{DC}$**: typically **200 V to 800 V** in industrial drives

### 1.4 Why Switch-Mode?

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use linear amplification for DC-to-AC?** Linear amplifiers waste up to 50% of input power as heat. Switch-mode operation achieves efficiencies of **95–98%** because transistors are either fully ON (low $V_{CE(sat)}$, high $I$) or fully OFF (high $V$, zero $I$) — minimizing the $P = V \times I$ dissipation product.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Inverter Topologies

Inverters can be classified based on several criteria:

| **Criterion** | **Category A** | **Category B** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Phases** | Single-phase | Three-phase |
| **Configuration** | Half-bridge, Full-bridge, Push-pull | 3-phase 6-step, 3-phase PWM |
| **Switching Device** | VSI (Voltage Source Inverter) | CSI (Current Source Inverter) |
| **Output Waveform** | Square wave, SPWM, SVPWM | Quasi-square, stepped wave |
| **Power Flow** | Uni-directional | Bi-directional (regenerative) |

### 2.2 Single-Phase Half-Bridge Inverter

**Circuit Composition:**
- **2 switching devices** ($S_1$, $S_2$) — typically IGBTs
- **2 equal-value capacitors** ($C_1 = C_2$) forming a **split DC bus**
- **Load** connected between the midpoint of switches and midpoint of capacitors

**Operating Principle:**
- $S_1$ ON for **0 to T/2** → output = $+V_{DC}/2$
- $S_2$ ON for **T/2 to T** → output = $-V_{DC}/2$
- Both OFF → output = 0 (dead zone)

### 2.3 Single-Phase Full-Bridge (H-Bridge) Inverter

**Circuit Composition:**
- **4 switching devices** ($S_1$, $S_2$, $S_3$, $S_4$) in H-configuration
- **Single DC source**
- **Load** connected between two midpoints

**Operating Principle:**
- $S_1, S_2$ ON → output = $+V_{DC}$
- $S_3, S_4$ ON → output = $-V_{DC}$
- Cross-pair switching produces bipolar output

### 2.4 Push-Pull Inverter

**Circuit Composition:**
- **2 switching devices** with **center-tapped transformer**
- Each switch drives one half of the primary winding

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains all high-yield formulas for Module 3 Inverter Topologies — memorized for KTU ESE.**

| **Parameter** | **Half-Bridge** | **Full-Bridge (Square Wave)** | **PWM (Sinusoidal)** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak Output Voltage $V_o$ | $V_{DC}/2$ | $V_{DC}$ | $m_a \cdot V_{DC}/2$ (linear) | **V (volts)** |
| RMS Output Voltage $V_{rms}$ | $V_{DC}/2$ | $V_{DC}$ | $V_{DC}/\sqrt{2} \cdot m_a$ (linear) | **V (volts)** |
| Fundamental RMS $V_{1,rms}$ | $2V_{DC}/(2\pi)\cdot\sqrt{2}$ | $4V_{DC}/(2\pi)\cdot\sqrt{2}$ | $m_a \cdot V_{DC}/\sqrt{2}$ | **V** |
| Fourier coefficient $V_n$ | $\dfrac{2V_{DC}}{n\pi}$ (odd $n$) | $\dfrac{4V_{DC}}{n\pi}$ (odd $n$) | — | **V** |
| THD (Square wave) | $48.34\%$ | $48.34\%$ | decreases with $m_f$ | **%** |
| Modulation Index $m_a$ | — | — | $A_r/A_c$ | dimensionless |
| Frequency Modulation Ratio $m_f$ | — | — | $f_s/f_1$ | dimensionless |
| RMS for quasi-square ($180°-2\alpha$ pulse) | — | $V_{DC}\sqrt{\frac{(\pi-\alpha)}{\pi}}$ | — | **V** |
| Distortion Factor $DF$ | $V_{1,rms}/V_{rms}$ | $V_{1,rms}/V_{rms}$ | — | dimensionless |
| Harmonic Factor $HF_n$ | $V_{n,rms}/V_{1,rms}$ | $V_{n,rms}/V_{1,rms}$ | — | dimensionless |

**Derived Identities:**
- $V_{1,rms} = \dfrac{4V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}\cdot\pi} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}\cdot V_{DC}}{\pi}$
- $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} \times 100\%$
- $DF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (THD)^2}}$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar PV grid-tie systems** — full-bridge PWM VSI is the industry standard
- **UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supplies)** — typically full-bridge with LC filter
- **Induction motor drives** — three-phase VSI with SVPWM
- **Electric vehicle traction inverters** — three-level NPC (neutral-point clamped) topology
- **HVDC transmission** — modular multilevel converters (MMC) — an advanced inverter topology

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Series of a Square-Wave Output (Full-Bridge Inverter)

A full-bridge inverter produces a bipolar square wave of amplitude $\pm V_{DC}$ with period $T$. The Fourier series expansion of this waveform is derived as follows.

**Step 1: Identify the function.**

The square wave $v_o(t)$ is an **odd, half-wave symmetric** function. By Fourier theory, only **odd harmonics** of the **sine** component will be present.

$$
v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +V_{DC}, & 0 \leq t \leq T/2 \\ -V_{DC}, & T/2 \leq t \leq T \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2: General Fourier Series.**

For a periodic function of period $T = 2\pi/\omega$:

$$
v_o(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t)\right]
$$

**Step 3: Compute $a_0$ (DC component).**

By symmetry, the average value over a full cycle is zero:

$$
a_0 = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_o(t)\,dt = \frac{1}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_{DC}\,dt + \int_{T/2}^T (-V_{DC})\,dt\right]
$$

$$
a_0 = \frac{1}{T}\left[V_{DC}\cdot\frac{T}{2} - V_{DC}\cdot\frac{T}{2}\right] = 0
$$

**Step 4: Compute $a_n$ (cosine coefficients).**

For an **odd function**, all $a_n = 0$. We verify:

$$
a_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T v_o(t)\cos(n\omega t)\,dt = 0 \quad \text{(by odd symmetry)}
$$

**Step 5: Compute $b_n$ (sine coefficients).**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T}\int_0^T v_o(t)\sin(n\omega t)\,dt
$$

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T}\left[\int_0^{T/2} V_{DC}\sin(n\omega t)\,dt + \int_{T/2}^T (-V_{DC})\sin(n\omega t)\,dt\right]
$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
\int_0^{T/2} V_{DC}\sin(n\omega t)\,dt = V_{DC}\left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\right]_0^{T/2}
$$

$$
= -\frac{V_{DC}}{n\omega}\left[\cos(n\pi) - 1\right] = \frac{V_{DC}}{n\omega}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

$$
\int_{T/2}^T (-V_{DC})\sin(n\omega t)\,dt = -V_{DC}\left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n\omega}\right]_{T/2}^T
$$

$$
= \frac{V_{DC}}{n\omega}\left[\cos(2n\pi) - \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{V_{DC}}{n\omega}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right]
$$

**Step 7: Combine.**

$$
b_n = \frac{2}{T}\cdot\frac{2V_{DC}}{n\omega}\left[1 - \cos(n\pi)\right] = \frac{4V_{DC}}{Tn\omega}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]
$$

**Step 8: Simplify using $\omega = 2\pi/T$.**

$$
b_n = \frac{4V_{DC}}{Tn\cdot (2\pi/T)}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right] = \frac{2V_{DC}}{n\pi}\left[1 - (-1)^n\right]
$$

**Step 9: Apply the parity condition.**

- For **even $n$**: $1 - (-1)^n = 0$, so $b_n = 0$ (even harmonics vanish)
- For **odd $n$**: $1 - (-1)^n = 2$, so $b_n = \dfrac{4V_{DC}}{n\pi}$

$$
\boxed{v_o(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\ldots}^{\infty} \frac{4V_{DC}}{n\pi} \sin(n\omega t)}
$$

**Step 10: Fundamental component ($n=1$).**

$$
v_{1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_{1,rms} = \frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_{DC}}{\pi}
$$

**Step 11: Total RMS voltage.**

$$
V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_o^2(t)\,dt} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\left[V_{DC}^2\cdot\frac{T}{2} + V_{DC}^2\cdot\frac{T}{2}\right]} = V_{DC}
$$

**Step 12: Compute THD.**

$$
THD = \frac{\sqrt{V_{rms}^2 - V_{1,rms}^2}}{V_{1,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{V_{DC}^2 - \left(\frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}}{\frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi\sqrt{2}}}
$$

$$
= \frac{\sqrt{1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2}}}{\frac{4}{\pi\sqrt{2}}} = \frac{\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2 - 8}{\pi^2}}}{\frac{4}{\pi\sqrt{2}}} = \frac{\pi\sqrt{2}}{4}\cdot\sqrt{\frac{\pi^2-8}{\pi^2}}
$$

$$
= \frac{\sqrt{2(\pi^2-8)}}{4} \times 100\% \approx 48.34\%
$$

This confirms the canonical THD of a square wave is **48.34%**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Single-Phase Full-Bridge RMS Output for Quasi-Square Wave

For a quasi-square wave where each pulse has width $(\pi - 2\alpha)$ instead of $\pi$ (used to control the fundamental amplitude).

**Step 1: Define the pulse angles.**

Output is $+V_{DC}$ from $\alpha$ to $(\pi-\alpha)$, then $-V_{DC}$ from $(\pi+\alpha)$ to $(2\pi-\alpha)$.

**Step 2: Set up the integral.**

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi-\alpha} V_{DC}^2\,d(\omega t) = \frac{V_{DC}^2}{\pi}(\pi - 2\alpha)
$$

**Step 3: Final form.**

$$
\boxed{V_{rms} = V_{DC}\sqrt{\frac{\pi-2\alpha}{\pi}}}
$$

**Step 4: Fundamental component (peak).**

By Fourier analysis of the quasi-square wave:

$$
V_{1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4V_{DC}}{\pi}\cos(\alpha)
$$

This shows that **increasing $\alpha$ reduces the fundamental** — a method of **amplitude control** in square-wave inverters.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Inverter Topologies

| **Topology** | **Switches** | **DC Source** | **Peak Output** | **Switch Stress** | **Cost** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Half-Bridge | 2 | Split ($V_{DC}/2$ each) | $V_{DC}/2$ | $V_{DC}$ | Low | Low-power UPS, fluorescent lamp drivers |
| Full-Bridge | 4 | Single | $V_{DC}$ | $V_{DC}$ | Medium | Motor drives, solar PV, induction heating |
| Push-Pull | 2 | Single + center-tap transformer | $V_{DC}$ | $2V_{DC}$ | Low (only 2 switches) | Low-voltage DC sources, telecom |
| Three-Phase VSI | 6 | Single | $V_{DC}$ (line), $V_{DC}/\sqrt{3}$ (phase) | $V_{DC}$ | High | Industrial AC drives, grid-tie |
| Three-Phase NPC | 12 (incl. 6 diodes) | Split DC link | $V_{DC}/2$ per device | $V_{DC}/2$ | High | High-voltage drives, traction |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: SPWM — Modulation Index and Output Voltage

In **Sinusoidal Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM)**, a sinusoidal reference $v_r = A_r \sin(\omega_r t)$ is compared with a triangular carrier $v_c = A_c \cdot \text{tri}(f_c t)$.

**Step 1: Define modulation parameters.**

$$
m_a = \frac{A_r}{A_c} \quad \text{(amplitude modulation ratio)}
$$

$$
m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_1} \quad \text{(frequency modulation ratio)}
$$

**Step 2: Output voltage in the linear region ($m_a \leq 1$).**

For single-phase full-bridge SPWM in linear modulation:

$$
V_{1,\text{peak}} = m_a \cdot V_{DC}
$$

**Step 3: RMS value of fundamental.**

$$
V_{1,rms} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{DC}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Step 4: In overmodulation ($m_a > 1$).**

The output tends toward a **square wave** as $m_a \to \infty$, and $V_{1,\text{peak}}$ saturates at $\dfrac{4V_{DC}}{\pi}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Harmonic Content of SPWM:** For $m_f$ large and odd, the dominant harmonic is at frequency $f_c$ with reduced low-order harmonic content compared to square wave — this is the key advantage of PWM.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A single-phase full-bridge inverter has $V_{DC} = 200$ V and operates in square-wave mode. Find (a) RMS output voltage, (b) peak fundamental component, (c) THD.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** For square wave, $V_{rms} = V_{DC} = 200$ V

**Part (b):** $V_{1,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{4V_{DC}}{\pi} = \dfrac{4 \times 200}{\pi} = \dfrac{800}{\pi} \approx 254.65$ V

**Part (c):** THD = 48.34% (standard for square wave)

**Part (a) [Alternative — for quasi-square]:** If pulse width is $120°$ (i.e., $\alpha = 30°$):

$$
V_{rms} = V_{DC}\sqrt{\frac{\pi - 2\alpha}{\pi}} = 200\sqrt{\frac{\pi - \pi/3}{\pi}} = 200\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} = 200 \times 0.8165 = 163.3 \text{ V}
$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: General Inverter System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[DC Source: Battery PV Fuel Cell] --> B[DC Link Filter: L and C]
    B --> C[Inverter Bridge: 4 Switches H-Bridge]
    C --> D[PWM Controller: SPWM SVPWM]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Output Filter: LC Low-Pass]
    E --> F[AC Load: Motor Grid Transformer]
    F --> G[Feedback: Voltage and Current Sensors]
    G --> D
    style A fill:#4a90e2,color:#fff
    style C fill:#e94e77,color:#fff
    style D fill:#f5a623,color:#fff
    style F fill:#7ed321,color:#fff
```

**Architectural Flow Description:**
- The **DC Source** supplies unregulated DC voltage
- The **DC Link Filter** (capacitor bank) stabilizes voltage and absorbs ripple
- The **Inverter Bridge** performs DC-to-AC conversion via high-frequency switching
- The **PWM Controller** generates gating signals based on a reference and carrier waveform
- The **LC Output Filter** smooths the PWM waveform into a near-sinusoidal shape
- The **AC Load** receives the conditioned power
- The **Feedback** loop ensures output regulation via closed-loop control

---

**Key features:** Two switches, two split capacitors. Output is taken between switch midpoint and capacitor midpoint. Output voltage is half the DC link.

```

**Switching Sequence:**
- State 1: S1 and S2 ON → current flows from +VDC through load to -VDC → output = +VDC
- State 2: S3 and S4 ON → current flows from -VDC through load to +VDC → output = -VDC
- State 3 and 4: Diagonal pairs OFF → no output (or zero-state for PWM)

```

**Operating Principle:** When $v_r > v_c$, comparator outputs HIGH → upper switch of one leg turns ON. When $v_r < v_c$, lower switch turns ON. The pulse widths vary sinusoidally → sinusoidal average output.
