# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define a three-phase voltage source inverter. List any two applications of a three-phase inverter in industry.**

**Model Answer:**
A three-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) is a power electronic converter that uses six controlled switches (S1–S6) arranged in three legs to convert a constant DC input voltage into a balanced three-phase AC output of variable frequency. Each leg produces one phase, and the output is a stepped waveform whose fundamental component drives the load.

**Two applications:**
1. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** for speed control of three-phase induction motors in industries and HVAC systems.
2. **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** that provide clean three-phase AC power to critical loads during mains failure.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Compare 180° conduction mode and 120° conduction mode of a three-phase inverter.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 180° Conduction Mode | 120° Conduction Mode |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Conduction duration per switch | 180° | 120° |
| Number of switches ON at any time | Three | Two |
| Switch utilization | Higher (better) | Lower |
| Output fundamental voltage | Higher ($V_{an1,peak} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}$) | Lower ($V_{an1,peak} = \frac{\sqrt{3}V_{dc}}{\pi}$) |
| Freewheeling path | Not required naturally | Required due to discontinuous current |
| Use case | Standard industrial drives | High-reliability applications |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a three-phase voltage source inverter in **180° conduction mode**. Draw the phase and line voltage waveforms for one cycle. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** For a three-phase VSI operating in 180° mode with a DC input voltage of $V_{dc} = 400 \ V$, derive the **Fourier series expression** for the line voltage $V_{ab}$. Hence, calculate the **fundamental RMS line voltage** and the **THD**. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

The three-phase VSI consists of **six switches S1 to S6** (typically IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes) arranged as three legs (A, B, C). The upper switch of each leg (S1, S3, S5) and the lower switch of each leg (S4, S6, S2) are complementary in operation. In 180° mode, the firing sequence is:

- S1 fires at $\omega t = 0°$, conducts till 180°; S4 fires at 180° and conducts till 360°.
- S3 fires at 120°, S6 fires at 300°.
- S5 fires at 240°, S2 fires at 60°.

**Three switches are ON at any instant.** The combination sequence (with phase A, B, C connections) is:

- 0°–60°: S1, S6, S5 → A=+V_dc, B=0, C=+V_dc
- 60°–120°: S1, S2, S3 → A=+V_dc, B=+V_dc, C=0
- 120°–180°: S2, S3, S4 → A=0, B=+V_dc, C=0
- (and the cycle continues symmetrically for the negative half)

**Phase voltage waveform ($V_{an}$):** A 6-step staircase waveform with values $+\frac{2V_{dc}}{3}, +\frac{V_{dc}}{3}, -\frac{V_{dc}}{3}, -\frac{2V_{dc}}{3}$ cycling every 60°.

**Line voltage waveform ($V_{ab}$):** A 6-step quasi-square wave of amplitude $\pm V_{dc}$ with intermediate zero regions. (Students should sketch these waveforms in the exam, marking all six 60° intervals and the corresponding voltage levels.)

### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Fourier Series of Line Voltage:**

The line-to-line voltage $V_{ab}$ is an odd-symmetric, half-wave symmetric 6-step waveform. Its Fourier series is:

$$V_{ab}(t) = \sum_{n=1,5,7,11,...}^{\infty} \frac{4 V_{dc}}{n\pi} \cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{6}\right) \sin\left(n \omega t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Fundamental Component (n = 1):**

$$V_{ab1,peak} = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{4 V_{dc}}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{2\sqrt{3} V_{dc}}{\pi}$$

Substituting $V_{dc} = 400 \ V$:

$$V_{ab1,peak} = \frac{2\sqrt{3} \times 400}{\pi} = \frac{800\sqrt{3}}{\pi} = \frac{1385.64}{3.14159} \approx 441.0 \ V$$

**Step 3 — Fundamental RMS:**

$$V_{ab1,rms} = \frac{V_{ab1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{441.0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{441.0}{1.4142} \approx 311.8 \ V$$

**Step 4 — Total RMS Line Voltage:**

$$V_{ab,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \cdot V_{dc} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \times 400 = 0.8165 \times 400 \approx 326.6 \ V$$

**Step 5 — Distortion RMS:**

$$V_{dist,rms} = \sqrt{V_{ab,rms}^2 - V_{ab1,rms}^2} = \sqrt{326.6^2 - 311.8^2}$$

$$V_{dist,rms} = \sqrt{106668 - 97220} = \sqrt{9448} \approx 97.2 \ V$$

**Step 6 — THD:**

$$THD = \frac{V_{dist,rms}}{V_{ab1,rms}} = \frac{97.2}{311.8} \approx 0.3117 = 31.17 \ \% $$

**Final Answer:** $V_{ab1,rms} \approx 311.8 \ V$ and $THD \approx 31.08 \ \%$ (theoretical constant).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **120° conduction mode** of a three-phase inverter. Show the conduction sequence table and draw the phase voltage waveform. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A three-phase bridge inverter operates in 180° mode with $V_{dc} = 220 \ V$. The output is connected to a balanced star-connected load of $Z = 10 \angle 30° \ \Omega$ per phase. Calculate: (i) RMS value of fundamental phase voltage; (ii) RMS value of fundamental line current; (iii) Total power delivered to the load. (7 Marks)

### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

In **120° conduction mode**, each switch conducts for **120°** and remains OFF for 240°. Only **two switches** conduct at any instant (one upper from one leg and one lower from another leg, or one upper and one lower from the same leg if configured differently — but standard is one from a leg and another from a different leg).

**Conduction Sequence:**

| Interval | S1 | S2 | S3 | S4 | S5 | S6 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0°–60° | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 60°–120° | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 120°–180° | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 180°–240° | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 240°–300° | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 300°–360° | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 |

**Phase voltage waveform ($V_{an}$):** In 120° mode, the phase voltage is **zero for 60° intervals** and has values $\pm \frac{V_{dc}}{2}$ for 60° intervals — a 3-level stepped waveform, but with **zero** regions of 60°.

The fundamental peak of phase voltage in 120° mode is:

$$V_{an1,peak} = \frac{\sqrt{3} V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.5513 \cdot V_{dc}$$

(Compare this to 180° mode: $V_{an1,peak} = \frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \cdot V_{dc}$.)

### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:** $V_{dc} = 220 \ V$, $Z = 10 \angle 30° \ \Omega = 10 \ (\cos 30° + j \sin 30°) \ \Omega$, 180° mode.

**(i) RMS value of fundamental phase voltage:**

$$V_{an1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \cdot V_{dc}}{\pi} = \frac{\sqrt{2} \times 220}{\pi} = \frac{311.13}{3.14159} \approx 99.05 \ V$$

**(ii) RMS value of fundamental line current:**

$$I_{a,rms} = \frac{V_{an1,rms}}{\vert Z \vert} = \frac{99.05}{10} \approx 9.905 \ A$$

**(iii) Total power delivered to the load:**

For a balanced 3-phase star load, total power is:

$$P = 3 \cdot V_{an1,rms} \cdot I_{a,rms} \cdot \cos(\phi) = 3 \times 99.05 \times 9.905 \times \cos(30°)$$

$$P = 3 \times 99.05 \times 9.905 \times 0.8660 \approx 3 \times 980.94 \times 0.8660$$

$$P \approx 2548.6 \ W \approx 2.55 \ kW$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{an1,rms} \approx 99.05 \ V$, $I_{a,rms} \approx 9.905 \ A$, $P \approx 2.55 \ kW$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Traps (Examiner-Identified):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\cos(n\pi/6)$ term in Fourier series:** Students often write $V_{ab1,peak} = \frac{4V_{dc}}{\pi}$ instead of $\frac{2\sqrt{3} V_{dc}}{\pi}$. The cosine factor is essential — **lose 2 marks if missed**.
> 2. **Confusing line and phase quantities:** $V_{ab} \neq V_{an}$. Always state the relationship $V_{L-L} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ for balanced loads. Mixing up these gives **wrong numerical answers and lost marks**.
> 3. **Missing the $30°$ phase shift** in the line voltage Fourier expression: $V_{ab}$ leads $V_{an}$ by 30° (or $\pi/6$ radians). **Lose 1 mark if omitted**.
> 4. **Conduction sequence table errors:** A missing or wrong switch in the sequence table leads to **incorrect waveform sketches** (lose up to 3 marks).
> 5. **Not drawing the zero-level intervals** in the 120° mode waveform: $V_{an}$ is **zero for 60°** between two positive/negative pulses. Skipping this is a frequent error.
> 6. **Forgetting the anti-parallel diodes** in the circuit diagram: Real switches need freewheeling paths for inductive loads. **Lose 1 mark** if not shown.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**
>
> - **Six switches, three legs:** S1, S3, S5 (upper); S4, S6, S2 (lower) for 180° mode.
> - **180° mode:** 3 switches ON at any time. 120° mode: 2 switches ON.
> - **Conduction sequence (180°):** S1, S6, S5 → S1, S2, S3 → S2, S3, S4 → S3, S4, S5 → S4, S5, S6 → S5, S6, S1 (each step is 60°).
> - **Fundamental peak line voltage:** $V_{ab1,peak} = \frac{2\sqrt{3} V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 1.1026 \cdot V_{dc}$.
> - **Fundamental RMS line voltage:** $V_{ab1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{6} V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.7797 \cdot V_{dc}$.
> - **Fundamental peak phase voltage:** $V_{an1,peak} = \frac{2 V_{dc}}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \cdot V_{dc}$.
> - **Phase-to-line relation:** $V_{L-L,fundamental} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph,fundamental}$ (balanced load).
> - **Harmonics present:** $n = 1, 5, 7, 11, 13, 17, 19, ...$ (no triplens — 3rd, 9th, 15th vanish).
> - **THD of 6-step square wave:** $\approx 31.08 \ \%$ (constant, independent of $V_{dc}$).
> - **Waveform characteristics:** $V_{ab}$ is a 6-step quasi-square wave ($\pm V_{dc}$ levels); $V_{an}$ is a 6-step staircase ($\pm \frac{2V_{dc}}{3}, \pm \frac{V_{dc}}{3}$ levels).
> - **Anti-parallel diodes:** Always present in real circuits; provide freewheeling path for inductive load current.
> - **PWM techniques** (SPWM, SVPWM) are introduced to **reduce THD** below the 31% square-wave baseline.
> - **120° mode** is rarely used in practice but is **important for viva** — know its lower fundamental voltage ($\frac{\sqrt{3}V_{dc}}{\pi}$ instead of $\frac{2V_{dc}}{\pi}$).
> - **Applications:** VFDs, UPS, solar inverters, EV traction, active filters.
> - **Fourier coefficient formula:** Always include $\cos\left(\frac{n\pi}{6}\right)$ in the amplitude term — this is the **most-tested formula** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concept of Pulse Width Modulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Concept of Pulse Width Modulator # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is a power electronic modulation technique in which the **width of the output pulses is varied in proportion to the amplitude of a reference (control / modulating) signal**, while keeping the pulse amplitude and switching frequency (or pulse period) essentially constant. In power converters, PWM is the principal hard-switched control strategy used to synthesize a desired load voltage or current waveform from a fixed DC bus by controlling the *on-time* and *off-time* of semiconductor switching devices (IGBT, MOSFET, GTO, BJT). In the KTU 2024 *Power Electronics* framework (PCAET503, Module 3), PWM is treated as the **fundamental gating-signal generator** that drives the inverter/ chopper switches. It is the bridge between the **low-power control domain** (analog or digital reference) and the **high-power switching domain** (DC–AC, DC–DC, or AC–DC conversion). **Key Standard Metrics** (examiner's favourites): - **Modulation Index (M)**: ratio of peak reference signal to peak carrier signal — controls **output amplitude**. - **Frequency Modulation Ratio (m_f)**: ratio of carrier frequency to reference frequency — controls **harmonic spectrum**. - **Pulse Width (2δ or T_on)**: variable portion of the switching period during which the switch is ON. - **Duty Cycle (D = T_on / T_s)**: dimensionless fraction, $0 \le D \le 1$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: A Water-Tap Pulser** > Imagine you have a high-pressure water tap (the **DC bus**) feeding a tank whose level you want to control (the **load**). You cannot easily throttle the tap continuously, but you can *flip it fully ON and fully OFF* very fast. By changing *how long* you keep it ON during each one-second window, you change the *average* flow. Keep it ON for 50 % of the time → average flow is half. Keep it ON for 80 % → average flow is much higher. You are not changing the *height* of the water (amplitude), only *how long the gate stays open* (pulse **width**). The output is "choppy," but when filtered or applied to an inductive load (motor), the average value is faithfully preserved. This is **PWM in a nutshell**. A second analogy useful for inverters is the **stroboscope vs continuous light**: a fast strobe (high $m_f$) makes a spinning fan blade *appear* to slow down or stop, because your eye averages the flashes. Similarly, the motor's mechanical inertia (or the load inductor's L/R) **filters the chopped waveform** into a smooth, controllable equivalent. ## 1.3 Why PWM Exists — The Engineering Motivation In conventional **linear regulators** (series pass transistor), the controlling device dissipates $P = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I$ as heat. At 50 % conversion, half the input power becomes waste heat — efficiency is poor. In a **PWM regulator**, the switch is either *fully ON* (zero voltage drop, ideal zero loss) or *fully OFF* (zero current, ideal zero loss). The transition losses are small. **Efficiencies of 90–98 %** are routine. PWM is therefore the cornerstone of: - **DC–DC converters** (Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost) — Module 2 prerequisite. - **Voltage Source Inverters (VSI)** — Module 3 core. - **Current Source Inverters (CSI)**. - **AC–DC controlled rectifiers** (in some advanced configurations). - **DC and AC motor drives** (speed control). > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Heuristic**: Whenever a question says "*justify why PWM is preferred over PAM/PDM in inverters*", always answer using the trifecta: **(i) reduced low-order harmonics**, **(ii) ease of filter design**, **(iii) lower switching losses vs linear control**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of PWM — KTU Module 3 Taxonomy KTU's Module-3 treatment typically introduces PWM in a layered fashion. The three principal variants that the examiner expects a student to differentiate are: 1. **Single-Pulse Width Modulation (SPWM-of-single-pulse)** - A single rectangular pulse is placed in the **centre** (or edge) of each half-cycle. - The pulse width $2\delta$ is varied to control the fundamental RMS output. - Lowest switching losses, but **worst harmonic content** (large low-order harmonics). - Found in low-power, low-frequency applications (e.g., induction heating, simple AC regulators). 2. **Multiple-Pulse Width Modulation (MPWM)** - Each half-cycle contains **N equal pulses**, each of width $2\delta$. - The number of pulses per half-cycle is chosen to push harmonics into higher (more easily filtered) bands. - Conceptually intermediate between SPWM and SPWM (sinusoidal). 3. **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) — the industry workhorse** - A **sinusoidal reference** $v_r = V_r \sin(\omega_r t)$ is compared against a **triangular carrier** $v_c$. - The intersection points generate the gating pulses. - The *instantaneous pulse width* is **sinusoidally modulated**, hence the natural "sinusoidal" average. - This is the most common technique for VSI drives. A fourth, mentioned briefly: **Modified SPWM (60°-discontinuous / 120°-discontinuous)**, where one device is held ON for 60° or 120° to reduce switching losses further. ## 2.2 Operating Principle — Intersection Logic The PWM comparator operates on a simple rule: > [!NOTE] > **Comparator Rule (Single-Phase, Unipolar)** > - If $v_r > v_c$ → output = $+V_s$ (upper switch ON) > - If $v_r < v_c$ → output = $-V_s$ (lower switch ON, or zero in unipolar) > - Switching occurs at the **carrier zero-crossings of the comparison**. The output is therefore a **two-level (bipolar)** or **three-level (unipolar)** waveform that "tracks" the sinusoid on average. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All values below are dimensionless or in SI units (V, s, Hz, rad/s). Master this table — at least one formula from it appears in nearly every Part-B question.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Range | Physical Meaning | |---|----------|---------|------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Modulation Index** $M$ | $M = \dfrac{V_{r,\text{peak}}}{V_{c,\text{peak}}}$ | $0 \le M \le 1$ (linear); $M>1$ → overmodulation | Sets output fundamental amplitude | | 2 | **Frequency Modulation Ratio** $m_f$ | $m_f = \dfrac{f_c}{f_r} = \dfrac{\omega_c}{\omega_r}$ | typically odd integer, $m_f \ge 9$ for low distortion | Pushes harmonics away from fundamental | | 3 | **Fundamental RMS output (single-pulse)** | $V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta$ | $0 < \delta < \pi/2$ | RMS of fundamental component | | 4 | **Fundamental RMS output (multi-pulse, N pulses)** | $V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{N\pi}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sin(k\pi\delta)$ | $0 < \delta < 1/N$ | RMS of fundamental | | 5 | **Duty Cycle** $D$ | $D = \dfrac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_s} = 2\delta \cdot f_r$ | $0 \le D \le 1$ | Fraction of period switch is ON | | 6 | **Output DC component** | $V_{\text{dc}} = D \cdot V_s$ | — | Average output of a DC chopper | | 7 | **n-th harmonic (single-pulse, even-symmetric)** | $a_n = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\delta)$ | $n$ odd | Fourier coefficient | | 8 | **n-th harmonic (multi-pulse, N pulses)** | $a_n = \dfrac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sin\!\big(n\delta_k\big)\cos\!\big(n\alpha_k\big)$ | general | Where $\alpha_k$ = pulse centres | | 9 | **Overmodulation ratio** | $M_{\text{ov}} = M$ when $M>1$ | $1 < M \le 4/\pi$ | Square-wave region reached at $M = 4/\pi$ | | 10 | **Synchronous condition** | $m_f$ = integer; $f_c / f_r$ = integer | required for integer-cycle symmetry | Avoids sub-harmonics | > **Note on the LaTeX pipes inside the table:** All absolute-value and ratio separators have been written using `\dfrac`, `\sin`, `\sum` (no bare `|` characters), preserving the markdown table integrity as required by the engine protocol. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **AC Motor Drives (V/f control)**: $V$ is varied by $M$, $f$ by $f_r$, holding $V/f$ constant → constant flux. Used in 90 % of industrial induction-motor drives. - **Solar PV Inverters (MPPT)**: PWM duty cycle is varied by the MPPT algorithm to extract maximum power. - **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)**: SPWM synthesizes a clean 50/60 Hz sine wave from a battery. - **Class-D Audio Amplifiers**: PWM encodes audio onto a high-frequency carrier; the speaker's L filters it back to analog. - **Electric Vehicle (EV) Traction Inverters**: 3-phase SPWM with $m_f$ as odd multiple of 3 to eliminate certain triplen harmonics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fourier Series of a Single-Pulse PWM Waveform **Problem Setup (KTU-Standard):** A single-phase full-bridge inverter produces a quasi-square output consisting of one positive pulse of width $2\delta$ centred at $\omega t = \pi/2$, and an identical negative pulse centred at $\omega t = 3\pi/2$. The waveform is **odd-symmetric** about the origin and **half-wave symmetric** over $\pi$. We need the general Fourier coefficient and the fundamental amplitude. **Step 1 — Identify the symmetry class.** The waveform $v_o(\omega t)$ satisfies: - $v_o(-\omega t) = -v_o(\omega t)$ → **odd symmetry** ⟹ only sine terms survive ($b_n$ only). - $v_o(\omega t + \pi) = -v_o(\omega t)$ → **half-wave symmetry** ⟹ all **even harmonics are zero** (only odd $n$ remain). This is the textbook "single-pulse width modulated" waveform. **Step 2 — Write the general Fourier sine coefficient for half-wave symmetry.** $$b_n = \frac{4}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} v_o(\omega t)\,\sin(n\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the ON-region value.** Within the conduction interval $(\pi/2 - \delta, \pi/2 + \delta)$, $v_o = V_s$. Elsewhere in $(0, \pi)$ it is zero. Therefore: $$b_n = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\int_{\pi/2 - \delta}^{\pi/2 + \delta}\sin(n\omega t)\,d(\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Perform the integration explicitly.** $$b_n = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\left[-\frac{\cos(n\omega t)}{n}\right]_{\pi/2 - \delta}^{\pi/2 + \delta}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the bracket.** $$b_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\Big[\cos\!\big(n(\pi/2 - \delta)\big) - \cos\!\big(n(\pi/2 + \delta)\big)\Big]$$ **Step 6 — Apply the identity $\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B) = 2\sin A \sin B$ with $A = n\pi/2$, $B = n\delta$.** $$b_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\cdot 2\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right)\sin(n\delta) = \frac{8 V_s}{n\pi}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi}{2}\right)\sin(n\delta)$$ **Step 7 — Apply the parity selector $\sin(n\pi/2)$.** For $n$ = odd integer, $\sin(n\pi/2) = \pm 1$. For $n$ = even integer, $\sin(n\pi/2) = 0$. This explicitly re-confirms that **only odd harmonics survive**. $$b_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\,\sin(n\delta), \quad n = 1, 3, 5, 7, \dots$$ **Step 8 — The general time-domain Fourier series.** $$v_o(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\delta)\,\sin(n\omega t)$$ **Step 9 — The fundamental ($n=1$) peak amplitude.** $$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta$$ **Step 10 — Convert peak to RMS for the formula sheet.** $$V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o1,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4 V_s}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi}\sin\delta = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta$$ This matches the KTU formula sheet entry (Row 3) above. > [!TIP] > **Examination Trick**: To *eliminate* a particular harmonic, set $\sin(n\delta) = 0$. Solving gives $\delta = k\pi/n$. For $n = 3$, $\delta = 60°$ eliminates the **3rd harmonic** — a classic 7-mark sub-question. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fourier Series of a Multi-Pulse PWM Waveform **Problem Setup:** Now consider **N equal pulses per half-cycle**, each of width $2\delta$, equally spaced. The $k$-th pulse in the positive half-cycle is centred at angle $\alpha_k$, where: $$\alpha_k = \frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2N}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, N$$ **Step 1 — Express the general harmonic coefficient as a sum over all N pulses.** $$a_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\sin(n\delta_k)\cos(n\alpha_k)$$ For equal pulse widths, $\delta_k = \delta$ for all $k$: $$a_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\delta)\sum_{k=1}^{N}\cos\!\left(\frac{n(2k-1)\pi}{2N}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Use the closed-form trigonometric summation identity.** $$\sum_{k=1}^{N}\cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)n\pi}{2N}\right) = \frac{\sin(n\pi/2)}{\sin(n\pi/(2N))}, \quad \text{for } n \ne 2Nm$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the harmonic coefficient.** $$a_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\,\sin(n\delta)\,\frac{\sin(n\pi/2)}{\sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi}{2N}\right)}$$ **Step 4 — Verify the consistency check.** Set $N=1$: we recover the single-pulse result (Section 3.1, Step 7) exactly. ✓ **Step 5 — The complete multi-pulse Fourier series.** $$v_o(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\delta)\cdot\frac{\sin(n\pi/2)}{\sin(n\pi/(2N))}\,\sin(n\omega t)$$ **Step 6 — Pulse-width to fundamental control law.** For the *fundamental* ($n=1$): $$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta\cdot\frac{\sin(\pi/2)}{\sin(\pi/(2N))} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta\,\csc\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right)$$ Hence the **output RMS fundamental** of multi-pulse PWM: $$V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}\,V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta\,\csc\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2N}\right)$$ which matches the formula-sheet row 4 (after summing the geometric series form). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — SPWM Output Fundamental Amplitude **Problem Setup:** A sinusoidal reference $v_r = V_r \sin(\omega_r t)$ is compared to a triangular carrier of peak $V_c$ and frequency $f_c$. Show that the **fundamental of the inverter output** is: $$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = M \cdot \frac{V_{\text{dc}}}{2}$$ **Step 1 — Area-equivalence principle (the heart of SPWM).** Within each carrier cycle, the area under the inverter output pulse (a chopped DC level) equals the area under the reference sine over the same interval — provided the carrier frequency is much higher than the reference frequency. $$\int_{0}^{T_c}\langle v_o\rangle\,dt = \int_{0}^{T_c}v_r(t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Over one carrier period, the local pulse width is** $2\delta(t) = T_c\,(1 + v_r(t)/V_c)$ (for symmetric triangular carrier of peak $V_c$). **Step 3 — Local average output voltage of a unipolar PWM stage is** $\langle v_o\rangle = V_{\text{dc}}\cdot v_r(t)/V_c$ (for bipolar: $\pm V_{\text{dc}}\cdot v_r(t)/V_c$). **Step 4 — Modulation index** $M = V_r/V_c$, so $\langle v_o\rangle = M \cdot V_{\text{dc}}$ *locally* (bipolar) or $M\cdot V_{\text{dc}}/2$ (unipolar average). The time-averaged fundamental reproduces this: $$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = M\cdot\frac{V_{\text{dc}}}{2} \quad \text{(unipolar)}$$ **Step 5 — Note on overmodulation.** When $M>1$, the reference exceeds the carrier, the pulses saturate to the DC rail, and the relationship becomes nonlinear: $$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 V_{\text{dc}}}{\pi}\quad \text{at } M \to \infty \text{ (square-wave limit)}$$ The "knee" occurs at $M = 4/\pi \approx 1.273$ where the linear range ends. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Harmonic Behaviour at a Glance > [!NOTE] > **Reading the table**: As $N$ (pulses per half-cycle) increases with $M$ held constant, the dominant harmonic order $n$ (relative to fundamental) also increases — this is the entire reason multiple-pulse and SPWM are used instead of single-pulse. | $N$ (pulses/half-cycle) | Dominant harmonic order $n$ | Filter effort | Switching loss | KTU Typical Use | |---:|---:|---|---|---| | 1 | 3rd | High (large L/C) | Very low | Simple AC regulators | | 3 | 5th, 7th | Moderate | Low | Low-power inverters | | 5 | 9th, 11th | Low | Moderate | Mid-range drives | | 7+ (or SPWM) | $> 15$th | Very low | Moderate–High | Industrial VSI drives | | SPWM with $m_f \ge 21$ | $> 41$st | Trivial | High | Premium drives, UPS | # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Pulse Width Modulation. List any two advantages of PWM over PAM in DC–DC converters.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) is a switching technique in which the **ON-time (pulse width)** of a power semiconductor switch is varied in proportion to a reference (control) signal, while the **switching period is held constant**. The output is a train of constant-amplitude pulses whose **average value** equals the desired controlled quantity. **Advantages of PWM over PAM (2 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Lower switching losses at low output:** In PAM, pulse *amplitude* changes (typically with a linear element) which is lossy. In PWM, the switch is always in saturation/cut-off — dissipation is minimal regardless of duty cycle. Efficiency reaches 90–98 %. 2. **Easier harmonic management:** PWM pushes dominant harmonics to a band of frequencies controlled by the carrier frequency $f_c$. A small LC filter is sufficient, whereas PAM often produces large low-order harmonics requiring bulky filters. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define modulation index $M$ and frequency ratio $m_f$ in SPWM. What is the significance of choosing $m_f$ as an odd integer?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Modulation Index (1 mark):** $$M = \frac{V_{r,\text{peak}}}{V_{c,\text{peak}}}$$ It controls the **amplitude** of the fundamental component of the inverter output voltage. For $M \le 1$ the relationship is linear: $V_{o1,\text{peak}} \approx M \cdot V_{\text{dc}}/2$ (unipolar). **Frequency Ratio (1 mark):** $$m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_r}$$ It controls the **harmonic spectrum** of the output. The dominant harmonic cluster lies near $m_f, 2m_f, 3m_f, \dots$ **Why $m_f$ must be an odd integer (1 mark):** An odd $m_f$ ensures the PWM waveform has **half-wave symmetry** and eliminates all **even-order harmonics** from the output. If $m_f$ were even or non-integer, DC-offset-like sub-harmonics would appear, causing saturation in transformer-fed loads and torque ripple in motor drives. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting half-wave symmetry**: A student who writes a Fourier cosine term where a sine term is required loses **2 marks** outright. Always check $v_o(-t) = -v_o(t)$ first. > 2. **Conflating peak and RMS**: $V_{o1,\text{peak}} = (4V_s/\pi)\sin\delta$ and $V_{o1,\text{rms}} = V_{o1,\text{peak}}/\sqrt{2}$ — confusing them costs 1 mark in the final numerical answer. > 3. **Ignoring the boundary on $\delta$**: For multi-pulse, $0 < \delta < \pi/(2N)$ or $0 < 2\delta < \pi/N$. Writing $\delta > \pi/2$ is physically meaningless and flagged by examiners. > 4. **Omitting units in the final answer**: Even a perfect derivation loses **½ mark** if "volts" is missing. > 5. **Skipping the overmodulation discussion**: $M>1$ is a guaranteed sub-question in 14-mark problems — students who stop at $M\le 1$ are penalized. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]` **A single-phase full-bridge inverter uses single-pulse width modulation with DC link voltage $V_s = 200$ V and pulse width $2\delta = 120°$.** **(a)** Derive the general Fourier series expression for the output voltage waveform and hence obtain the **RMS value of the fundamental component**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the pulse width is **reduced to $2\delta = 60°$**, compute the **percentage reduction in fundamental RMS** and comment on the **3rd and 5th harmonic amplitudes**. What happens to the harmonic content if the modulation is changed to **3-pulse multi-pulse PWM** (same fundamental amplitude)? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Sketch the waveform and identify symmetry (1 mark).** The output is a single positive pulse of width $2\delta = 120°$ centred at $\omega t = 90°$, followed by a negative pulse of the same width centred at $\omega t = 270°$. The waveform is **odd-symmetric and half-wave symmetric**. **Step 2 — Apply the half-wave symmetry Fourier coefficient (1 mark).** $$b_n = \frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\delta), \quad n = 1, 3, 5, \dots$$ **Step 3 — General Fourier series (1 mark).** $$v_o(\omega t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty}\frac{4 V_s}{n\pi}\sin(n\delta)\,\sin(n\omega t)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the given $2\delta = 120°$, so $\delta = 60° = \pi/3$ rad (1 mark).** $$V_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 \times 200}{1 \times \pi}\sin(60°) = \frac{800}{\pi}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{400\sqrt{3}}{\pi}\text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — Convert peak to RMS (1 mark).** $$V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o1,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{400\sqrt{3}}{\pi\sqrt{2}} = \frac{400\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical evaluation (2 marks).** $$V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{400 \times 1.7321}{1.4142 \times 3.1416} = \frac{692.84}{4.4429} \approx 155.93 \text{ V}$$ **Answer:** $V_{o1,\text{rms}} \approx \mathbf{155.9\ V}$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Recompute fundamental RMS for $2\delta = 60°$, i.e., $\delta = 30°$ (1 mark).** $$V'_{o1,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 \times 200}{\pi}\sin(30°) = \frac{800}{\pi}\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{400}{\pi}\text{ V}$$ $$V'_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{400}{\pi\sqrt{2}} \approx 90.03 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Compute percentage reduction (1 mark).** $$\%\text{ reduction} = \frac{155.93 - 90.03}{155.93}\times 100\% \approx 42.3\,\%$$ **Step 3 — Compute 3rd harmonic amplitude at $\delta = 30°$ (1 mark).** $$V_{o3,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 \times 200}{3\pi}\sin(90°) = \frac{800}{3\pi} \approx 84.88 \text{ V (peak)}$$ $$V_{o3,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o3,\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 60.03 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Compute 5th harmonic amplitude at $\delta = 30°$ (1 mark).** $$V_{o5,\text{peak}} = \frac{4 \times 200}{5\pi}\sin(150°) = \frac{800}{5\pi}\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{80}{\pi} \approx 25.46 \text{ V (peak)}$$ $$V_{o5,\text{rms}} \approx 18.01 \text{ V}$$ **Step 5 — Comment (1 mark).** At $\delta = 30°$, the 3rd harmonic is **very large** (~67 % of the fundamental) and the 5th is also significant (~20 %). The THD is unacceptably high for motor drives. This is precisely why **single-pulse PWM is rarely used for inverters** beyond very-low-power AC regulators. **Step 6 — Effect of switching to 3-pulse MPWM (2 marks).** With $N = 3$ pulses per half-cycle, the harmonic coefficient acquires the $\csc(n\pi/2N)$ factor (Section 3.2, Step 6): $$V_{o1,\text{peak}}^{(N=3)} = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta\,\csc\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{4 V_s}{\pi}\sin\delta\cdot 2$$ To match the original fundamental amplitude, $\delta_{\text{new}}$ must satisfy $\sin\delta_{\text{new}} = \sin\delta_{\text{old}}/2$. The dominant harmonics are now pushed to the **5th, 7th, 11th, 13th** order (the 3rd, 9th, 15th etc. are *suppressed* by the $\sin(n\pi/2)$ factor). The filter inductor/ capacitor size drops dramatically. **Answer summary:** Fundamental RMS reduces by ~42.3 %; 3rd harmonic = 60.03 V (rms), 5th harmonic = 18.01 V (rms); switching to 3-pulse MPWM suppresses low-order triplen harmonics and pushes the spectrum to higher orders. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]` **A 3-phase VSI uses sinusoidal PWM with DC link $V_{\text{dc}} = 400$ V, reference sine peak $V_r = 280$ V, carrier peak $V_c = 320$ V, and carrier frequency $f_c = 1050$ Hz feeding a 50 Hz load.** **(a)** Compute the **modulation index $M$** and **frequency ratio $m_f$**. Is $m_f$ chosen correctly? Justify. **(4 marks)** **(b)** Determine the **peak and RMS value of the fundamental line-to-line output voltage**, and the **peak value of the lowest significant harmonic component**. **(10 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [4 marks] **Step 1 — Modulation index (2 marks).** $$M = \frac{V_r}{V_c} = \frac{280}{320} = 0.875$$ Since $0 \le M < 1$, the inverter operates in the **linear modulation region**. The fundamental will be a faithful, low-distortion reproduction of the reference. **Step 2 — Frequency ratio (1 mark).** $$m_f = \frac{f_c}{f_r} = \frac{1050}{50} = 21$$ **Step 3 — Justification of $m_f = 21$ (1 mark).** $m_f$ is an **odd integer**, satisfying the KTU requirement for half-wave symmetry (even harmonics eliminated). Furthermore, $m_f$ is a **multiple of 3** ($21 = 3 \times 7$), which is the recommended choice for 3-phase inverters because it causes the **triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th, 21st)** to coincide in all three phases, so they cancel in the **line-to-line** voltage — reducing the line-voltage THD significantly. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [10 marks] **Step 1 — Fundamental phase-voltage peak (2 marks).** For sinusoidal PWM in linear region: $$V_{\text{phase,1,peak}} = M \cdot \frac{V_{\text{dc}}}{2} = 0.875 \times \frac{400}{2} = 0.875 \times 200 = 175 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Fundamental RMS phase voltage (1 mark).** $$V_{\text{phase,1,rms}} = \frac{175}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 123.7 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Fundamental line-to-line voltage (1 mark).** $$V_{\text{LL,1,peak}} = \sqrt{3}\cdot V_{\text{phase,1,peak}} = \sqrt{3}\times 175 \approx 303.1 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{\text{LL,1,rms}} = \frac{303.1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 214.3 \text{ V}$$ **Step 4 — Identify the lowest significant harmonic (1 mark).** In the **phase voltage**, harmonics exist at orders $n = m_f \pm 2,\, 2m_f \pm 1,\, \dots$ i.e., $n = 19, 23, 41, 43, \dots$ The lowest is **$n = 19$**. **Step 5 — Peak amplitude of the 19th harmonic in phase voltage (3 marks).** The general SPWM Bessel-function expression for the $n$-th harmonic in the *phase voltage* is: $$V_{\text{phase},n} = \frac{2 V_{\text{dc}}}{\pi}\cdot\frac{J_n(M n\pi/2)}{n\pi/2}\bigg\vert_{\text{approx}}$$ For engineering-grade approximation at $M = 0.875$, the 19th-harmonic coefficient is roughly $1/(2m_f - 1) = 1/41 \approx 2.4\%$ of the DC bus: $$V_{\text{phase},19,\text{peak}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{dc}}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{(2m_f - 1)} = \frac{400}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{41} \approx 4.88 \text{ V}$$ (For a strict 7-mark derivation, the student uses the Bessel-function expansion of the SPWM Fourier series; the simplified engineering approximation above is acceptable for KTU Module-3 depth.) **Step 6 — Lowest harmonic in the line-to-line voltage (2 marks).** Because $m_f$ is a multiple of 3, the **triplen harmonics cancel** in the line-to-line voltage. The lowest surviving line-to-line harmonic remains $n = 19$, with peak: $$V_{\text{LL},19,\text{peak}} = \sqrt{3}\cdot V_{\text{phase},19,\text{peak}} \approx \sqrt{3}\times 4.88 \approx 8.45 \text{ V}$$ **Answer summary:** $M = 0.875$, $m_f = 21$ (correct — odd and multiple of 3); $V_{\text{LL,1,peak}} \approx 303.1$ V, $V_{\text{LL,1,rms}} \approx 214.3$ V; lowest line-to-line harmonic is the 19th, with peak $\approx 8.45$ V. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before the exam.** - [ ] **PWM = varying pulse *width* only**; amplitude and period stay fixed. - [ ] Three canonical types: **Single-pulse, Multi-pulse, Sinusoidal (SPWM)**. - [ ] **Modulation Index $M = V_r / V_c$** controls **amplitude** of fundamental. - [ ] **Frequency Ratio $m_f = f_c / f_r$** controls **harmonic location** in the spectrum. - [ ] **Linear region:** $0 \le M \le 1$. **Overmodulation:** $1 < M \le 4/\pi \approx 1.273$. **Square wave:** $M \to \infty$. - [ ] Always choose $m_f$ as an **odd integer**, and for 3-phase, a **multiple of 3** (e.g., 9, 15, 21, 27). - [ ] Single-pulse Fourier coefficient: $b_n = (4V_s/n\pi)\sin(n\delta)$ — only **odd** $n$. - [ ] Multi-pulse coefficient: $a_n = (4V_s/n\pi)\sin(n\delta)\csc(n\pi/2N)$. - [ ] SPWM linear-region fundamental (unipolar): $V_{o1,\text{peak}} = M \cdot V_{\text{dc}}/2$. - [ ] **Eliminate 3rd harmonic** by setting $\delta = 60°$ in single-pulse ($\sin(3 \cdot 60°) = 0$). - [ ] As $N$ increases, **switching loss increases** but **filter size decreases** — design trade-off. - [ ] **PWM advantages over PAM:** lower loss, smaller filter, better harmonic control, easier digital implementation. - [ ] **Comparator rule:** $v_r > v_c$ → $+V_s$ (high), $v_r < v_c$ → $-V_s$ (low) for bipolar; 0/+$V_s$ for unipolar. - [ ] **PWM applications:** VSI drives, DC–DC converters, UPS, Class-D amplifiers, solar MPPT, EV traction. - [ ] Examiner's pet trick: ask for *which $\delta$ eliminates the $k$-th harmonic* — answer is $\delta = m\pi/k$ for integer $m$ that lies within the feasible range.

topics.title: Applications (Concepts Only) - DC Motor Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications (Concepts Only) - DC Motor Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DC Motor Drive** is an electromechanical speed-torque control system that uses **power electronic converters** (rectifiers, choppers, or dual converters) to regulate the voltage or current supplied to a DC motor, thereby achieving precise control over its **speed**, **torque**, and **direction of rotation**. In the KTU 2024 Power Electronics framework, DC motor drives form the foundational industrial application that links static power conversion with dynamic mechanical load behaviour.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus here is strictly on **concepts** — the qualitative understanding of *why* and *how* a given converter topology drives a DC motor, the **four-quadrant operation** philosophy, and the **speed-torque characteristic** transformations produced by different firing angles ($\alpha$) or duty ratios ($D$).

The complete drive system consists of three integrated blocks:

1. **Power Electronic Converter** — The "muscle" of the drive (AC–DC rectifier, DC–DC chopper, or dual converter).
2. **DC Motor** — The "load" being controlled (separately excited, series, shunt, or compound).
3. **Controller / Firing Circuit** — The "brain" that decides firing angle $\alpha$ or duty ratio $D$ based on feedback signals.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water Tap" Analogy:**
> Imagine a DC motor as a **water wheel** in a garden channel.
> - The **water flow** = electrical power fed to the motor.
> - The **valve (tap)** = the power electronic converter (rectifier or chopper).
> - The **wheel's spinning speed** = the motor's shaft speed.
> - Opening the tap a little → less water → slow rotation. Opening it fully → fast rotation.
> - Similarly, increasing the **firing angle $\alpha$** of a converter → less average voltage → slow motor. Decreasing $\alpha$ → more voltage → fast motor.

Now imagine you have **two taps** (one for the field winding, one for the armature). You can independently control the **field strength** (water pressure) and the **armature flow** (water quantity). This dual control is exactly what makes separately excited DC motors the *most flexible* drives in industry.

### 1.3 Why DC Drives Still Matter in 2024+

> [!TIP]
> Even though AC drives (VFDs) dominate the market today, DC drives remain critical in:
> - **Rolling mills, paper mills, printing machines** — where wide, smooth speed range is needed.
> - **Electric traction (locomotives, EVs, cranes, elevators)** — because of excellent starting torque.
> - **Robotics and servo systems** — because of fast dynamic response.
> - **Battery-powered systems** — where DC–DC choppers naturally interface with battery voltage.

The typical **speed range** of a DC drive is **20 : 1** (constant torque region) plus another **3 : 1** in the field-weakening (constant power) region, giving a combined range of roughly **60 : 1**.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics (KTU Board Favourite Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Back EMF Constant** $K_b$ and **Torque Constant** $K_a$ are numerically equal in SI units: $K_a = K_b$.
> - **Standard armature supply voltages:** $230 \text{ V DC}$, $460 \text{ V DC}$, $600 \text{ V DC}$.
> - **Firing angle $\alpha$** range for full converter: $0° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$ (rectifier mode), $90° \leq \alpha \leq 180°$ (inverter mode).
> - **Duty ratio $D$** for chopper: $0 \leq D \leq 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DC Motor — Foundational Equations

Any DC motor, regardless of type, obeys these two universal laws:

**Law 1 — Voltage Equation (Armature Loop, Kirchhoff's Voltage Law):**

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$

**Law 2 — Back EMF (Faraday's Law for Rotating Machines):**

$$E_b = K_b \cdot \Phi \cdot \omega$$

**Law 3 — Electromagnetic Torque Developed:**

$$T = K_a \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three equations are the **backbone of every KTU numerical on DC drives**. Memorize them as the "Holy Trinity" of DC motor analysis.

### 2.2 The Two Speed Control Strategies — The "Heart" of DC Drives

Since $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$, we see **three knobs** that can change speed:

1. **Armature Voltage Control** $V_a$ — vary $V_a$, keep $\Phi$ constant. *(Most common in KTU problems.)*
2. **Field Flux Control** $\Phi$ — vary $\Phi$, keep $V_a$ constant. *(Field weakening region.)*
3. **Armature Resistance Control** $R_a$ — series resistance drop. *(Inefficient, rarely asked.)*

### 2.3 Converter Topologies for DC Drives — Conceptual Mapping

| Topology | Symbol | Speed Control Action | Quadrants Possible |
|----------|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Single-Phase Half-Wave Converter | Single SCR | Rectifier (1st quadrant only) | **1** |
| Single-Phase Semi-Converter | Two SCRs + Two Diodes | Rectifier, no inversion | **1** |
| Single-Phase Full Converter | Four SCRs (full bridge) | Rectifier + Inverter | **1 & 4** |
| Single-Phase Dual Converter | Two full converters antiparallel | Full four-quadrant | **1, 2, 3, 4** |
| Step-Down Chopper (Buck) | IGBT/MOSFET + diode | Motor mode (forward motoring) | **1** |
| Step-Up Chopper (Boost) | IGBT/MOSFET + diode | Regenerative braking | **2** |
| Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class A/B/C) | — | Forward motoring + braking | **1 & 2** |
| Four-Quadrant Chopper (Class D / H-bridge) | Four switches | Full bidirectional | **1, 2, 3, 4** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern Memory Aid:**
> "**Half converter = 1 quadrant, Full converter = 2 quadrants, Dual converter = 4 quadrants**" — this is a guaranteed 3-mark question every exam cycle.

### 2.4 The Four Quadrants of Operation

The mechanical plane of any motor can be divided into four quadrants based on the **signs of speed ($\omega$) and torque ($T$)**:

| Quadrant | Speed | Torque | Mode | Power Flow |
|----------|-------|--------|------|------------|
| **I — Forward Motoring** | +ve | +ve | Motor draws power | Source → Motor → Load |
| **II — Forward Braking** | +ve | –ve | Plugging / regen | Motor → Source |
| **III — Reverse Motoring** | –ve | –ve | Reverse motoring | Source → Motor → Load |
| **IV — Reverse Braking** | –ve | +ve | Reverse regen | Motor → Source |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrant II and IV are regenerative (braking) modes** — the motor acts as a generator, sending power back to the source. This is where **dual converters** or **Class D choppers** shine.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Operating Mode |
|----------|---------|------|----------------|
| Armature Voltage (avg), 1-$\phi$ half converter | $V_a = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | V | Discontinuous, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 180°$ |
| Armature Voltage (avg), 1-$\phi$ semi-converter | $V_a = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | V | Continuous, $0 \leq \alpha \leq 180°$ |
| Armature Voltage (avg), 1-$\phi$ full converter | $V_a = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | V | Rectifier $\alpha \in [0,90°]$, Inverter $\alpha \in [90°,180°]$ |
| Armature Voltage (avg), 3-$\phi$ full converter | $V_a = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | V | Same quadrant logic as above |
| Chopper Output Voltage | $V_a = D \cdot V_s$ | V | Buck mode, $0 \leq D \leq 1$ |
| Motor Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$ | rad/s | Universal |
| Developed Torque | $T = K_a \Phi I_a$ | N·m | Universal |
| Power Developed (mech.) | $P_m = T \omega = E_b I_a$ | W | Universal |
| Input Power (electrical) | $P_{in} = V_a I_a$ | W | Universal |
| Power Lost as Heat | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | W | Universal |
| Slip / Duty Ratio | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | — | Choppers |
| Field Current | $I_f = \dfrac{V_f}{R_f}$ | A | Constant for sepex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Rule:** Note that in the table above, absolute-value-style bars have been replaced with $\vert$ rendering. In your answer sheets, always write $\vert \cos\alpha \vert$ or use parentheses for clarity — this is the official KTU-recommended notation.

### 2.6 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Traction (locomotive) designers** use the full converter equation to compute average DC link voltage at every $\alpha$, ensuring torque demand is met at each notch of the driver's throttle.
- **EV battery management systems** use chopper duty-ratio equations to regulate the DC bus feeding an inverter that ultimately drives the wheels (indirectly).
- **Steel rolling mill engineers** rely on dual converters because the rolling process oscillates between motoring (metal being compressed) and regenerative braking (metal spring-back).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Output Voltage of a Single-Phase Full Converter

**Statement to prove:**

$$V_a = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$$

**Starting Point:** The full converter output voltage for a purely resistive load (or highly inductive load with continuous conduction) is a rectified sinusoid whose firing is delayed by $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Set up the integral of the supply voltage between firing angles $\alpha$ and $\pi+\alpha$:**

$$V_a = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Integrate $\sin(\omega t)$:**

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\omega t)\Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the limits:**

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[-\cos(\pi+\alpha) - (-\cos\alpha)\Big]$$

**Step 4 — Apply trigonometric identity $\cos(\pi+\alpha) = -\cos\alpha$:**

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[-(-\cos\alpha) + \cos\alpha\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$V_a = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\Big[2\cos\alpha\Big]$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_a = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic commentary:** When $\alpha = 0°$, $\cos\alpha = 1$, so $V_a = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ — the maximum uncontrolled rectifier output. When $\alpha = 90°$, $\cos\alpha = 0$, so $V_a = 0$ — the boundary between rectification and inversion. When $\alpha > 90°$, $V_a$ becomes negative, meaning **power flows back to the source** (inverter operation).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Speed Equation of a Separately Excited DC Motor Under Converter Control

**Statement to prove:**

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$$

**Step 1 — Write KVL around the armature loop:**

$$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Faraday's back-EMF relation $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$:**

$$V_a = K_b \Phi \omega + I_a R_a$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $\omega$:**

$$K_b \Phi \omega = V_a - I_a R_a$$

$$\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the converter output $V_a$ for a single-phase full converter:**

$$\omega = \frac{\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{\frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha - I_a R_a}{K_b \Phi}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** As firing angle $\alpha$ increases from $0° \to 90°$, $\cos\alpha$ decreases from $1 \to 0$, so $V_a$ decreases, and therefore $\omega$ decreases. This is the fundamental principle behind **phase-angle control of DC drives**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chopper Output Voltage (Step-Down / Buck)

**Statement to prove:**

$$V_a = D \cdot V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T}\,V_s$$

**Step 1 — Define the switching period and duty ratio:**

$$T = T_{on} + T_{off}, \qquad D = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

**Step 2 — The chopper output is $V_s$ when the switch is ON (duration $T_{on}$) and $0$ when the switch is OFF (duration $T_{off}$).**

**Step 3 — Compute the average output voltage over one period:**

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v_o(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{T}\Big[\int_0^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^T 0\, dt\Big]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals:**

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T}\Big[V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0\Big]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the definition of duty ratio:**

$$V_a = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T} = V_s \cdot D$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_a = D \cdot V_s}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Speed Range and Field Weakening

When $V_a$ is held at rated value and $\Phi$ is reduced (field weakening), the no-load speed **rises** above its rated value:

$$\omega_{NL} = \frac{V_a}{K_b \Phi} - \text{(small drop)}$$

**Step 1 — At rated flux $\Phi_{rated}$:**

$$\omega_{NL,\text{rated}} \approx \frac{V_{rated}}{K_b \Phi_{rated}}$$

**Step 2 — At reduced flux $\Phi = 0.5 \Phi_{rated}$ (i.e., $I_f$ halved):**

$$\omega_{NL,\text{reduced}} = \frac{V_{rated}}{K_b \cdot 0.5 \Phi_{rated}} = 2 \omega_{NL,\text{rated}}$$

**Interpretation:** Halving the field flux doubles the no-load speed — this is the basis of the **constant-power (field-weakening) region** of the speed-torque curve.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Converter Action on Speed-Torque Curve

| Control Method | What is varied | Effect on No-Load Speed | Effect on Rated Torque | Quadrants |
|----------------|----------------|------------------------|------------------------|-----------|
| Armature voltage (rectifier) | $V_a$ via $\alpha$ | Decreases as $\alpha \uparrow$ | Stays roughly constant (limited by $I_a$ and cooling) | 1 (or 1+4 with full conv.) |
| Field flux (field weakening) | $\Phi$ via $I_f$ | Increases as $\Phi \downarrow$ | Torque falls as $\Phi \downarrow$ | 1 (constant power) |
| Chopper (buck) duty cycle | $V_a$ via $D$ | Decreases as $D \downarrow$ | Roughly constant | 1 |
| Chopper + reverse switches | $V_a$ polarity | Both directions | Constant | 1, 2, 3, 4 (Class D) |
| Dual converter | $\alpha_1$ (positive), $\alpha_2 = 180°-\alpha_1$ (negative) | Both directions, full control | Constant | 1, 2, 3, 4 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — General DC Motor Drive Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACS[AC Supply or DC Source] --> CONV[Power Electronic Converter]
    CONV --> ARM[Armature Circuit]
    CONV --> FIELD[Field Winding]
    ARM --> MOTOR[DC Motor Shaft]
    FIELD --> MOTOR
    MOTOR --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    MOTOR -.Speed Sensor.-> FB[Feedback Controller]
    FB -.Firing Pulses.-> CONV
```

> [!NOTE]
> The feedback loop is the *closed-loop* control. In open-loop (manual $\alpha$ or $D$ setting), the converter simply executes the command without verifying the actual speed.

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Converter Topology Selection

```mermaid
graph TD
    START([Application Requirement]) --> Q{Qualitative Quadrant Needs}
    Q -- Forward Motoring only --> H[1-Phase Half Converter]
    Q -- Forward + Plugging --> F[1-Phase Full Converter]
    Q -- All Four Quadrants --> D[1-Phase Dual Converter]
    Q -- Battery Powered --> C[Class D Chopper H-Bridge]
    H --> M[Motor Drives Quadrant 1]
    F --> M2[Motor Drives Quadrants 1 and 4]
    D --> M3[Motor Drives All Four Quadrants]
    C --> M4[Motor Drives All Four Quadrants]
```

### 4.3 Four-Quadrant Operation Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph Q1 [Quadrant I Forward Motoring]
        Q1A[+ Speed, + Torque]
    end
    subgraph Q2 [Quadrant II Forward Braking Regenerative]
        Q2A[+ Speed, - Torque]
    end
    subgraph Q3 [Quadrant III Reverse Motoring]
        Q3A[- Speed, - Torque]
    end
    subgraph Q4 [Quadrant IV Reverse Braking Regenerative]
        Q4A[- Speed, + Torque]
    end
    Q1 -- Reduce firing angle or reverse voltage --> Q2
    Q2 -- Reverse field or armature --> Q3
    Q3 -- Regen braking --> Q4
    Q4 -- Return to forward --> Q1
```

### 4.4 Closed-Loop Speed Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    REQ[Reference Speed omegaRef] --> SUM[Junction Summing Point]
    SUM --> CTRL[PI Controller]
    CTRL --> FP[Firing Pulse Generator alpha or D]
    FP --> CONV[Converter]
    CONV --> MOTOR[DC Motor]
    MOTOR --> SPEED[Shaft Speed omegaActual]
    MOTOR -.Torque.-> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    SPEED -- Sensor --> FBK[Feedback Path]
    FBK --> SUM
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The PI (Proportional-Integral) controller is the heart of every industrial DC drive. Its job is to minimise the *error* $e = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}$ continuously, producing a smooth firing command $\alpha(t)$ or $D(t)$.

### 4.5 Speed-Torque Characteristic Curves (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph A [Armature Voltage Control]
        A1[Line 1: V_a1 rated] --> A2[Line 2: V_a2 lower]
        A2 --> A3[Line 3: V_a3 even lower]
    end
    subgraph B [Field Flux Control Field Weakening]
        B1[Line 1: Phi rated] --> B2[Line 2: Phi halved]
        B2 --> B3[Line 3: Phi one third]
    end
```

### 4.6 Power Flow Diagram — Motoring vs. Braking

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source] -- Motoring P in --> CONV[Converter]
    CONV -- P to Motor --> MOTOR[Motor]
    MOTOR -- Mechanical P out --> LOAD[Load]
    MOTOR -.Regenerative Braking.-> CONV
    CONV -.Returns P to Source.-> SRC
```

> [!TIP]
> Notice that the arrows **reverse direction** during regenerative braking — the motor acts as a generator, and the converter acts as an inverter, feeding power back into the source. This is the magic of **dual converters** and **Class D choppers**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q1.** What is a DC motor drive? List any **two advantages** of using power electronic converters in DC motor speed control over traditional rheostatic control.

**Model Answer:**

A DC motor drive is a system that uses a **power electronic converter** to regulate the voltage or current supplied to a DC motor, achieving controlled speed and torque. Two advantages over rheostatic control:
1. **Higher efficiency** — no continuous power dissipation in a series resistor.
2. **Smooth, stepless, and precise** control via firing angle $\alpha$ or duty ratio $D$.
3. **Fast dynamic response** — closed-loop control with feedback enables rapid corrections.
4. **Compactness** — semiconductor switches are far smaller than rheostats.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **single-phase semi-converter** and **single-phase full-converter** drives for DC motors in terms of **quadrants of operation** and **regenerative braking capability**.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Semi-Converter Drive | Full Converter Drive |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| No. of SCRs | 2 SCRs + 2 Diodes | 4 SCRs |
| Quadrants | **1 only** (forward motoring) | **2 quadrants** (forward motoring + forward regen braking) |
| Polarity of $V_a$ | Always positive | Can be positive or negative (depends on $\alpha$) |
| Regen braking | Not possible | Possible, $\alpha > 90°$ |
| $V_a$ equation | $\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1+\cos\alpha)$ | $\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| Applications | Small fans, pumps | Elevators, hoists |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a closed-loop DC motor drive** using a single-phase full converter. Discuss the role of the **PI controller** and **speed feedback**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **single-phase full converter** feeds a separately excited DC motor with **$V_s = 230 \text{ V}$, $50\text{ Hz}$**, and the motor has $R_a = 0.5 \ \Omega$, $K_b \Phi = 1.2 \text{ V·s/rad}$, and field is held constant. For $\alpha = 60°$, compute the **average armature voltage**, **back EMF**, and **no-load speed** in rad/s. Take armature current at no-load as $I_a = 2 \text{ A}$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Block diagram** (refer Section 4.4): Reference speed $\omega_{ref}$ → Comparator → PI controller → Firing pulse generator $\alpha$ → Single-phase full converter → DC motor → Shaft speed $\omega_{actual}$.
2. **PI controller** produces an output proportional to error $e = \omega_{ref} - \omega_{actual}$ and its integral. It adjusts $\alpha$ so that error tends to zero in steady state.
3. **Speed feedback** (tachogenerator or encoder) ensures actual speed follows reference.
4. **Advantage:** Provides zero steady-state error, good transient response, and load disturbance rejection.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Peak supply voltage:**

$$V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_s = \sqrt{2} \cdot 230 = 325.27 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Average armature voltage of full converter:**

$$V_a = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{2 \cdot 325.27}{\pi}\cos 60°$$

$$V_a = \frac{650.54}{3.14159} \cdot 0.5 = 207.07 \cdot 0.5 = 103.53 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Back EMF from armature equation:**

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 103.53 - (2)(0.5) = 103.53 - 1 = 102.53 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — No-load speed:**

$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{102.53}{1.2} = 85.44 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega \text{ in rpm} = 85.44 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = 815.84 \text{ rpm}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **principle of four-quadrant operation** of a DC motor drive using a **dual converter**. Sketch the **block diagram** and describe how **circulating current mode** and **non-circulating current mode** differ. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **step-down chopper** drives a separately excited DC motor with supply $V_s = 220 \text{ V}$, motor constants $K_b \Phi = 0.8 \text{ V·s/rad}$, $R_a = 0.4 \ \Omega$. For **duty ratio $D = 0.6$** and armature current $I_a = 30 \text{ A}$, calculate the **armature voltage**, **back EMF**, and **motor speed in rpm**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Four-quadrant operation** requires both magnitude AND direction control of armature voltage and current. A **dual converter** (two full converters connected in antiparallel) achieves this.
2. **Converter 1 (positive group):** fires at $\alpha_1$, provides $+V_a$.
3. **Converter 2 (negative group):** fires at $\alpha_2 = 180° - \alpha_1$, provides $-V_a$.
4. **Quadrant I:** Converter 1 in rectifier mode, $V_a$ positive, current positive → forward motoring.
5. **Quadrant II:** Converter 2 in inverter mode, $V_a$ negative, current positive → forward regen braking.
6. **Quadrant III:** Converter 2 in rectifier mode, $V_a$ negative, current negative → reverse motoring.
7. **Quadrant IV:** Converter 1 in inverter mode, $V_a$ positive, current negative → reverse regen braking.
8. **Circulating current mode:** Both converters conduct simultaneously — current circulates between them; smooth transition, no dead time, but requires reactors.
9. **Non-circulating current mode:** Only one converter conducts at a time — logic switching needed; no circulating losses but slight dead time.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Armature voltage:**

$$V_a = D \cdot V_s = 0.6 \cdot 220 = 132 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Back EMF:**

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 132 - (30)(0.4) = 132 - 12 = 120 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Motor speed in rad/s:**

$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_b \Phi} = \frac{120}{0.8} = 150 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to rpm:**

$$N = 150 \cdot \frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{9000}{6.2832} \approx 1432.4 \text{ rpm}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting $K_b \Phi = K_a \Phi$** — students often use different symbols, leading to unit mismatch.
> 2. **Mixing up $V_m$ and $V_{rms}$** — KTU always expects $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{rms}$ when the supply is given in RMS.
> 3. **Sign error in quadrant analysis** — remember, in motoring, $V_a$ and $I_a$ are **same sign**; in regen, they are **opposite sign**.
> 4. **Not converting rad/s to rpm** — KTU board examiners deduct 1 mark if final answer is in wrong unit.
> 5. **Confusing chopper equations** — Step-down chopper gives $V_a = DV_s$, step-up gives $V_a = V_s / (1-D)$.
> 6. **Skipping block diagrams** — a question on "explain with block diagram" without a diagram will lose at least 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **DC motor drive** = power electronic converter + DC motor + control circuit.
- ✅ Three universal equations: $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$, $E_b = K_b \Phi \omega$, $T = K_a \Phi I_a$.
- ✅ **Speed control methods:** armature voltage, field flux, armature resistance (KTU rarely asks the last).
- ✅ **Half converter → 1 quadrant**, **Full converter → 2 quadrants**, **Dual converter → 4 quadrants**.
- ✅ **Field-weakening** raises no-load speed but reduces torque — constant power region.
- ✅ **Chopper equation:** $V_a = D \cdot V_s$ for buck; $V_a = V_s / (1-D)$ for boost.
- ✅ **Full converter:** $V_a = (2V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$.
- ✅ **Three-phase full converter:** $V_a = (3\sqrt{3}V_m/\pi)\cos\alpha$.
- ✅ **Four quadrants:** Q1 forward motoring, Q2 forward braking (regen), Q3 reverse motoring, Q4 reverse braking.
- ✅ **PI controller** gives zero steady-state error in closed-loop drives.
- ✅ **Dual converter modes:** circulating current (smooth, with reactors) vs non-circulating (no overlap, dead time).
- ✅ **Applications:** rolling mills, paper machines, traction, elevators, cranes, servo systems.
- ✅ **Typical speed range:** 20:1 constant torque + 3:1 field weakening = ~60:1 total.
- ✅ $K_a = K_b$ in SI units — a frequently tested identity.
- ✅ Always state whether $\alpha$ is in rectifier or inverter region when solving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Induction Motor Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Induction Motor Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Induction Motor Drive** is an integrated electromechanical system that combines a **three-phase (or single-phase) AC induction motor** with a **power electronic converter/controller** to achieve precise, efficient, and programmable control of speed, torque, and direction of rotation, in accordance with the load demand.

A *drive*, in the strict KTU engineering sense, consists of three inseparable functional blocks:
1. **Power Electronic Converter** (Rectifier → DC Link → Inverter, or AC-AC Converter)
2. **Control Unit** (Microcontroller / DSP / Analog circuitry)
3. **Electric Motor** (Squirrel Cage or Slip-Ring Induction Motor)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Approximately **60–70%** of all industrial motors in the world are **3-phase squirrel-cage induction motors** because of their rugged construction, low cost, and maintenance-free operation. (Bolded key industry metric.)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Tap and the Faucet"

Imagine a **water tap** connected to a high-pressure water pipeline.
- The **water pressure** = Voltage (V) supplied to the stator.
- The **tap opening** = Frequency (f) of the supply.
- The **water flow rate** = Speed (N) of the motor.
- The **pipe diameter (bottleneck)** = Rotor resistance.

| If You... | Motor Behaviour | Real-World Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reduce the pressure alone | Torque drops, motor stalls | V reduced, f constant → poor operation |
| Reduce the flow rate (tap) | Flow rate decreases, pressure maintained | f reduced → speed reduces |
| Reduce both pressure and flow | Smooth, efficient speed reduction | **V/f control** (the holy grail) |
| Add a partial blockage in the pipe | Flow rate drops with more friction | Adding rotor resistance (slip-ring) |

> [!TIP]
> **The Golden Rule of Induction Motor Drives:** To maintain **constant flux (Φ)** and therefore **constant torque capability**, the ratio **V/f must be held constant** at all speeds. This is the single most tested concept in KTU Module 4.

---

### 1.3 Classification of Induction Motor Drives

```
INDUCTION MOTOR DRIVES
├── 1. Stator Side Control
│       ├── (a) Stator Voltage Control (AC Voltage Controller)
│       └── (b) Variable Frequency Control (VFD / VSI / CSI)
│
├── 2. Rotor Side Control (Slip-Ring / Wound Rotor Only)
│       ├── (a) Static Rotor Resistance Control
│       ├── (b) Slip Power Recovery (Kramer System)
│       └── (c) Scherbius System (Static Scherbius)
│
└── 3. Combined V/f + Vector Control (Advanced / Out of basic scope)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 4 focus is on **stator voltage control**, **variable frequency (V/f) control**, **PWM inverter-fed drives**, and **slip power recovery systems** (Kramer and Scherbius).

---

### 1.4 Why Do We Need Induction Motor Drives?

Direct-on-line (DOL) starting of an induction motor creates problems:
- **High inrush current** (typically **5 to 7 times** the rated current).
- **Mechanical shock** to the driven load (belts, gears, conveyor).
- **Poor speed regulation** — induction motors are essentially **constant-speed machines** at fixed frequency (slip varies only 1–5%).
- **No soft starting**, no braking, no reversal flexibility.

> A **Power Electronic Drive** solves all four problems elegantly using semiconductor switches (SCR, IGBT, MOSFET, GTO).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Equations of an Induction Motor

#### (A) Synchronous Speed
The rotating magnetic field produced by the 3-phase stator rotates at a fixed speed determined only by supply frequency and number of poles.

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P}$$

Where:
- $N_s$ = Synchronous speed in **rpm**
- $f$ = Supply frequency in **Hz**
- $P$ = Total number of poles (always even)

#### (B) Slip
Slip is the relative difference between synchronous speed and actual rotor speed, expressed as a per-unit or percentage.

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

#### (C) Rotor Frequency
The frequency of induced EMF in the rotor is directly proportional to slip.

$$f_r = s \cdot f$$

#### (D) Air-Gap Power Flow (Power Stages)

$$P_{in} \rightarrow P_{ag} \rightarrow P_{cu,rotor} \rightarrow P_{mech} \rightarrow P_{out}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $P_{ag} = \dfrac{P_{cu,rotor}}{s}$ and $P_{mech} = P_{ag} (1 - s) = \dfrac{P_{cu,rotor}(1-s)}{s}$

#### (E) Electromagnetic Torque
The torque developed by an induction motor is given by the classic KTU formula:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2}{\left( \dfrac{R_r}{s} \right)^2 + X^2} \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}$$

Where:
- $V$ = per-phase stator voltage
- $R_r, X$ = rotor resistance and standstill reactance (referred to stator)
- $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / P/2$ = synchronous speed in rad/s

#### (F) Condition for Maximum Torque
Maximum (breakdown) torque occurs when $\dfrac{R_r}{s} = X$, i.e., at a critical slip:

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_r}{X}$$

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X} \;\; \text{(independent of } R_r\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pearl:** $T_{max}$ depends **only on V²** and **not on rotor resistance**. Adding rotor resistance **shifts** the max-torque point toward higher slip without changing $T_{max}$ magnitude.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | rpm | Sets the no-load ceiling speed |
| 2 | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | per-unit / % | Determines rotor copper loss fraction |
| 3 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s \cdot f$ | Hz | Influences rotor reactance at running |
| 4 | Air-gap power | $P_{ag} = T_e \cdot \omega_s$ | W | Power crossing the air-gap |
| 5 | Rotor copper loss | $P_{cu,r} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | W | Heat dissipated in rotor bars |
| 6 | Mechanical power developed | $P_{m} = (1-s) P_{ag}$ | W | Available at the shaft (less friction) |
| 7 | Max torque condition | $R_r / s = X$ | — | Critical slip for breakdown torque |
| 8 | Max torque value | $T_{max} = 3V^2 / (2 \omega_s X)$ | N·m | Independent of $R_r$ |
| 9 | V/f ratio (constant flux) | $V / f = \text{constant} = k\Phi$ | V/Hz | Core of VFD design |
| 10 | Per-phase equivalent EMF | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi \, k_w$ | V | Flux $\Phi$ depends on V/f |
| 11 | Slip power (recoverable) | $P_{slip} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ | W | Energy fed back via Kramer/Scherbius |
| 12 | Stator voltage (reduced) | $V = k \cdot V_s$ | V | $k$ = firing angle factor for AC controller |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Drive Method Used | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cranes / Hoists** | Kramer / Scherbius | Energy recovery during lowering |
| **Pumps / Fans** | V/f PWM VFD | Massive energy savings (Affinity Laws) |
| **Conveyor Belts** | Soft-starter (AC voltage controller) | Smooth start, no jerk |
| **Traction (Trains)** | V/f + Vector | Wide speed range, high starting torque |
| **Wind Energy** | Double-fed IM (Scherbius) | Variable speed generation |

> [!NOTE]
> According to industry studies, replacing DOL pumps/fans with **VFDs** typically saves **30–50%** electrical energy. (Bolded key industry statistic.)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Air-Gap Power and Rotor Copper Loss Relationship

**Statement to prove:** $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag}$

**Starting Point — Power Crossing the Air Gap:**
The power transferred electromagnetically across the air-gap from stator to rotor is the air-gap power, $P_{ag}$. This entire power must be dissipated in the rotor circuit resistance (at standstill) or partly converted to mechanical form (when running).

**Step 1:** Express the rotor circuit equivalent resistance as a function of slip.

At standstill, the rotor frequency equals stator frequency $f$. The rotor resistance is $R_r$ and standstill reactance is $X_2$.

When the rotor is rotating at slip $s$, the rotor frequency is $f_r = sf$, and the rotor reactance becomes $X_{2r} = s X_2$, while the resistance remains $R_r$ (rotor resistance does not depend on frequency).

The per-phase rotor current referred to the stator is:

$$I_r' = \frac{V}{\sqrt{ \left( R_s + \dfrac{R_r'}{s} \right)^2 + (X_s + X_2')^2 }}$$

For simplicity, neglecting stator impedance drop (KTU assumption for short analysis):

$$I_r' \approx \frac{V}{ \sqrt{ \left( \dfrac{R_r'}{s} \right)^2 + X_2'^2 } }$$

**Step 2:** Compute the air-gap power per phase as the power transferred to the rotor circuit.

$$P_{ag,\phi} = I_r'^2 \cdot \frac{R_r'}{s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the actual rotor copper loss per phase (real $I^2R$ loss at standstill resistance).

$$P_{cu,\phi} = I_r'^2 \cdot R_r'$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio of rotor copper loss to air-gap power.

$$\frac{P_{cu,\phi}}{P_{ag,\phi}} = \frac{I_r'^2 \cdot R_r'}{I_r'^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r'}{s}} = s$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, multiplying throughout by 3 (for 3-phase) and recognizing the ratio holds per phase:

$$\boxed{P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Since $s$ is small (typically 0.02 to 0.05) at normal operation, only a small fraction of $P_{ag}$ is wasted as rotor heat. This is precisely why slip-power recovery systems (Kramer/Scherbius) can recover enormous energy at high slips.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Condition for Maximum Torque

**Step 1:** The electromagnetic torque expression per-phase is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2}{ \left( \dfrac{R_r'}{s} \right)^2 + X^2 } \cdot \dfrac{R_r'}{s}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $T_e$ with respect to slip $s$ and equate to zero for the maximum.

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = 0$$

Let $y = R_r'/s$. Then $dy/ds = -R_r'/s^2$.

The torque becomes:

$$T_e = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{y}{y^2 + X^2}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $s$.

$$\frac{dT_e}{ds} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{(y^2 + X^2)\frac{dy}{ds} - y \cdot 2y \frac{dy}{ds}}{(y^2 + X^2)^2} = 0$$

The numerator must vanish:

$$(y^2 + X^2)\frac{dy}{ds} - 2y^2 \frac{dy}{ds} = 0$$

$$(X^2 - y^2)\frac{dy}{ds} = 0$$

Since $dy/ds \neq 0$ (we're not at standstill), we must have $X^2 = y^2$, i.e., $y = X$.

**Step 4:** Substitute back $y = R_r'/s$:

$$\boxed{\frac{R_r'}{s_{mT}} = X \quad \Rightarrow \quad s_{mT} = \frac{R_r'}{X}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $s_{mT}$ into the torque equation to obtain $T_{max}$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{X}{X^2 + X^2} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X}$$

$$\boxed{T_{max} = \frac{3 V^2}{2 \omega_s X}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** $T_{max}$ is **independent of rotor resistance $R_r$** — a result of central importance in slip-ring motor control. Changing $R_r$ only shifts the *location* of max-torque along the slip axis, not its magnitude.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: V/f Control — Why It Maintains Constant Flux

**Step 1:** The RMS value of the induced EMF per phase in the stator winding is governed by the transformer EMF equation (same as for a stator winding excited by a rotating field):

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi \, k_w$$

**Step 2:** Neglecting the small stator impedance drop, $V \approx E$, therefore:

$$V \approx 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi \, k_w$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the air-gap flux $\Phi$:

$$\Phi = \frac{V}{4.44 \, f \, N \, k_w}$$

**Step 4:** All terms $4.44, N, k_w$ are constants for a given machine, so:

$$\Phi \propto \frac{V}{f}$$

**Step 5:** To keep flux $\Phi$ constant (and thus keep torque capability unchanged), the ratio $V/f$ must be a constant:

$$\boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = k}$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, if we reduce frequency $f$ from 50 Hz to 25 Hz, we must reduce voltage $V$ to exactly half its rated value, in order to keep flux at its rated (nominal) value.

> [!TIP]
> At very low frequencies (below ~5 Hz), the stator resistance drop $I_s R_s$ becomes significant and the simple V/f rule under-fluxes the motor. The practical fix is to add a small **voltage boost** (also called *torque boost*) in the low-frequency region.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Slip Power and Kramer System Recovery

**Step 1:** The power lost as rotor copper loss when running at slip $s$ is:

$$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag}$$

**Step 2:** This rotor copper loss appears across the slip-ring terminals. If a portion $k$ of the slip power is recovered and returned to the supply:

$$P_{recovered} = k \cdot s \cdot P_{ag}$$

**Step 3:** The net "effective" rotor copper loss in the Kramer system becomes:

$$P_{cu,eff} = (1 - k) \cdot s \cdot P_{ag}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore the operating slip is reduced by a factor $(1-k)$, giving:

$$s_{Kramer} = s_{DOL} (1 - k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This proves that slip-power recovery allows a slip-ring motor to operate at **subsynchronous speeds** with high efficiency — the recovered energy is no longer burned as heat in the rotor.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor has rotor resistance $R_2 = 0.2 \;\Omega$ and standstill rotor reactance $X_2 = 1.0 \;\Omega$ per phase. At a certain load, slip $s = 0.04$. Find:
- (a) Synchronous speed $N_s$
- (b) Rotor speed $N_r$
- (c) Rotor frequency $f_r$
- (d) The slip at which maximum torque occurs

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Synchronous speed

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (b):** Rotor speed

$$N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 1500 \cdot (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \cdot 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (c):** Rotor frequency

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \cdot 50 = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

**Part (d):** Critical slip for maximum torque

$$s_{mT} = \frac{R_2}{X_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2 = 20\%$$

Since $s_{mT} = 0.2 \gg s = 0.04$, the motor is operating in the **stable linear region** of the torque-slip curve (good operating point).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: V/f Controlled PWM Inverter-Fed Induction Motor Drive

This is the **most commonly tested block diagram** in KTU Module 4. It represents the architecture of a modern Variable Frequency Drive (VFD).

```mermaid
graph TD
    ACMains[AC Mains Supply 50Hz] --> Rectifier[Three Phase Diode Rectifier]
    Rectifier --> DCLink[DC Link Filter Capacitor]
    DCLink --> Inverter[Voltage Source Inverter VSI PWM]
    Inverter --> IM[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    Inverter --> CurSensor[Current Sensors]
    CurSensor --> ControlUnit[Control Unit DSP Microcontroller]
    SpeedSensor[Speed Encoder] --> ControlUnit
    ReferenceInput[Speed Reference Potentiometer] --> ControlUnit
```
