```mermaid
    ControlUnit --> Inverter
```

**Explanation of the block flow:**
1. **AC Mains** → fixed 50 Hz, 415 V 3-phase supply.
2. **Diode Rectifier** → converts AC to DC (uncontrolled, simple, rugged).
3. **DC Link Capacitor** → filters the rectified DC into a smooth DC bus.
4. **PWM Inverter (VSI)** → chops the DC into variable-voltage, variable-frequency AC using IGBTs at high switching frequency (typically 2–16 kHz).
5. **Induction Motor** → receives variable V/f AC and runs at the commanded speed.
6. **Sensors** → feedback current and rotor speed.
7. **Control Unit** → computes PWM gating signals using V/f law.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Kramer Slip-Power Recovery System

The Kramer system uses a **diode rectifier + DC link + line-commutated inverter** to feed the slip-frequency power back to the AC mains.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRM[Slip Ring Induction Motor] --> SR[Slip Rings]
    SR --> DBR[Diode Bridge Rectifier]
    DBR --> DCLinkK[DC Link Inductor L]
    DCLinkK --> INV[Line Commutated Inverter]
    INV --> Transformer[Step Down Transformer]
    Transformer --> ACSupply[AC Mains Supply]
    ACSupply --> SRM
    ControlK[Inverter Firing Angle Control] --> INV
```

**Working Principle in Words:**
- Slip-frequency AC is taken from the rotor slip rings.
- A diode bridge rectifies it into DC.
- A line-commutated thyristor inverter inverts the DC back to AC at mains frequency.
- The recovered power is fed back to the supply via a transformer.
- The **firing angle of the inverter** controls the *amount* of power recovered → which controls the motor's subsynchronous speed.

> [!NOTE]
> The Kramer system is restricted to **subsynchronous operation** (speed below synchronous) because the diode bridge is unidirectional.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Static Scherbius System (Dual Converter)

The Scherbius system uses a **forced-commutated or PWM converter** that can handle bidirectional power flow, allowing both **sub- and super-synchronous** operation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    SlipRings --> FCR[Force Commutated Rectifier PWM]
    FCR --> DCBus[DC Link Inductor]
    DCBus --> FCI[Force Commutated Inverter PWM]
    FCI --> MainsStep[Transformer]
    MainsStep --> ACSupply
    ACSupply --> StatorOfMotor
    StatorOfMotor --> SRM
```

**Comparison Summary:**

| Feature | Kramer System | Static Scherbius |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Converter type | Diode + Line-commutated inverter | Force-commutated / PWM bidirectional |
| Speed range | Subsynchronous only | Sub- and super-synchronous |
| Power flow direction | Rotor → Mains only | Rotor ↔ Mains (bidirectional) |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Harmonic pollution | Higher | Lower (PWM) |

---

### 4.4 Schematic: AC Voltage Controller for Stator Voltage Control

For soft starting and limited speed control of fan/pump-type loads (where torque ∝ N²), an AC voltage controller using back-to-back thyristors per phase is used.

```mermaid
graph TD
    SupplyR[Phase R AC Supply] --> TR1[Thyristor 1]
    TR1 --> LoadR[Motor Stator Phase R]
    LoadR --> TR2[Thyristor 2]
    TR2 --> SupplyR
    SupplyY[Phase Y] --> TY1[Thyristor 3]
    TY1 --> LoadY[Stator Phase Y]
    LoadY --> TY2[Thyristor 4]
    TY2 --> SupplyY
    SupplyB[Phase B] --> TB1[Thyristor 5]
    TB1 --> LoadB[Stator Phase B]
    LoadB --> TB2[Thyristor 6]
    TB2 --> SupplyB
    GateDriver[Firing Circuit Alpha Control] --> TR1
    GateDriver --> TR2
    GateDriver --> TY1
    GateDriver --> TY2
    GateDriver --> TB1
    GateDriver --> TB2
```

**Working Principle:**
- Each phase has **two antiparallel thyristors** (one for positive half-cycle, one for negative).
- Firing angle **α** is varied from 0° to 180°.
- The RMS voltage across the motor stator is:

$$V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{ 1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi} }$$

- As **α increases**, the RMS voltage to the motor **decreases**, reducing speed and torque.

> [!WARNING]
> Stator voltage control works well only for **fan and pump loads** (quadratic torque loads). For constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists), this method is unsuitable because the motor cannot develop rated torque at reduced voltage.

---

### 4.5 Power Flow Diagram: Stages in an Induction Motor

```mermaid
graph LR
    Pin[Pin Electrical Input to Stator] --> PstatorLoss[Stator Copper Loss 3 Is2 Rs]
    PstatorLoss --> Pag[Pag Air Gap Power]
    Pag --> ProtorCuLoss[Rotor Copper Loss s Pag]
    Pag --> Pmech[Mechanical Power Developed 1 minus s Pag]
    Pmech --> Pfriction[Friction and Windage Loss]
    Pfriction --> Pshaft[Shaft Output Power Pout]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- $P_{ag} = P_{in} - P_{stator,cu}$
- $P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ (slip-dependent heat)
- $P_{mech} = (1-s) P_{ag}$ (available as shaft work)
- $P_{out} = P_{mech} - P_{friction}$
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **What is meant by V/f control in induction motor drives? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
V/f control is a speed-control technique in which the stator voltage $V$ and supply frequency $f$ are varied together such that the ratio $V/f$ remains constant. From the EMF equation $E = 4.44 f N \Phi k_w$, flux $\Phi \propto V/f$. Maintaining V/f = constant keeps the air-gap flux at its rated value, ensuring that the motor can deliver its rated torque over the entire speed range. Without this ratio control, reducing frequency alone would increase flux, saturating the magnetic circuit, while reducing voltage alone would weaken flux and reduce torque capability. *(3 marks: definition 1M, EMF relation 1M, necessity 1M.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State the condition for maximum torque in a 3-phase induction motor. Why is the maximum torque independent of rotor resistance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The condition for maximum (breakdown) torque is that the rotor resistance referred to slip equals the standstill rotor reactance, i.e., $R_r/s_{mT} = X$, giving $s_{mT} = R_r/X$. Substituting into the torque expression yields $T_{max} = 3V^2 / (2 \omega_s X)$, which contains no $R_r$ term. Mathematically, the $R_r$ terms cancel out when the maximum condition is enforced. Physically, increasing $R_r$ shifts the maximum-torque point to higher slip but does not raise the torque ceiling because the increased resistance simultaneously reduces rotor current. *(3 marks: condition 1M, formula 1M, physical reason 1M.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> **Question A.** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **V/f Controlled PWM Inverter Drive**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **variable frequency PWM inverter-fed 3-phase induction motor drive**. Discuss how V/f control is implemented and the role of the rectifier, DC link, and inverter. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V induction motor is to be operated from a PWM VFD at 25 Hz. The motor has a V/f ratio of 415/50 = 8.3 V/Hz. Find:
- (i) The voltage to be applied at 25 Hz
- (ii) The new synchronous speed
- (iii) The expected rotor speed at slip 0.04 *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Operation:**

The VFD consists of the following blocks in cascade:
1. **3-phase Diode Rectifier**: Converts fixed 50 Hz mains AC into a constant DC voltage. The output is unregulated DC ≈ 415 × 1.35 ≈ 560 V DC.
2. **DC Link Capacitor (C)**: Smoothes the rectified DC into a stiff DC bus. Also acts as an energy reservoir for instantaneous power exchange.
3. **PWM Inverter (VSI)**: Uses 6 IGBTs (or MOSFETs) arranged in 3 legs. The IGBTs are switched using sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) or space-vector PWM (SVPWM) to synthesize a variable-voltage, variable-frequency quasi-sinusoidal AC output. The switching frequency is typically 2–16 kHz.
4. **Control Unit**: A DSP or microcontroller reads the speed command (reference frequency $f^*$) and computes the modulating signal. It enforces the V/f = constant law.
5. **Feedback**: Speed sensor (encoder) and current sensors provide closed-loop feedback.

**V/f Law Implementation:**
- Reference frequency $f^*$ is set by the user (e.g., 25 Hz).
- The control unit computes the required reference voltage $V^* = (V_{rated}/f_{rated}) \times f^* = 8.3 \times 25$ V/Hz.
- The PWM modulator generates gating pulses whose modulation index $m = V^*/V_{DC}$ produces the desired RMS line-to-line voltage at the inverter output.
- The fundamental frequency of the PWM waveform equals $f^*$.

**Advantages**: smooth speed control, energy efficient, no inrush current, regeneration possible.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V, V/f = 8.3 V/Hz

**(i) Voltage at 25 Hz:**

$$V_{25} = \text{V/f ratio} \times f_{new} = 8.3 \times 25 = 207.5 \text{ V (line-to-line)}$$

**(ii) New Synchronous Speed at 25 Hz:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \cdot 25}{4} = 750 \text{ rpm}$$

**(iii) Rotor Speed at Slip 0.04:**

$$N_r = N_s(1 - s) = 750 \times (1 - 0.04) = 750 \times 0.96 = 720 \text{ rpm}$$

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice).** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Slip Power Recovery — Kramer System**

**(a)** Explain with a block diagram the operation of the **Kramer slip-power recovery system** for speed control of a slip-ring induction motor below synchronous speed. Derive the expression for the recovered slip power. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz slip-ring induction motor has an air-gap power of 60 kW at a slip of 0.05. Calculate:
- (i) Rotor copper loss
- (ii) Mechanical power developed
- (iii) The recovered slip power if a Kramer system recovers 70% of the slip power
- (iv) The effective new operating slip *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Kramer System Operation:**

The Kramer system is a **subsynchronous cascade** that recovers the slip-frequency rotor power and feeds it back to the AC mains, instead of wasting it in external rotor resistances.

**Block diagram:** Stator ← Mains; Rotor slip-rings → Diode bridge rectifier → DC link inductor (smoothing) → Line-commutated thyristor inverter → Step-down transformer → AC Mains.

**Working:**
1. The 3-phase rotor EMF at frequency $f_r = sf$ is taken out via slip rings.
2. The diode bridge rectifies this AC into DC. The DC link inductor ensures a near-ripple-free DC current.
3. The thyristor inverter inverts this DC back to AC at mains frequency, allowing it to be synchronized with the supply.
4. The firing angle α of the inverter determines the back-EMF of the inverter, which controls how much power is pumped back. Larger α → less recovery → higher slip → lower speed.
5. A transformer matches the inverter output voltage to the mains.

**Derivation of Recovered Slip Power:**

The slip-ring power is $P_{slip} = s \cdot P_{ag}$. If a fraction $k$ of this slip power is recovered:

$$P_{recovered} = k \cdot s \cdot P_{ag}$$

The remaining (1-k) fraction is dissipated in the rotor circuit:

$$P_{cu,eff} = (1 - k) \cdot s \cdot P_{ag}$$

**Advantages of Kramer:** Subsynchronous speed control without external resistors → high efficiency, smooth speed control.

**Disadvantages:** Limited to subsynchronous; introduces harmonics; cannot operate above synchronous speed.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** 6-pole, 50 Hz, $P_{ag} = 60$ kW, $s = 0.05$, $k = 0.7$

**(i) Rotor Copper Loss:**

$$P_{cu,rotor} = s \cdot P_{ag} = 0.05 \times 60 = 3 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Mechanical Power Developed:**

$$P_{mech} = (1 - s) P_{ag} = 0.95 \times 60 = 57 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Recovered Slip Power:**

$$P_{recovered} = k \cdot s \cdot P_{ag} = 0.7 \times 3 = 2.1 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Effective New Operating Slip:**

$$s_{new} = s_{old} (1 - k) = 0.05 \times (1 - 0.7) = 0.05 \times 0.3 = 0.015$$

This represents a much more efficient operating point (1.5% slip vs 5% original).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers → KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Drawing the block diagram without labeling** the function of each block → expect -2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Kramer and Scherbius** in 14-mark questions — they are NOT the same. Kramer = subsynchronous only with diode bridge; Scherbius = bidirectional with force-commutated converter.
> 4. **Writing "V/f control maintains constant torque"** without explanation — you must mention flux, EMF equation, and torque relationship.
> 5. **Forgetting the low-frequency voltage boost** in V/f control → examiner will deduct 1 mark for incomplete practical analysis.
> 6. **Wrong slip formula**: always use $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$, not $(N_s - N_r)/N_r$.
> 7. **Not specifying "per-phase" or "3-phase"** in torque calculations — KTU examiners are strict about this.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 — Induction Motor Drives**

- ☐ **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120f/P$. **Memorize it cold.**
- ☐ **Slip range at rated load:** typically 0.02 to 0.05 (squirrel-cage); 0.05 to 0.1 (slip-ring).
- ☐ **Rotor copper loss = s × P_ag** — the most derived and tested identity in Module 4.
- ☐ **Maximum torque condition:** $R_r/s_{mT} = X_2$. **$T_{max}$ independent of $R_r$** — a Board favourite.
- ☐ **V/f = constant** for constant flux → constant torque capability. Below 5 Hz, add voltage boost.
- ☐ **PWM VFD architecture:** Rectifier → DC Link → Inverter → Motor. **I know each block's function.**
- ☐ **Stator voltage control** is suitable only for **fan/pump loads** (quadratic torque). NOT for constant-torque loads.
- ☐ **Kramer system** = subsynchronous only, uses **diode bridge + line-commutated inverter**.
- ☐ **Scherbius system** = sub- and super-synchronous, uses **bidirectional force-commutated converter**.
- ☐ **Recovered slip power:** $P_{rec} = k \cdot s \cdot P_{ag}$. **New effective slip:** $s_{new} = s_{old}(1-k)$.
- ☐ **Power flow stages:** $P_{in} \to P_{ag} \to P_{mech} \to P_{out}$. **I can label losses in each stage.**
- ☐ **Vector control and DTC** are advanced topics, generally not in the basic 14-mark scope — focus on V/f and slip-power recovery.
- ☐ **Industrial application examples** I should remember: **Cranes = Kramer; Pumps/Fans = V/f VFD; Conveyors = soft-starter; Traction = V/f + Vector**.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Power Electronics, KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Residential and Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Residential and Industrial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Residential and Industrial Applications of Power Electronics** refer to the systematic deployment of solid-state semiconductor switching devices (such as SCRs, MOSFETs, IGBTs, and GTOs) and associated converter topologies (AC-DC, DC-DC, DC-AC, and AC-AC) to control, convert, and condition electrical energy across two principal end-user domains:

1. **Residential (Domestic) Applications:** Low-to-medium power (typically **< 5 kW**), single-phase, consumer-grade power conversion systems used for household comfort, lighting, computing, and entertainment.
2. **Industrial Applications:** Medium-to-very-high power (**from 1 kW to several hundred MW**), three-phase, ruggedized conversion systems used for process control, manufacturing, traction, energy transmission, and heavy electrical drives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The 2024 scheme emphasizes that power electronics is the **enabling technology** for nearly every modern electrical system. The syllabus expects students to map each real-world application to its underlying converter family and switching device — this mapping forms the basis of most 14-mark questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — The "Tap and Bucket" Model of a Chopper (DC-DC Converter):**
> Imagine a water tap filling a bucket. You can either keep the tap fully open (full power) or fully closed (no power) — but never in between. By **rapidly flicking** the tap ON and OFF thousands of times per second, you control the *average* water flow into the bucket. A **chopper does exactly this** with electrical energy using semiconductor switches — and the ratio of ON-time to total time is called the **duty cycle (D)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — The UPS as a "Power Sponge":**
> A residential UPS is like a sponge sitting between the dirty, unstable municipal power line and your sensitive laptop. When mains power is healthy, the sponge absorbs energy from the grid and stores it in a battery. When mains fails, the sponge *pushes back*, releasing that stored energy through an inverter as a clean, regulated AC sine wave.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Induction Cooking vs. Gas Cooking:**
> On a gas stove, *flame* (hot gas) transfers heat to the pan via convection. On an induction cooktop, a **high-frequency magnetic field** (typically **20–100 kHz**) generated by a power-electronic inverter induces eddy currents directly inside the metallic pan. The pan itself becomes the heating element. The cooktop surface stays relatively cool — only the pan gets hot. This is the same principle used in industrial **induction melting furnaces** scaled up to megawatts.

## 1.3 Classification of Applications by Power Range

| Domain | Power Range | Typical Devices | Typical Converters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Residential (low power)** | 10 W – 5 kW | MOSFETs, IGBTs, TRIACs | SMPS, LED drivers, dimmers, induction cooktops |
| **Commercial / Light Industrial** | 5 kW – 100 kW | IGBTs, MOSFETs, intelligent power modules (IPMs) | UPS, VFDs, servo drives, welding sets |
| **Heavy Industrial** | 100 kW – 10 MW | SCRs, GTOs, IGCTs | Cycloconverters, load-commutated inverters, large VFDs |
| **Utility / Transmission** | 10 MW – 1000+ MW | Thyristors, HV-IGBTs | HVDC converters, STATCOMs, SVCs, large rectifier/inverter stations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The same physical law — **controlled semiconductor switching** — scales seamlessly from a phone charger (5 W) to a UHVDC transmission line (**800 kV, 12 GW** — the **±1100 kV Changji–Guquan** line in China is the world's largest). What changes is the **switching device rating**, **converter topology**, and **cooling strategy**.

## 1.4 The Four Converter Families — A Universal Mental Map

Every power-electronics application — whether residential or industrial — uses one or a cascade combination of the following four basic converter functions:

1. **Rectifier (AC → DC):** Used in battery chargers, DC motor drives, electroplating, HVDC.
2. **Chopper (DC → DC):** Used in SMPS, DC drives, electric vehicles, solar MPPT.
3. **Inverter (DC → AC):** Used in UPS, induction heating, motor drives, grid-tie solar.
4. **AC-AC Converter (AC → AC with different frequency/voltage):** Includes cycloconverters, AC voltage controllers — used in soft starters, traction, fan/pump drives.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"R-C-I-A" → R**ectifier, **C**hopper, **I**nverter, **A**C-AC. Every application is built from these four blocks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Residential Applications — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### 2.1.1 Switched Mode Power Supply (SMPS)

**Operating Principle:**
The SMPS first rectifies AC mains to an unregulated DC link, then uses a **high-frequency (typically 50 kHz – 1 MHz) switching converter** (almost always a flyback or forward topology at low power) to step the voltage up or down, and finally rectifies and filters the high-frequency AC back to DC.

The key advantage over linear (series-pass) regulators is that power is dissipated only **during the switching transitions**, not continuously. This pushes practical efficiencies to **80%–96%**, compared to **30%–50%** for linear regulators.

**Key Performance Metrics:**
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{out} I_{out}}{V_{in} I_{in}}$
- **Switching Loss per transition:** $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{ds} I_d (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_s$
- **Total Switching Loss (per device):** $P_{sw,total} = \dfrac{V_{in} I_{out}}{2}(t_{on}+t_{off}) f_s$
- **Conduction Loss:** $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{ds(on)}$ (for MOSFET)
- **Hold-up Time:** Typically **20–30 ms** for ATX PC supplies, allowing the output to remain regulated during brief mains dropouts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high frequency?** Because the size of the isolating transformer and output filter inductors/capacitors is inversely proportional to frequency ($L \propto 1/f$, $C \propto 1/f$). Operating at **100 kHz** instead of **50 Hz** shrinks magnetic components by a factor of **2000**, dramatically reducing size, weight, and cost.

### 2.1.2 Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)

**Architecture (Online Double-Conversion UPS — the dominant residential/SME type):**

Mains → **Rectifier** → **DC Bus** → **Inverter** → Load, with a parallel **Battery Bank** charging from the DC bus and supplying it during mains failure.

**Key Specifications:**
| Parameter | Typical Residential Value |
|---|---|
| **Rating** | 0.5 kVA – 10 kVA |
| **Output Voltage THD** | < 3% (linear load), < 5% (non-linear) |
| **Crest Factor** | 3:1 |
| **Transfer Time (online)** | 0 ms (true online) |
| **Backup Time** | 5 – 30 min (extendable with battery banks) |
| **Power Factor (input)** | > 0.95 (with PFC front-end) |

### 2.1.3 Induction Heating (Domestic Induction Cooktop)

**Physical Mechanism:**
A copper coil beneath the ceramic glass carries high-frequency AC (typically **20–100 kHz**, generated by a **resonant half-bridge or full-bridge inverter**). The alternating magnetic field $\vec{B}$ induces eddy currents in the ferromagnetic pan base. By Joule heating:

$$P_{heat} = I_{eddy}^2 \cdot R_{pan}$$

The **depth of penetration** of the magnetic field into the pan is given by the skin depth:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$$

where $\rho$ is resistivity, $\mu$ is permeability, and $f$ is switching frequency. Higher frequency → shallower heating → **more efficient** heat transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **ferromagnetic steel** at $f = 25$ kHz, $\delta \approx 0.3$ mm — exactly the depth of the pan's cladding layer, ensuring maximum heat generation in the layer in direct thermal contact with the food.

### 2.1.4 Lighting Control

- **Phase-Controlled Dimmer (TRIAC-based):** Firing angle $\alpha$ controls RMS voltage. RMS output:
$$V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$$
- **LED Driver:** A constant-current SMPS (buck or buck-boost) with PFC. Modern drivers achieve **> 90% efficiency** and **PF > 0.9** to meet IEC 61000-3-2 harmonic limits.

### 2.1.5 Residential Solar PV Inverter

Converts variable DC from rooftop PV panels to grid-synchronized AC. Includes **MPPT** (Maximum Power Point Tracking) — typically perturb-and-observe or incremental conductance algorithm — to extract peak panel power under varying irradiance.

## 2.2 Industrial Applications — Detailed Engineering Analysis

### 2.2.1 Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) for AC Induction Motors

**The most widespread industrial power-electronics application.** A VFD rectifies mains AC to DC, smooths it on a DC link, and uses a **PWM inverter (typically space-vector PWM, SVPWM)** to synthesize a variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC output.

**The V/f (Volts per Hertz) Law — Core to all VFDs:**
To maintain constant air-gap flux $\Phi$ in an induction motor:

$$\dfrac{V}{f} = \text{constant} = 4.44 \cdot f \cdot N \cdot \Phi_{max}$$

Hence below base frequency, voltage must scale linearly with frequency.

**Synchronous Speed:**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$$
where $P$ = number of poles.

**Slip:**
$$s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$$

**Torque (simplified):**
$$T \approx \dfrac{3 V^2 R_r / s}{\omega_s \left[(R_s + R_r/s)^2 + (X_s + X_r)^2\right]}$$

**Energy Savings:** Replacing a throttling valve (pump) or damper (fan) with a VFD typically saves **30%–60%** of input energy, as the **affinity laws** state:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

Thus reducing fan/pump speed by 20% cuts power to **51.2%** of rated.

### 2.2.2 HVDC Transmission

For transmitting bulk power over distances **> 600–800 km** (overhead) or **> 50 km** (submarine cable), HVDC becomes economically superior to HVAC because:
- No reactive power loss in cables.
- No skin effect.
- Two conductors instead of three.
- Asynchronous grid interconnection possible.

**Modern systems use Line-Commutated Converters (LCC)** built from **thyristor valves** at ratings up to **±800 kV, 5–12 GW**, or **Voltage Source Converters (VSC-HVDC)** using IGBTs for weaker grids and offshore wind.

**Power transmitted:**
$$P_{DC} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}$$

### 2.2.3 Static VAR Compensator (SVC) and STATCOM

Used at substations and large industrial loads (arc furnaces, rolling mills) to provide **dynamic reactive power support**, suppress flicker, stabilize voltage, and damp power oscillations.

- **SVC:** TCR (Thyristor-Controlled Reactor) + TSC (Thyristor-Switched Capacitor).
- **STATCOM:** Self-commutated VSC using IGBTs — faster response (**< 5 ms** vs **20–40 ms** for SVC), smaller footprint.

### 2.2.4 Industrial Induction Heating (Melting, Forging, Hardening)

- **Coreless induction furnace (melting steel):** 1–50 MW, **50 Hz to 10 kHz**, water-cooled copper crucible coil. Uses cyclo-inverter or load-commutated inverter topology.
- **Induction hardening of gears/shafts:** Surface heating to austenitization temperature by **MHz-range** current; followed by quench. Penetration depth tuned via skin depth equation.

### 2.2.5 Welding Power Sources

Modern welding inverters use a **high-frequency IGBT full-bridge** (typically 20–100 kHz) feeding a high-frequency transformer, dramatically reducing weight (from ~80 kg to ~6 kg for a 250 A MMA welder) compared to legacy 50 Hz transformer-rectifier sets.

### 2.2.6 Electrochemical Processes (Electrolysis, Electroplating)

High-current low-voltage DC supplies built from **thyristor rectifiers** (12-pulse or 24-pulse for low harmonics). Typical load: aluminum smelting cells at **300–500 kA, 4–5 V per cell**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **This is your one-page revision sheet — memorize before the exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SMPS Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_o I_o}{V_{in} I_{in}}$ | Ratio, typically 0.80–0.96 |
| 2 | Switching Loss | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{V_{in} I_o}{2}(t_{on}+t_{off}) f_s$ | Watts; $f_s$ in Hz |
| 3 | TRIAC Dimmer RMS | $V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left[\pi-\alpha+\tfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right]}$ | $V_m$ peak mains, $\alpha$ firing angle |
| 4 | Skin Depth | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\pi \mu f}}$ | m; $\rho$ in $\Omega\cdot$m, $\mu$ in H/m |
| 5 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM |
| 6 | Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit, 0–1 |
| 7 | V/f Law | $\dfrac{V}{f} = 4.44 \, N \, \Phi_{max}$ | Constant below base speed |
| 8 | Affinity Law (Power) | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | Fans/pumps |
| 9 | HVDC Power | $P_{DC} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}$ | W |
| 10 | Transformer EMF | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{max}$ | V/turn |
| 11 | Chopper (Buck) | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | D = duty cycle |
| 12 | Inverter THD | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1}$ | Ratio × 100% |
| 13 | Active Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | Three-phase |
| 14 | Reactive Power | $Q = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L \sin\phi$ | VAR |
| 15 | Apparent Power | $S = \sqrt{3} \, V_L I_L$ | VA |
| 16 | Power Factor | $PF = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | 0–1 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VFDs in HVAC and process plants** are the largest single industrial energy-saving intervention, accounting for an estimated **60–70% of global industrial electricity consumption** and saving approximately **10–15%** of total industrial electricity worldwide.
- **HVDC** interconnects asynchronous grids (e.g., the **±500 kV HVDC Bahrat–Gujarat** link in India) and enables offshore wind farms (**DolWin3 in Germany — 900 MW, 79 km offshore cable**).
- **Domestic induction cooking** is **84% efficient** vs. **40%** for gas and **30%** for electric resistance — significant residential energy and emissions impact.
- **STATCOMs** at arc-furnace installations reduce voltage flicker to within IEEE 519 harmonic limits, enabling furnace operation without disturbing neighboring consumers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Output Voltage of a Single-Phase Full-Wave Phase-Controlled Rectifier Feeding an R Load (Industrial DC Drive)

This derivation underpins many industrial rectifier-fed DC drives used in electroplating, electrolysis, and DC motor armature supply.

**Problem Statement:**
A single-phase full-wave controlled rectifier feeds a purely resistive load $R$. The source is $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$. Derive an expression for the average output voltage $V_{avg}$ as a function of firing angle $\alpha$.

**Step 1: Define the conduction intervals.**
During the positive half-cycle, the SCR pair (T1, T2) conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\pi$. During the negative half-cycle, the other pair (T3, T4) conducts from $\omega t = (\pi + \alpha)$ to $2\pi$.

**Step 2: Write the average voltage integral.**
$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral.**
$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \left[-\cos(\pi) + \cos(\alpha)\right]$$

$$V_{avg} = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi} \left[1 + \cos(\alpha)\right]$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**
At $\alpha = 0$ (full conduction): $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$ — the familiar average of a full-wave rectified sine.
At $\alpha = \pi$ (no conduction): $V_{avg} = 0$.
The output DC voltage is monotonically decreasing with $\alpha$, which is exactly how a **thyristor-controlled DC drive** regulates speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The above equation assumes a purely resistive load. For an **R-L load** (highly inductive, such as a DC motor armature), the current continues to flow even after the source voltage reverses — until the next SCR pair is fired at $\omega t = (\pi + \alpha)$. With continuous conduction, the same equation holds, but the output becomes *less* sensitive to $\alpha$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Voltage of an Ideal Buck Chopper (Residential SMPS Building Block)

**Problem Statement:**
Derive the steady-state output voltage of an ideal buck converter operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM).

**Step 1: Define the switching intervals.**
- Switch ON for time $t_{on}$: diode reverse-biased; inductor sees $V_s - V_o$; current rises linearly.
- Switch OFF for time $t_{off}$: diode forward-biased (freewheels); inductor sees $-V_o$; current falls linearly.

**Step 2: Apply the volt-second balance on the inductor.**
In steady state, the average voltage across the inductor over one switching period $T_s = t_{on} + t_{off}$ must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would drift unboundedly).

$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot t_{on} + (-V_o) \cdot t_{off} = 0$$

**Step 3: Substitute duty cycle $D = t_{on} / T_s$.**
$$(V_s - V_o) \cdot D \cdot T_s - V_o \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T_s = 0$$

**Step 4: Divide by $T_s$ and expand.**
$$V_s D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D = 0$$

$$V_s D = V_o$$

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s}$$

**Step 5: Range of operation.**
Since $0 \le D \le 1$, the output is always **less than** the input — the "buck" (step-down) function. This is the foundation of every SMPS in your laptop, TV, and phone charger.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often asks the student to *reverse-derive* — given a required $V_o$ and known $V_s$, find $D$. Practice this and its counterparts (boost: $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$, buck-boost: $V_o = -V_s D/(1-D)$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Synchronous Speed and Slip Relationship (Industrial Motor Drive)

**Step 1: From Faraday's law, the rotating magnetic field of a three-phase induction motor has a mechanical synchronous speed:**
$$N_s = \dfrac{120 \, f}{P}$$

where $f$ is supply frequency (Hz) and $P$ is the number of magnetic poles (an even integer: 2, 4, 6, …).

**Step 2: The rotor can never reach $N_s$** (this would require zero induced EMF and zero torque). It rotates at $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ where $s$ is the slip.

**Step 3: The rotor induced EMF frequency is $s \cdot f$.**
This is the principle by which a **VFD** controls motor speed: by varying $f$ in the supply inverter, $N_s$ scales proportionally, and the motor follows. Below base frequency, voltage is also scaled (V/f law) to maintain torque.

**Step 4: Numerical sanity check.**
For a 4-pole motor at 50 Hz: $N_s = 120 \times 50 / 4 = 1500$ RPM. With $s = 0.04$, $N_r = 1440$ RPM. If we reduce frequency to 25 Hz (and voltage to half), $N_s = 750$ RPM, $N_r \approx 720$ RPM — half-speed operation at the same torque. This is the engineering basis for variable-speed pumps, fans, conveyors.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Affinity Law — Power vs. Speed for Centrifugal Loads

The affinity (or "cube") law describes why variable-speed control of fans and pumps is so much more efficient than throttling.

**Step 1:** Flow rate $Q \propto N$ (rotational speed).
**Step 2:** Pressure $H \propto N^2$.
**Step 3:** Power $P = Q \cdot H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$.

**Step 4:** Therefore:
$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

**Step 5: Numerical example for KTU problem.**
A fan rated at **20 kW** at **1500 RPM** is throttled to **75% flow**. Using affinity law:
- New speed: $N_2 = 0.75 \times 1500 = 1125$ RPM.
- New power: $P_2 = 20 \times (0.75)^3 = 20 \times 0.4219 = 8.44$ kW.

**Power saved = 20 − 8.44 = 11.56 kW (≈ 58% reduction)** — by simply slowing the motor with a VFD rather than dumping excess energy across a damper.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Applications → Converters → Devices

> [!TIP]
> **This is the single most important mapping for KTU exams.** Examiners often give an application and ask you to identify the converter and switch.

| Application | Power Range | Primary Converter | Switching Device | Special Features |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mobile phone charger | 5 – 65 W | Flyback SMPS | MOSFET (650 V) | High-frequency transformer isolation |
| Laptop adapter | 45 – 240 W | Flyback / PFC + LLC | MOSFET | Active PFC + synchronous rectification |
| Induction cooktop | 1 – 3.5 kW | Resonant half/full-bridge | IGBT (1200 V) | 20–100 kHz resonant operation |
| LED streetlight driver | 50 – 250 W | PFC + buck/boost | MOSFET | Constant current, > 0.95 PF |
| Residential UPS | 0.5 – 10 kVA | Online double-conversion (rectifier + inverter) | IGBT | 0 ms transfer, battery backup |
| Solar PV inverter (residential) | 1 – 10 kW | VSC with HF transformerless | IGBT / SiC MOSFET | MPPT, anti-islanding, grid sync |
| Small AC drive (VFD) | 0.5 – 5 kW | Diode rectifier + IGBT inverter | IGBT | V/f, sensorless vector control |
| Industrial VFD | 5 – 500 kW | Active front-end + inverter | IGBT modules | Regenerative, 4-quadrant |
| Welding inverter | 5 – 30 kW | IGBT full-bridge + HF transformer | IGBT | 20–100 kHz, CC/CV control |
| Induction melting furnace | 0.5 – 50 MW | Cycloconverter / LCI | SCR / IGCT | 50 Hz – 10 kHz, water-cooled coil |
| Arc-furnace SVC | 50 – 300 MVAR | TCR + TSC | Thyristor | Flicker mitigation, ±100 MVAR range |

| Electrolysis rectifier | 50 – 600 kA | 12/24-pulse thyristor rectifier | Thyristor | Ultra-low voltage, ultra-high current |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Example:** A 4-pole, 3-phase, 50 Hz induction motor is controlled by a VFD. At full load the rotor runs at **1440 RPM**. Calculate: (i) synchronous speed, (ii) slip, (iii) rotor current frequency.

**Solution:**

(i) $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500$ RPM

(ii) $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} = \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} = \dfrac{60}{1500} = 0.04$ pu (or 4%)

(iii) $f_r = s \cdot f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2$ Hz

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Online Double-Conversion UPS (Residential/SME)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MAINS[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> RECT[Diode Bridge Rectifier with PFC]
    RECT --> DCBUS[DC Link Capacitor 400V DC]
    DCBUS --> INV[PWM IGBT Inverter with LC Filter]
    INV --> LOAD[Critical Load Laptop Server Medical]
    BAT[Sealed Lead Acid or Li-ion Battery Bank] --> CHARGER[Battery Charger DC DC]
    DCBUS --> CHARGER
    CHARGER --> BAT
    BYPASS[Static Bypass Switch] -.-> LOAD
    CTRL[Digital Controller DSP FPGA] -.-> RECT
    CTRL -.-> INV
    CTRL -.-> BYPASS
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Under normal mains: mains → rectifier → DC bus → inverter → load. The DC bus simultaneously trickle-charges the battery.
- Under mains failure: battery feeds the DC bus → inverter continues to feed load. **Transfer time = 0 ms**.
- Under overload: static bypass closes in **< 4 ms**, routing raw mains to the load.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) for Industrial AC Motor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUPPLY[Three Phase AC Mains 415V 50Hz] --> CONV[Six Pulse Diode or Active Rectifier]
    CONV --> DC[DC Link Capacitor Bank]
    DC --> INVERTER[IGBT PWM Inverter SVPWM]
    INVERTER --> MOTOR[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    CTRL[VFD Controller Microcontroller DSP] --> INVERTER
    CTRL --> CONV
    SENSE[Current and Voltage Sensors] --> CTRL
    KEYPAD[HMI Keypad Display] --> CTRL
    PROT[Protection Overcurrent Overvoltage Ground Fault] --> CTRL
```

**Stages explained:**
1. **Rectifier:** Either a passive six-pulse diode bridge (basic) or an active IGBT front-end (regenerative, low-harmonic).
2. **DC link:** Stores energy, smooths ripple, momentarily absorbs regenerative energy.
3. **Inverter:** Six IGBTs (or MOSFETs at low power) switched via Space-Vector PWM to synthesize a sinusoidal-like output of variable $V$ and $f$.
4. **Controller:** Runs V/f or vector control algorithm at typically **4–16 kHz** switching frequency.
5. **Sensors & Protection:** Hall-effect CTs, shunts, isolation amplifiers feeding back to trip the inverter in **< 10 µs** on fault.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Domestic Induction Cooktop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AC[230V 50Hz AC Mains] --> RECT1[Bridge Rectifier]
    RECT1 --> SMOOTH[DC Link Capacitor]
    SMOOTH --> INVH[Half Bridge Resonant Inverter 20 to 100 kHz]
    INVH --> COIL[Flat Copper Induction Coil Beneath Ceramic Glass]
    COIL --> PAN[Ferromagnetic Pan Induced Eddy Currents]
    PAN --> HEAT[Heat Generated in Pan]
    CTL[Microcontroller with Pan Detection] --> INVH
    INVH -.-> CTL
```

**Key point:** The coil and the pan form a **loosely coupled transformer** with the pan base acting as a **shorted single-turn secondary**. The resonant inverter maintains operation at the LC tank's natural frequency, where impedance is at minimum and current is maximum for efficient power transfer.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — HVDC Transmission System (Line Commutated Converter)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    GRID1[AC Grid 1 Sending End] --> TX1[Converter Transformer with Tap Changer]
    TX1 --> RECT[12 Pulse Thyristor Rectifier Valve Hall]
    RECT --> DC[DC Transmission Line Plus Minus 500 to 800 kV]
    DC --> INV[12 Pulse Thyristor Inverter Valve Hall]
    INV --> TX2[Converter Transformer]
    TX2 --> GRID2[AC Grid 2 Receiving End]
    CTRL[Master Controller with Telecoms] -.-> RECT
    CTRL -.-> INV
    FILTER1[AC Harmonic Filters Sending End] -.-> GRID1
    FILTER2[AC Harmonic Filters Receiving End] -.-> GRID2
    DCREACT[DC Smoothing Reactor] --- DC
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — STATCOM for Industrial Load Compensation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BUS[Industrial Bus PCC] --> LOAD[Non Linear Load Arc Furnace or LCI Drive]
    BUS --> VSC[Voltage Source Converter IGBT Multilevel]
    VSC --> DC[DC Link Capacitor]
    VSC --> REACTOR[Coupling Reactor]
    REACTOR --> BUS
    CTRL[Reactive Power Controller dq Frame PLL] --> VSC
    SENSE[Voltage and Current Sensors] --> CTRL
```

**Operating principle:** The VSC generates a voltage $V_c$ that is in phase with the bus voltage $V_s$ but with controllable magnitude. The resulting reactive current $I = (V_c - V_s)/jX$ flows through the reactor, supplying or absorbing reactive power without any physical capacitor or reactor bank — purely electronically, in **< 5 ms**.
