# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Explain with a block diagram the operation of an online UPS system. List any two advantages over an offline UPS.**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words, 3-mark expected coverage):**

An **online (double-conversion) UPS** continuously processes power through the rectifier-inverter chain regardless of mains health. The AC mains is first rectified to DC, which charges the battery and feeds a PWM inverter that synthesizes a clean 230 V, 50 Hz sine wave for the load.

**Block Diagram (text representation):**
AC Mains → Rectifier → DC Bus → Battery Charger ↔ Battery Bank; DC Bus → Inverter → LC Filter → Load; Static Bypass → Load.

**Two Advantages over Offline UPS:**
1. **Zero transfer time** to battery — the load is always fed from the inverter, so there is no switchover transient.
2. **Complete isolation** of the load from mains-borne noise, surges, sags, and frequency variations — the load always sees a clean, regulated sine wave.

*(Word count target: ~110. Diagrams and bullet points acceptable.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**What is meant by V/f control in induction motor drives? Why is it necessary below base speed?**

**Model Answer:**

In **V/f (Volts per Hertz) control**, the inverter output voltage is varied in **direct proportion** to its output frequency, keeping the ratio $V/f$ constant. This is based on the EMF equation:

$$E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{max}$$

For constant air-gap flux $\Phi_{max}$ (and hence constant torque capability), $V$ must scale linearly with $f$.

**Necessity below base speed:**
Below rated frequency, if voltage is held constant while $f$ is reduced, $f$ decreases but $V$ stays the same, causing $V/f$ to rise → flux increases → magnetic saturation of the iron core, excessive magnetizing current, and overheating. Reducing voltage proportionally prevents saturation and preserves torque capability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question B Option 1 — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Describe the working of an SMPS with a neat block diagram. Compare it with a linear power supply.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) With the help of a circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the working of a single-phase full-bridge inverter using IGBTs feeding an induction heating load. Mention the role of resonant operation.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Block Diagram of SMPS:**

AC Mains → **Input Rectifier + EMI Filter** → **PFC Stage (Boost)** → **High-Voltage DC Link (≈ 400 V)** → **High-Frequency Inverter (MOSFET/IGBT full-bridge or half-bridge, 50–500 kHz)** → **HF Isolation Transformer** → **Output Rectifier (Schottky or synchronous)** → **Output LC Filter** → **DC Load**; with feedback via **Optocoupler + TL431** to the PWM controller.

**Working Principle:**
The incoming 50 Hz AC is rectified and power-factor-corrected to a stable high-voltage DC (~400 V). This DC is then chopped at high frequency (typically 100 kHz) by an inverter, creating a high-frequency AC waveform. This is fed through a much smaller and lighter HF transformer that provides galvanic isolation and voltage step-down. The secondary is rectified and filtered to produce a smooth low-voltage DC output. A feedback loop senses the output and modulates the PWM duty cycle to maintain regulation against input voltage or load variations.

**Comparison Table (must appear in answer):**

| Feature | Linear Power Supply | SMPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Frequency** | 50 Hz (mains transformer) | 50 kHz – 1 MHz (HF transformer) |
| **Transformer Size** | Large, heavy, iron core | Small, light, ferrite core |
| **Efficiency** | 30%–50% | 80%–96% |
| **Heat Dissipation** | High (series pass element) | Low (switching losses only) |
| **Line/Load Regulation** | Excellent | Excellent (with feedback) |
| **Ripple & Noise** | Very low (< 5 mV) | Moderate (HF ripple, must filter) |
| **Hold-up Time** | Long (large reservoir cap) | Short (small caps) |
| **Cost at low power** | Lower | Slightly higher |
| **Cost at high power** | Higher | Lower |
| **EMI** | Negligible | Higher (switching noise, needs filter) |

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Circuit Description:**
A single-phase full-bridge inverter consists of four IGBT switches **T1, T2, T3, T4** arranged in an H-bridge, with the resonant tank-load (induction heating coil + compensating capacitor) connected across the bridge output **A–B**. Anti-parallel diodes D1–D4 carry the reverse current.

**Operating Modes:**

- **Mode 1 (0 to T/2):** T1 and T2 ON; T3 and T4 OFF. Current flows from +DC bus through T1 → load → T2 → −DC bus. Output voltage $v_{AB} = +V_{DC}$.
- **Mode 2 (T/2 to T):** T3 and T4 ON; T1 and T2 OFF. Current reverses through T3 and T4. $v_{AB} = -V_{DC}$.

This produces a **quasi-square wave** of amplitude $\pm V_{DC}$.

**Resonant Operation — Why:**
The induction coil $L$ and series/parallel capacitor $C$ form an **LC resonant tank** with natural frequency:

$$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

The inverter is switched at or near $f_0$. At resonance:
- Tank impedance is purely resistive (minimum in series, maximum in parallel).
- Load current is sinusoidal even with square-wave excitation.
- **Zero-voltage switching (ZVS)** or **zero-current switching (ZCS)** becomes possible because the load current naturally crosses zero at the end of each half-cycle, allowing the switches to commutate with negligible switching loss.
- This pushes efficiency above **95%** at MW class industrial melting furnaces.

**Waveforms to draw:**
1. Gate pulses: $V_{g1}, V_{g3}$ (complementary to $V_{g2}, V_{g4}$).
2. Output voltage $v_{AB}$: quasi-square wave $\pm V_{DC}$.
3. Load current $i_{load}$: nearly sinusoidal at $f_0$.

**Advantages for Induction Heating:**
- Compact (HF transformer-less if coil is part of tank).
- Clean sinusoidal current → uniform eddy-current heating.
- Easy power control by **frequency variation** above $f_0$ (load angle control).

---

### **Question B Option 1 — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain with a block diagram the working of an HVDC transmission system using Line Commutated Converters (LCC). Mention any three advantages over HVAC for long-distance bulk power transmission.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) With necessary circuit diagram and waveform, explain the operation of a step-down chopper (buck converter) feeding a DC series motor. Derive the expression for output voltage.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Block Diagram (drawn in exam):**

AC Grid (Sending End) → Step-up **Converter Transformer** (with on-load tap changer) → **12-Pulse Thyristor Rectifier** (built from two 6-pulse bridges connected in series on the DC side, fed by Y–Y and Y–Δ transformer secondaries) → **DC Smoothing Reactor** → **±500 kV DC Overhead Transmission Line / Submarine Cable** → **DC Smoothing Reactor** → **12-Pulse Thyristor Inverter** → Step-down **Converter Transformer** → AC Grid (Receiving End). Harmonic filters (5th, 7th, 11th, 13th tuned, plus a high-pass filter) at both ends absorb reactive power and shunt harmonic currents.

**Working Principle:**
At the sending end, the 12-pulse thyristor rectifier converts AC to DC. Each thyristor valve is fired at a small delay angle $\alpha$ (typically 12°–18°) and operates as a fully controllable switch. DC power $P_{DC} = V_{DC} \cdot I_{DC}$ flows through the transmission line. At the receiving end, the 12-pulse thyristor inverter operates at extinction advance angle $\gamma$ (typically 15°–18°), converting DC back to AC. The DC voltage is:

$$V_{DC} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\alpha - R_{DC} I_{DC}$$

at the rectifier, and at the inverter:

$$V_{DC} = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos\gamma - R_{DC} I_{DC}$$

Control of $\alpha$ at the rectifier regulates DC current; control of $\gamma$ at the inverter regulates DC voltage. Together they govern real power flow $P$ and the inverter's reactive power consumption.

**Three Advantages of HVDC over HVAC for Long-Distance / Submarine Transmission:**

1. **Lower losses for distances > 600–800 km (overhead) or > 50 km (cable):** No $I^2R$ skin-effect loss, no dielectric loss in cables, and no reactive current flow on the DC line itself.
2. **Asynchronous interconnection:** HVDC links two grids of different frequencies (e.g., 50 Hz and 60 Hz) or same frequency but different phase angles without requiring synchronism.
3. **Narrower right-of-way:** Two conductors (or one bipolar pair with earth return) vs. three for HVAC — about **30–40%** less tower width, lower visual impact, lower land cost.
4. **Bonus advantage:** Power flow is fully and rapidly controllable — a key feature for stabilizing AC grids.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Buck Chopper Driving a DC Series Motor — Circuit:**

A single-pole solid-state switch (IGBT/MOSFET with anti-parallel diode) connects the DC source $V_s$ to the series combination of motor armature and series field winding. A freewheeling diode $D_F$ is connected across the motor to carry current when the switch is OFF.

**Operation:**

- **Mode 1 (Switch ON, duration $t_{on}$):** Current flows from $V_s$ through the closed switch into the motor armature and field. Armature voltage $V_a = V_s - I_a R_a$ (small drop). Motor current rises, EMF $E_b = K\Phi N$ is overcome. The motor accelerates.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF, duration $t_{off}$):** The motor is disconnected from source. Current freewheels through $D_F$, decaying through the motor's inductance. Armature sees $V_a \approx 0$ (only the small $I_a R_a$ drop and back-EMF). Motor decelerates.

**Waveforms:** Show $V_{sw}$ (gate pulse), $V_a$ (chopped armature voltage, with average $V_o$), $I_a$ (rising/falling ripple).

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

Applying **volt-second balance** across the armature inductance $L_a$ in steady state (ripple small):

During $t_{on}$: armature sees $V_s - I_a R_a - E_b$
During $t_{off}$: armature sees $- I_a R_a - E_b$ (freewheel)

For ripple-free steady state, the average voltage across $L_a$ is zero:

$$(V_s - I_a R_a - E_b) \cdot t_{on} + (-I_a R_a - E_b) \cdot t_{off} = 0$$

Ignoring $R_a$ drop (or rearranging), the average armature voltage:

$$V_o = V_s \cdot D - E_b$$

But the simpler ideal result (assuming $R_a \to 0$ and $L \to \infty$):

$$\boxed{V_o = D \cdot V_s = \dfrac{t_{on}}{T_s} \cdot V_s}$$

This gives the **speed control** equation: by varying $D$ (the duty cycle of the chopper), the average armature voltage varies linearly from 0 to $V_s$, providing smooth, stepless speed control of the DC series motor from zero to rated speed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for Module 4:**
> 1. **Confusing LCC-HVDC with VSC-HVDC.** Examiners will *specifically* look for the words *line-commutated* and *thyristor-based* when discussing LCC; mention *self-commutated*, *IGBT-based*, *PWM*, *black-start capable* when discussing VSC.
> 2. **Forgetting the freewheeling diode** in chopper diagrams. A buck/boost chopper without a freewheeling diode across the load is worth **zero** in the diagram part — even if the math is correct.
> 3. **V/f — not just V.** Students often say "reduce voltage to reduce speed." Below base speed, you reduce *both* $V$ and $f$ in the same proportion. Above base speed, you raise $f$ alone (field-weakening region).
> 4. **SMPS vs Linear — stating "SMPS is better" without numerical efficiency comparison.** Always quote 80–95% vs 30–50% — it demonstrates mastery.
> 5. **RMS vs Average in dimmer circuits.** The phase-controlled RMS equation given in the formula sheet is what *you* calculate; many students mistakenly use the average value, losing 2 marks.
> 6. **HVDC — forgetting the smoothing reactor.** The large series inductor (0.5–1 H) on the DC side prevents commutation failures and limits DC fault currents. It is a mandatory element in the block diagram.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ☐ **Four converter families:** Rectifier, Chopper, Inverter, AC-AC. Memorize their symbols and applications.
- ☐ **SMPS** uses high-frequency switching (50 kHz – 1 MHz) → small magnetics → 80–96% efficiency.
- ☐ **Online UPS** gives **0 ms** transfer; offline gives 4–8 ms. Battery is the heart of any UPS.
- ☐ **Induction heating** works on eddy-current principle; skin depth $\delta = \sqrt{\rho/\pi\mu f}$; higher $f$ → shallower heating.
- ☐ **VFD** uses V/f law below base speed; PWM inverter with SVPWM is the dominant modulation strategy.
- ☐ **Synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$; **slip** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$.
- ☐ **Affinity law** $P \propto N^3$ — slowing fans/pumps by 20% saves ~50% power.
- ☐ **HVDC** uses thyristor valves (LCC) or IGBT VSC for bulk long-distance/submarine transmission.
- ☐ **STATCOM/SVC** provide dynamic reactive power — STATCOM is faster (VSC), SVC uses thyristor-switched capacitors/reactors.
- ☐ **Buck chopper:** $V_o = D V_s$; **Boost:** $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$; **Buck-Boost:** $V_o = -V_s D/(1-D)$.
- ☐ **TRIAC dimmer RMS:** $V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{(1/\pi)[\pi - \alpha + (\sin 2\alpha)/2]}$.
- ☐ **Electrolytic/electrochemical industry** uses 12-pulse or 24-pulse thyristor rectifiers at very high currents.
- ☐ **Welding inverters** use HF IGBT bridge (~100 kHz) → 1/10th the weight of 50 Hz transformer-rectifier sets.
- ☐ **Solar PV inverter** must perform MPPT, anti-islanding, and grid synchronization (IEEE 1547 / IEC 61727).
- ☐ **Power semiconductor evolution:** Thyristor (1957) → GTO → BJT → MOSFET → IGBT (1980s) → IGCT → SiC MOSFET (2010s) → GaN HEMT (2020s) — *each generation enables new applications.*

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Whenever a question says "explain an application," always write **four** things: (1) **why** power electronics is needed, (2) **block diagram**, (3) **key formulas or numbers** (e.g., 25 kHz, 1500 RPM, 12-pulse), (4) **real-world example** (e.g., Tata Steel arc-furnace SVC, Bengaluru–Mumbai HVDC). This guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Utility Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET503 - POWER ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electric Utility Applications # Electric Utility Applications — KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) **Electric Utility Applications** of Power Electronics refer to the deployment of high-power semiconductor switching devices (SCRs, GTOs, IGBTs, IGCTs) in the generation, transmission, and distribution (T&D) infrastructure of an electrical grid. The principal domains include **HVDC (High Voltage Direct Current) Transmission**, **Static VAR Compensators (SVC)**, and the broader family of **FACTS (Flexible AC Transmission Systems)** controllers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 4 Syllabus Highlight:** > The module unifies three engineering sub-domains: > (a) **HVDC Converters** — Line Commutated Converters (LCC) and Voltage Source Converters (VSC). > (b) **Static VAR Compensators** — TCR, TSC, and combined SVC. > (c) **FACTS Controllers** — Shunt (STATCOM), Series (SSSC), and Combined (UPFC). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine an **electrical grid as a giant highway system**: - **HVDC Transmission** is like a **high-speed underground tunnel** that bypasses traffic (losses) of long-distance overhead AC highways. - **Static VAR Compensators (SVCs)** are like **traffic signal dimmers** at intersections — they instantly adjust the "pressure" (reactive power) to prevent voltage collapse. - **FACTS Controllers (STATCOM/SSSC/UPFC)** are **smart steering wheels** built into the grid that can reroute power flow on existing AC lines without rebuilding them. Without these systems, the grid would behave like a one-lane road: unstable, congested, and prone to blackouts during peak demand. > [!NOTE] > **Core Physical Constants / Metrics to Memorize for KTU Exams:** > - AC grid frequency in India: **$f = 50 \text{ Hz}$** > - Standard HVDC bipolar link voltage: **$\pm 500 \text{ kV}$** or **$\pm 800 \text{ kV}$** > - Reactive power symbol: **$Q$** (measured in **VAR / MVAR**) > - DC link voltage of VSC-HVDC: **$\pm 150 \text{ kV}$ to $\pm 320 \text{ kV}$** (typical modern systems) --- ### 1.3 Historical & Engineering Context Before power electronics, mechanical switching dominated. The advent of the **thyristor (SCR) in 1957** and later **IGBT (1980s)** revolutionized utility-scale power control. The first commercial **HVDC link (Gotland, Sweden, 1954)** used mercury-arc rectifiers; modern systems exclusively use **thyristor valves** for LCC-HVDC and **IGBT/IGCT valves** for VSC-HVDC. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 HVDC Transmission — Operating Principle HVDC transmits bulk power over long distances using **DC**, eliminating the inductive and capacitive charging currents of AC lines. #### 2.1.1 Line Commutated Converter (LCC) HVDC A **6-pulse Graetz bridge** is the building block. Two bridges phase-shifted by **$30°$** form a **12-pulse converter** (standard for utility-grade HVDC). **Operating Modes:** - **Rectifier Mode** ($0° < \alpha < 90°$): AC → DC, sending end. - **Inverter Mode** ($90° < \alpha < 180°$): DC → AC, receiving end. > [!NOTE] > **$\alpha$ = Firing Angle (delay angle)** — measured from the natural commutation point of the thyristor. > **$\gamma$ = Extinction Angle** — recovery margin (typically **$15° \leq \gamma \leq 20°$**). #### 2.1.2 Voltage Source Converter (VSC) HVDC Uses **self-commutated** devices (IGBTs with anti-parallel diodes). Enables: - **Independent control of active and reactive power** - **Black-start capability** - **Connection to weak/passive grids** --- ### 2.2 Static VAR Compensator (SVC) The SVC is a **shunt-connected** reactive power device. Its **V-I characteristic** is a sloped line within a controllable band: $$V = V_{ref} + k_{slope} \cdot (I - I_{ref})$$ #### 2.2.1 Two Principal Branches **(a) Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR):** A reactor in series with a **bidirectional thyristor pair (inverse-parallel SCRs)**. Provides **continuously variable** inductive reactive power. **(b) Thyristor Switched Capacitor (TSC):** A capacitor switched by a thyristor pair (with a damping reactor). Provides **discrete steps** of capacitive VARs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **TCR absorbs VARs (lagging)** while **TSC injects VARs (leading)**. Their combination makes the SVC a **bidirectional, fast-acting VAR source**. --- ### 2.3 FACTS Controllers (Flexible AC Transmission Systems) The IEEE defines FACTS as *"alternating current transmission systems incorporating power-electronics-based and other static controllers to enhance controllability and increase power transfer capability."* #### 2.3.1 Classification Table | FACTS Device | Connection Type | Function | KTU Weightage | |---|---|---|---| | **STATCOM** | Shunt | Voltage regulation, reactive power | High | | **SSSC** | Series | Line reactance compensation, power flow | Medium | | **UPFC** | Combined Shunt + Series | Unified P, Q, V control | Very High (14-mark favorite) | | **TCSC** | Series | Variable line reactance | Medium | | **SVC** | Shunt | Reactive power | High | --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. #### 2.4.1 HVDC Converter Equations | # | Formula | Description | Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{do} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot V_{LL}$ | No-load DC voltage (6-pulse bridge) | — | | 2 | $V_{d,\alpha} = V_{do} \cos \alpha - R_c I_d$ | Rectifier output with commutation | $\alpha < 90°$ | | 3 | $V_{d,\gamma} = V_{do} \cos \gamma - R_c I_d$ | Inverter output (extinction angle) | $\gamma \geq \gamma_{min}$ | | 4 | $\mu = \gamma - \cos^{-1}\!\left(\cos \gamma - \dfrac{\sqrt{2} I_d X_c}{V_{LL}}\right)$ | Overlap angle (commutation) | — | | 5 | $P_{dc} = V_d \cdot I_d$ | DC power transmitted | — | | 6 | $V_{d,12} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot (V_{LL,Y} + V_{LL,\Delta}) \cos \alpha$ | 12-pulse converter voltage | $\Delta$-wye phase shift $30°$ | > [!NOTE] > For absolute-value expressions inside tables, KTU examiners expect $\vert x \vert$ notation rendered as $\lvert x \rvert$ in handwriting. Always use a safe LaTeX delimiter like `\lvert` or `\mid` to avoid markdown conflicts. #### 2.4.2 SVC & STATCOM Equations | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_{SVC} = \dfrac{V^2}{X_{SVC}} \sin \delta$ | Reactive power exchange (SVC) | | 2 | $Q_{STATCOM} = V \cdot I_q$ | STATCOM reactive current control | | 3 | $\Delta Q = V_{ref} \cdot \Delta B_{SVC}$ | Reactive power variation | #### 2.4.3 FACTS Power Flow Equations | # | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin \delta$ | Uncompensated line active power | | 2 | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X - X_{comp}} \sin \delta$ | With series compensation | | 3 | $P_{UPFC} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X} \sin(\delta_1 - \delta_2) + \dfrac{V_1 V_{pq}}{X} \sin \delta$ | UPFC controlled power | | 4 | $Q = \dfrac{V_1}{X}(V_1 - V_2 \cos \delta)$ | Reactive power flow | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** These equations are the backbone of grid simulation tools like **PSCAD, EMTP-RV, and DIgSILENT PowerFactory**. Engineers use them to size compensators, predict voltage stability, and plan reactive support during contingencies. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: No-Load DC Voltage of a 6-Pulse Bridge **Starting Point:** The average DC output of a 6-pulse converter is the integral of the line-to-line voltage over one conduction period. For a 6-pulse bridge with thyristor firing at angle $\alpha$: $$V_{d,\alpha} = \dfrac{1}{\pi/3} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \sqrt{2} V_{LL} \sin \theta \, d\theta$$ Evaluating the integral: $$\int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \sin \theta \, d\theta = \left[-\cos \theta \right]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3}$$ $$= -\cos\!\left(\alpha + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) + \cos \alpha$$ Multiplying out and applying the identity $\cos(\alpha + \pi/3) = \cos \alpha \cos(\pi/3) - \sin \alpha \sin(\pi/3)$: $$= \cos \alpha \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin \alpha = \dfrac{1}{2}\cos \alpha + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin \alpha$$ Multiplying both terms by $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}}$: $$= \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\cos \alpha + \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin \alpha \right) = \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\!\left(\alpha - \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right)$$ Therefore the average DC voltage is: $$V_{d,\alpha} = \dfrac{3}{\pi} \cdot \sqrt{2} V_{LL} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \cos\!\left(\alpha - \dfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = \dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha$$ (assuming symmetrical firing, $\alpha - \pi/6 = \alpha$ for the standard formulation). > [!NOTE] > **Why it matters:** This is the **single most important formula** for any KTU HVDC question. Lossless case gives $V_{do} = (3\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_{LL} \approx 1.35 \, V_{LL}$. With overlap, we subtract $R_c I_d$ where $R_c = 3 X_c / \pi$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: SVC Reactive Power (V-I Characteristic) A SVC connected at a bus with voltage $V$ and current $I$ has the controllable susceptance $B_{SVC}$. The reactive power injected (or absorbed) is: $$Q_{SVC} = V^2 \cdot B_{SVC} = V \cdot I$$ Rearranging for the bus voltage using a slope reactance $X_{SL}$: $$V = V_{ref} + X_{SL} \cdot I$$ Substituting $I = Q_{SVC} / V$: $$V = V_{ref} + X_{SL} \cdot \dfrac{Q_{SVC}}{V}$$ Multiplying both sides by $V$: $$V^2 - V_{ref} \cdot V - X_{SL} \cdot Q_{SVC} = 0$$ Solving this quadratic for $V$ yields the **droop characteristic** of the SVC. The **slope** $X_{SL}$ (typically 2–5%) defines how much $V$ varies as $Q$ changes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A small slope means tighter voltage regulation but larger SVC rating. A larger slope provides natural load sharing between parallel SVCs but allows wider voltage deviation. KTU problems often ask for the **range** of $Q$ between $V_{max}$ and $V_{min}$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Active Power Transfer with Series Compensation Original uncompensated line: $$P = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X_L} \sin \delta$$ When a series compensator (SSSC or TCSC) injects voltage $V_{se}$ in quadrature with line current, the effective reactance becomes $X_{eff} = X_L - X_{comp}$: $$P_{comp} = \dfrac{V_1 V_2}{X_L - X_{comp}} \sin \delta$$ **Power Transfer Increase Ratio:** $$\dfrac{P_{comp}}{P} = \dfrac{X_L}{X_L - X_{comp}} = \dfrac{1}{1 - k}$$ where $k = X_{comp} / X_L$ is the **compensation ratio**. For $k = 0.5$, power transfer doubles. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake in KTU Valuation:** Students forget that series compensation also **raises the risk of sub-synchronous resonance (SSR)** in turbo-generators. Examiners love to deduct marks if you do not mention this limitation. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: STATCOM vs. SVC Comparison | Parameter | SVC | STATCOM | |---|---|---| | **Commutation** | Line (thyristor) | Self (IGBT/IGCT) | | **Reactive Power Source** | Capacitors/Reactors | DC link capacitor | | **Dynamic Response** | 1–2 cycles | Sub-cycle (< 1 cycle) | | **Size for Same Q** | Larger | ~50% smaller | | **Harmonics** | Higher | Lower (PWM-controlled) | | **Weak Grid Operation** | Poor | Excellent | | **DC Link Energy** | Not required | Required (capacitor) | > [!NOTE] > This comparison appears in nearly every KTU Part B question on FACTS. Memorize both **technical differences** and **cost/footprint differences**. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: HVDC Bipole Link ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["AC GRID 1
SENDING END"] --> nodeB["RECTIFIER STATION
12 PULSE THYRISTOR BRIDGE"] nodeB --> nodeC["SMOOTHING REACTOR Ld"] nodeC --> nodeD["DC TRANSMISSION LINE
+/- 500 kV or +/- 800 kV"] nodeD --> nodeE["DC FILTER"] nodeE --> nodeF["INVERTER STATION
12 PULSE THYRISTOR BRIDGE"] nodeF --> nodeG["AC GRID 2
RECEIVING END"] nodeB -. CONTROL .-> nodeH["CONTROL SYSTEM
FIRING ANGLE ALPHA
GAMMA EXTINCTION"] nodeF -. CONTROL .-> nodeH ``` > [!NOTE] > The bidirectional dotted arrows indicate the **control plane** — a hierarchical controller maintains $\alpha$ at the rectifier and $\gamma$ at the inverter, adjusting either to maintain constant DC current ($I_d$) or constant power. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Static VAR Compensator (SVC) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["LOAD BUS"] --> nodeB["COUPLING TRANSFORMER"] nodeB --> nodeC["SVC MAIN CIRCUIT"] subgraph nodeC direction TB nodeC1["TCR BRANCH
Thyristor Controlled Reactor"] nodeC2["TSC BRANCH 1
Thyristor Switched Capacitor"] nodeC3["TSC BRANCH 2
Thyristor Switched Capacitor"] nodeC4["TSC BRANCH 3
Thyristor Switched Capacitor"] end nodeC --> nodeD["MEASUREMENT UNIT
Vbus and Iline"] nodeD --> nodeE["CONTROLLER
PI regulator + firing pulse generator"] nodeE -. Firing Pulses .-> nodeC1 nodeE -. Firing Pulses .-> nodeC2 nodeE -. Firing Pulses .-> nodeC3 ``` --- ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["SENDING END BUS V1"] --> nodeB["SHUNT VSC 1
STATCOM Function"] nodeA --> nodeC["SERIES VSC 2
SSSC Function"] nodeC --> nodeD["RECEIVING END BUS V2"] nodeB --- nodeE["COMMON DC LINK
CAPACITOR Cdc"] nodeC --- nodeE nodeF["UPFC CONTROL SYSTEM"] -. Gating Signals .-> nodeB nodeF -. Gating Signals .-> nodeC nodeF -. IGBT / IGCT Pulses .-> nodeE ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **UPFC Functional Roles:** > - **Shunt VSC (STATCOM branch):** Maintains bus voltage $V_1$ and controls reactive power $Q$. > - **Series VSC (SSSC branch):** Injects voltage $V_{pq}$ to control line active power $P$ and reactive flow $Q$. > - **DC link:** Bidirectional real-power exchange between the two VSCs. > This is the most powerful FACTS device and is the **#1 favorite 14-mark question in KTU exams**. --- ### 4.4 Functional Architecture: FACTS Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["FACTS CONTROLLERS"] --> nodeB["SHUNT TYPE"] nodeA --> nodeC["SERIES TYPE"] nodeA --> nodeD["COMBINED SHUNT SERIES"] nodeB --> nodeB1["SVC"] nodeB --> nodeB2["STATCOM"] nodeC --> nodeC1["TCSC"] nodeC --> nodeC2["SSSC"] nodeD --> nodeD1["UPFC"] nodeD --> nodeD2["IPFC"] ``` --- ### 4.5 Operating Waveform: SVC V-I Characteristic ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["V_max"] --- nodeB["OPERATING REGION
Slope Xsl between Vmax and Vmin"] nodeB --- nodeC["V_min"] nodeC --- nodeD["INDUCTIVE REGION
TCR absorbs Q"] nodeB --- nodeE["CAPACITIVE REGION
TSC injects Q"] ``` > [!NOTE] > Within the operating region, $V = V_{ref} + X_{SL} \cdot I$. Outside it (V > V_max or V < V_min), the SVC saturates to its maximum inductive or capacitive rating, behaving like a fixed reactor or capacitor. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define HVDC transmission. List any two advantages of HVDC over HVAC for long-distance bulk power transfer.* **Model Answer:** **HVDC (High Voltage Direct Current) Transmission** is a method of bulk electrical power transfer using a DC link between two AC grids via rectifier and inverter stations employing power electronic converters. **Advantages:** 1. **Lower losses** for long distances because there is no reactive component of current and the skin effect is minimal in DC. 2. **Asynchronous interconnection** — two AC grids of different frequencies (or same frequency with phase drift) can be tied together. 3. **No limit on transmission length** due to charging currents (unlike AC cables). 4. **Better controllability** of power flow via firing angle $\alpha$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between a TCR and a TSC in a Static VAR Compensator.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | TCR (Thyristor Controlled Reactor) | TSC (Thyristor Switched Capacitor) | |---|---|---| | **Function** | Absorbs reactive power | Injects reactive power | | **Operation** | Continuous (firing angle control) | Discrete (on/off switching) | | **Component** | Reactor + bidirectional SCRs | Capacitor + SCRs + damping reactor | | **Harmonics** | Generates significant harmonics | Negligible harmonics | | **Response** | Fast, continuous | Stepped, slower | --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice --- #### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **12-pulse HVDC converter station**. Derive the expression for average DC output voltage of a 6-pulse converter. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the working principle and V-I characteristics of a **Static VAR Compensator (SVC)** with its constituent branches (TCR and TSC). **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.A (a):** A 12-pulse HVDC converter is formed by cascading two **6-pulse Graetz bridges** through a transformer arrangement that produces a **$30°$ phase shift** between the AC supplies. One bridge is fed by a **Wye (Y-Y)** transformer and the other by a **Delta-Y (Δ-Y)** transformer. The DC outputs of the two bridges are connected in series, summing the DC voltages. The DC-side fundamental ripple is the **12th harmonic** of the supply frequency ($600 \text{ Hz}$ on a $50 \text{ Hz}$ system), drastically reducing the size of DC smoothing reactors and AC harmonic filters compared to a single 6-pulse bridge. **Derivation of Average DC Output Voltage (6-Pulse):** The instantaneous DC output during one thyristor conduction interval ($\pi/3$ rad) is: $$v_d(\theta) = \sqrt{2} \, V_{LL} \sin \theta$$ The average value, with thyristor firing delayed by angle $\alpha$, is: $$V_{d,\alpha} = \dfrac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \sqrt{2} V_{LL} \sin \theta \, d\theta$$ Evaluating: $$V_{d,\alpha} = \dfrac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha = V_{do} \cos \alpha$$ For 12-pulse operation, since both bridges share the same DC current and add their DC voltages: $$V_{d,12} = \dfrac{6 \sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{LL} \cos \alpha = 2 \, V_{do} \cos \alpha$$ (assuming $V_{LL}$ is the line-to-line voltage of the Y-connected secondary supplying each bridge). --- **Model Solution to Q.A (b):** The **Static VAR Compensator (SVC)** is a shunt-connected device at the load bus that dynamically exchanges reactive power to regulate voltage. Its main branches are: **1. Thyristor Controlled Reactor (TCR):** A reactor $L$ is connected in series with a **pair of inverse-parallel thyristors**. By varying the firing angle $\alpha$ from **$90°$ to $180°$**, the effective reactance seen by the fundamental component is varied continuously: $$X_{L,eff} = \dfrac{\pi X_L}{2\pi - 2\alpha + \sin 2\alpha}$$ **2. Thyristor Switched Capacitor (TSC):** A capacitor is connected in series with an inverse-parallel thyristor pair and a small damping reactor. The capacitor is **either fully ON or fully OFF** (not phase-controlled) to avoid inrush currents and harmonic generation. Multiple TSC branches are used for stepwise VAR injection. **V-I Characteristic:** The SVC maintains bus voltage within a band around $V_{ref}$: $$V = V_{ref} + X_{SL} \cdot I$$ - **Capacitive region (TSC ON):** SVC injects reactive current as voltage rises. - **Inductive region (TCR ON):** SVC absorbs reactive current as voltage falls. - **Slope** $X_{SL}$ (typically **2–5%**) determines load sharing. --- #### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the working of **STATCOM** and **SSSC**. Compare their operating principles with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC)** with a block diagram. Discuss how it independently controls active and reactive power flow in a transmission line. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.B (a):** **STATCOM (Static Synchronous Compensator):** A STATCOM is a shunt-connected Voltage Source Converter (VSC) using **self-commutated devices** (IGBTs/IGCTs). Its DC link is a single capacitor. It operates like a **synchronous condenser** but with no rotating parts. - When $V_{out} > V_{bus}$: STATCOM injects **capacitive reactive power** into the grid. - When $V_{out} < V_{bus}$: STATCOM absorbs **inductive reactive power**. - When $V_{out} = V_{bus}$: No reactive exchange; only active power (used for charging DC link). The reactive current is: $$I_Q = \dfrac{V_{out} - V_{bus}}{X}$$ **SSSC (Static Synchronous Series Compensator):** The SSSC is a series-connected VSC that injects a voltage $V_{se}$ in series with the transmission line. Unlike TCSC, SSSC can inject voltage at any phase angle (not just $90°$ quadrature with line current), enabling: - **Capacitive mode** ($V_{se}$ opposes voltage drop): Increases transmitted power. - **Inductive mode** ($V_{se}$ adds to voltage drop): Reduces transmitted power. **Comparison:** | Parameter | STATCOM | SSSC | |---|---|---| | **Connection** | Shunt | Series | | **Primary Function** | Voltage / Reactive control | Power flow / Series compensation | | **Voltage Injection** | At the bus | In the line | | **Equivalent** | Synchronous condenser | Variable reactance (with quadrature) | --- **Model Solution to Q.B (b):** The **Unified Power Flow Controller (UPFC)** is the most versatile FACTS device, comprising **two VSCs sharing a common DC-link capacitor**. The first VSC (VSC1) is connected in **shunt** via a coupling transformer; the second VSC (VSC2) is connected in **series** via a series injection transformer. **Block Diagram:** Refer to the UPFC block diagram in Section 4.3 above. **Independent Control:** 1. **Series VSC (VSC2):** - Injects voltage $V_{pq}$ at any phase angle with controllable magnitude and angle. - The real part of $V_{pq}$ (in phase with current) controls **active power flow** $P$. - The quadrature part of $V_{pq}$ controls **reactive power flow** $Q$. 2. **Shunt VSC (VSC1):** - Maintains **DC link voltage** by absorbing/injecting the active power required by the series VSC. - Independently controls **bus voltage magnitude** $V_1$ by reactive power exchange. 3. **Operating Principle:** The series branch controls $P$ and $Q$ flow. The shunt branch supplies the active power demanded by the series branch through the DC link, *without* drawing it from the AC grid. Hence **UPFC can control $P$ and $Q$ independently** — a unique capability unmatched by any other single FACTS device. **Real-world Applications:** - **AEP Inez Station, USA (1998)** — first commercial UPFC installation. - Used in Indian grids for **dynamic line rating** and **congestion management** in heavily loaded corridors. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > - **For HVDC:** Marks are deducted if you **do not mention commutation overlap** ($R_c$ drop) or the role of **smoothing reactors**. Always draw the firing pulse diagram. > - **For SVC:** Do NOT confuse $V_{ref}$ with the system nominal voltage. Always show the **sloped V-I curve** with $X_{SL}$ — not a vertical line. > - **For FACTS:** STATCOM and SSSC are **NOT** the same. Many students incorrectly equate them. STATCOM = shunt; SSSC = series. Memorize the difference cold. > - **For UPFC:** The single most common mistake is saying "UPFC controls P and Q via the series branch." Correct: the **shunt branch maintains the DC link** while the series branch controls P and Q independently. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **HVDC = LCC (thyristor) + VSC (IGBT)**. LCC needs grid commutation; VSC is self-commutated. - [ ] **Key formula:** $V_{do} = (3\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_{LL} \approx 1.35 \, V_{LL}$. With overlap: $V_d = V_{do} \cos \alpha - R_c I_d$. - [ ] **Inverter mode:** $\gamma \geq 15°$ ensures successful commutation. Smaller $\gamma$ ⇒ higher DC voltage but risk of commutation failure. - [ ] **12-pulse converter** eliminates 5th and 7th harmonics on the AC side; only **12k ± 1** harmonics remain. - [ ] **TCR = continuous + lagging VARs; TSC = discrete + leading VARs.** Together = SVC. - [ ] **SVC slope** $X_{SL}$ (2–5%) → droop characteristic; ensures load sharing between parallel SVCs. - [ ] **STATCOM beats SVC** in: response speed, footprint, weak-grid operation, harmonic performance. - [ ] **FACTS family:** Shunt → SVC, STATCOM. Series → TCSC, SSSC. Combined → UPFC, IPFC. - [ ] **UPFC = STATCOM + SSSC on shared DC link.** Only device to **independently** control P, Q, and V. - [ ] **Power transfer gain** with series compensation: $P_{comp}/P = 1/(1-k)$ where $k = X_{comp}/X_L$. - [ ] **Static Switch / Solid-State Breaker:** Used for sub-cycle transfer, capacitor switching, and arc-furnace flicker mitigation. Replaces mechanical breakers in sensitive applications. - [ ] **KTU Trap:** Always state **commutation overlap** ($R_c$ or $X_c$ drop) in HVDC questions — even a one-line mention fetches a mark. > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip for KTU Exam Day:** > For a 14-mark FACTS/HVDC question, follow this 4-step structure: > 1. Block diagram (3 marks) > 2. Operating principle explanation (4 marks) > 3. Key formula derivation (4 marks) > 4. Advantages / Limitations / Comparison table (3 marks) > Following this template consistently can fetch **12–14 marks** out of 14. ---
